Each orbital holds two electrons; therefore a subshell can accommodate 2(2l+1) electrons, where l is the azimuthal quantum number of that subshell.

concepts.concept_title: Energy and size trend with principal quantum number
concepts.description: As n increases, orbitals become larger and lie farther from the nucleus, requiring more energy to place an electron there; consequently, both size and energy of orbitals increase with n.

concepts.concept_title: Energy of hydrogen‑like atoms depends only on n
concepts.description: For hydrogen and hydrogen‑like ions (He⁺, Li²⁺, …) the energy of an electron is a function solely of the principal quantum number n; l and mₗ do not affect the energy.

concepts.concept_title: Total number of orbitals for a principal quantum number
concepts.description: For a given principal quantum number n, the total number of atomic orbitals is n². This arises from the sum of (2l+1) orbitals for each allowed l value (0 to n‑1).

concepts.concept_title: Allowed values of azimuthal quantum number l
concepts.description: For a given principal quantum number n, the azimuthal (orbital) quantum number l can take integer values from 0 to n‑1. Each l value corresponds to a subshell (s, p, d, f…).

concepts.concept_title: Allowed values of magnetic quantum number m
concepts.description: For a specific l, the magnetic quantum number m can assume (2l+1) integral values ranging from –l to +l, defining the orientation of the orbital in space.

The square of the atomic wave function, ψ², gives the probability density of finding an electron at a particular point in space. Multiplying ψ² by a small volume element dV yields the probability of locating the electron in that volume.
An ns orbital possesses (n‑1) radial nodes where ψ = 0. Thus 1s has no node, 2s has one node, 3s has two nodes, and so on. The number of nodes increases with the principal quantum number.
Nodes are surfaces (or points) where the wave function ψ becomes zero. They have no physical electron density and separate regions of opposite phase in the orbital.

concepts.concept_title: Radial probability distribution for 1s and 2s orbitals
concepts.description: The radial probability density ψ²(r) for a 1s orbital peaks at the nucleus and decreases monotonically. For a 2s orbital, ψ²(r) first falls to zero (node), then rises to a secondary maximum before decaying, reflecting one radial node.

concepts.concept_title: Correspondence between l values and orbital symbols
concepts.description: The azimuthal quantum number l is linked to the spectroscopic notation: l = 0 → s, l = 1 → p, l = 2 → d, l = 3 → f. This notation is used to denote subshells in electron configurations.

concepts.concept_title: Calculation of total orbitals using n² relation
concepts.description: The total number of orbitals in a given principal shell can be directly obtained by squaring n (n²). For example, n = 3 gives 9 orbitals: 1 s, 3 p, and 5 d.

The probability of finding an electron in a region is given by the probability density |ψ|², where ψ is the atomic wavefunction. The density varies with position and integrates to unity over all space.

concepts.concept_title: Boundary (contour) surface of constant probability density
concepts.description: A boundary surface is drawn through space where the value of |ψ|² is constant, providing a visual representation of the orbital shape that encloses a chosen fraction (e.g., 90%) of the electron probability.

All s‑orbitals are spherically symmetric; the probability of finding an electron at a given distance from the nucleus is the same in every direction. Their size increases with the principal quantum number (n).

concepts.concept_title: Size order of s‑orbitals with principal quantum number
concepts.description: For a given atom, the radii of s‑orbitals follow the order 4s > 3s > 2s > 1s, reflecting the increase in the principal quantum number n.

concepts.concept_title: Shape of p‑orbitals and nodal plane
concepts.description: p‑orbitals (l = 1) consist of two lobes separated by a planar node that passes through the nucleus. The boundary surface is therefore non‑spherical and consists of two regions of high probability density.

concepts.concept_title: Equivalence of constant |ψ|² and constant |ψ| surfaces
concepts.description: If the probability density |ψ|² is constant on a surface, the magnitude of the wavefunction |ψ| is also constant on that same surface, allowing either to be used for drawing orbital boundaries.

concepts.concept_title: Orbital energy ordering in multi‑electron atoms
concepts.description: In atoms with more than one electron, the energy of orbitals increases in the sequence 1s < 2s = 2p < 3s = 3p = 3d < 4s = 4p = 4d = 4f … . This ordering reflects the combined influence of principal and azimuthal quantum numbers.

Orbitals that belong to the same principal quantum number and have the same azimuthal quantum number possess identical energy and are called degenerate; for example, the three 3p orbitals are degenerate.

concepts.concept_title: Subshell energy hierarchy (s < p < d < f)
concepts.description: Within a given principal quantum number, the energy of subshells follows the order s < p < d < f because electrons in s‑orbitals penetrate closer to the nucleus and experience a higher effective nuclear charge.
The net positive charge felt by an electron after accounting for the shielding by inner‑shell electrons is termed the effective nuclear charge, Z_eff = Z – σ, where σ is the shielding constant.
Electrons in inner shells partially cancel the full nuclear charge, reducing the attractive force experienced by outer‑shell electrons; this phenomenon is called shielding or screening.

concepts.concept_title: Electron interactions in hydrogen vs. multi‑electron atoms
concepts.description: A hydrogen atom experiences only the attractive electron‑nucleus interaction, whereas multi‑electron atoms have additional repulsive interactions between electrons, influencing orbital energies and stability.

concepts.concept_title: Stability of electrons in multi‑electron atoms
concepts.description: In multi‑electron atoms, an electron remains stable when the total attractive forces (nuclear charge) outweigh the repulsive forces from other electrons, especially those in inner shells.

Certain orbitals do not follow the simple n‑value rule; for instance, 4s is lower in energy than 3d, 6s lower than 5d, and 4f lower than 6p, reflecting the combined effects of penetration and shielding.

concepts.concept_title: Effective nuclear charge (Z_eff) and its variation with azimuthal quantum number
concepts.description: Effective nuclear charge experienced by an electron decreases as the azimuthal quantum number (l) increases within the same principal shell. Consequently, s‑electrons feel a larger Z_eff than p‑electrons, which in turn feel a larger Z_eff than d‑electrons.

concepts.concept_title: Differential shielding ability of s, p and d orbitals
concepts.description: Electrons in s orbitals shield outer electrons more effectively than those in p orbitals, and p electrons shield better than d electrons, even when all belong to the same principal shell.

concepts.concept_title: Penetration effect of electrons in different subshells
concepts.description: Because of their spherical shape, s‑electrons spend more time close to the nucleus compared to p‑ and d‑electrons, leading to higher penetration and lower energy for s electrons within a given shell.

concepts.concept_title: Energy ordering of subshells within the same principal quantum number
concepts.description: Within a given principal quantum number, the energy of subshells follows the order:  s < p < d < f. The lower the azimuthal quantum number, the more negative (lower) the orbital energy.

concepts.concept_title: (n + l) rule (Madelung rule) for orbital energies
concepts.description: The energy of an orbital increases with the sum (n + l); orbitals with lower (n + l) have lower energy. When two orbitals have the same (n + l), the one with lower n is of lower energy.
Electrons fill atomic orbitals in order of increasing energy, following the (n + l) rule, until all electrons of the atom are accommodated, giving the ground‑state electron configuration.
No two electrons in an atom can have the same set of four quantum numbers; each orbital can accommodate at most two electrons with opposite spins.

Electrons occupy orbitals in the order of increasing energy, filling the lowest‑energy orbital available before moving to higher ones. This guides the construction of an atom's electronic configuration.

Subshells are arranged by the sum of principal quantum number (n) and azimuthal quantum number (l); lower (n + l) values are filled first. If two subshells have the same (n + l), the one with lower n fills earlier.
For orbitals of equal energy, electrons occupy separate orbitals with parallel spins before pairing up. This maximises total spin and stabilises the atom.
No two electrons in an atom can share the same set of four quantum numbers; thus, a maximum of two electrons can occupy a single orbital, and they must have opposite spins.
Each subshell can accommodate a fixed number of electrons: s = 2, p = 6, d = 10, f = 14. This follows from the number of orbitals in each subshell and the Pauli principle.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Effective Nuclear Charge on Orbital Energies
concepts.description: Variations in effective nuclear charge cause certain orbitals (e.g., 4s) to be lower in energy than others (e.g., 3d) despite having a higher principal quantum number, influencing the filling order.

concepts.concept_title: Exceptions to the General Filling Order
concepts.description: In some atoms the expected (n + l) order is altered; for example, the 4s orbital is filled before the 3d orbital, as seen in the electron configuration of potassium.

concepts.concept_title: Maximum electrons in a shell (2n² rule)
concepts.description: The maximum number of electrons that can be accommodated in a shell with principal quantum number n is given by 2n².
No two electrons in an atom can have the same set of four quantum numbers; consequently, each orbital can hold at most two electrons with opposite spins.
Electrons fill degenerate orbitals of a subshell singly with parallel spins before any pairing occurs, maximizing total spin.

concepts.concept_title: Extra stability of half‑filled and fully‑filled subshells
concepts.description: A set of degenerate orbitals that is either half‑filled or completely filled possesses additional stability due to symmetric electron distribution.

concepts.concept_title: Order of filling of orbitals (Aufbau principle)
concepts.description: Electrons occupy atomic orbitals in order of increasing energy, following the sequence 1s → 2s → 2p → 3s → 3p → 4s → 3d → 4p …
The distribution of electrons is written by indicating the principal quantum number, subshell symbol, and the number of electrons as a superscript, e.g., 1s² 2s² 2p⁴.
Each orbital is shown as a box; electrons are represented by arrows indicating spin direction (↑ for up, ↓ for down), illustrating Hund's rule and Pauli exclusion.

concepts.concept_title: Capacities of s, p, d, f subshells
concepts.description: The s, p, d, and f subshells contain 1, 3, 5, and 7 orbitals respectively, allowing a maximum of 2, 6, 10, and 14 electrons.

concepts.concept_title: Stability of half‑filled and fully filled subshells
concepts.description: Subshells that are exactly half‑filled (e.g., p³, d⁵) or completely filled (e.g., p⁶, d¹⁰) possess lower energy and greater stability than partially filled ones.

concepts.concept_title: Symmetrical electron distribution and reduced shielding
concepts.description: In half‑filled or fully filled subshells, electrons are symmetrically distributed, leading to minimal inter‑electron shielding and stronger attraction to the nucleus.

concepts.concept_title: Exchange energy and its contribution to subshell stability
concepts.description: When electrons with parallel spins occupy degenerate orbitals, they can exchange positions, releasing exchange energy; the maximum exchange energy occurs for half‑filled or fully filled subshells, enhancing stability.

concepts.concept_title: Hund’s rule and its link to exchange energy
concepts.description: Hund’s rule states that electrons fill degenerate orbitals singly with parallel spins before pairing, a consequence of maximizing exchange energy and thereby stabilising the atom.

concepts.concept_title: Anomalous electron configurations of chromium and copper
concepts.description: Cr and Cu deviate from the expected Aufbau order to achieve a half‑filled d⁵ (Cr: 3d⁵ 4s¹) or a fully filled d¹⁰ (Cu: 3d¹⁰ 4s¹) configuration, illustrating the extra stability of these subshells.

concepts.concept_title: Energy ordering of 4s and 3d orbitals and electron promotion
concepts.description: Although 4s is filled before 3d, the 3d orbitals become lower in energy after 4s is occupied; an electron may be promoted from 4s to 3d to achieve a more stable half‑filled or fully filled d subshell.

concepts.concept_title: Aufbau principle and the filling sequence up to xenon
concepts.description: The systematic order of orbital filling (1s → 2s → 2p → 3s → 3p → 4s → 3d → 4p → 5s → 4d → 5p) determines the ground‑state configurations of elements from hydrogen to xenon.

concepts.concept_title: Electron configuration trends from lanthanum to mercury
concepts.description: From La (Z=57) to Hg (Z=80), electrons are added to the 4f and subsequently to the 5d subshells, following the pattern of filling 4fⁿ 5dⁿ⁻¹ 6s², which is essential for understanding the transition series.

concepts.concept_title: Valence electronic configuration and its role in chemical behavior
concepts.description: The valence configuration, comprising the electrons in the outermost s and p (or d) orbitals, dictates an element’s bonding patterns, oxidation states, and reactivity.

concepts.concept_title: Stability of half‑filled and fully filled subshells
concepts.description: Half‑filled and completely filled subshells are unusually stable because they experience relatively small shielding, lower coulombic repulsion among electrons and a larger exchange energy.
Exchange energy arises from parallel spin electrons exchanging positions; for a d⁵ configuration the maximum number of exchange interactions leads to extra stability.

The distribution of electrons among atomic orbitals follows the Aufbau principle, Hund’s rule and Pauli exclusion principle, giving characteristic configurations for each element, including exceptional cases.
Dalton proposed that elements consist of indivisible atoms, atoms of a given element are identical, and chemical reactions involve rearrangement of these atoms.
Experiments in the late 19th century established that atoms are divisible into electrons, protons and neutrons, leading to modern atomic models.
Thomson suggested that an atom is a sphere of uniform positive charge with negatively charged electrons embedded within it.
The deflection of alpha particles by a thin gold foil revealed a small, dense, positively charged nucleus at the centre of the atom.

concepts.concept_title: Rutherford’s nuclear model of the atom
concepts.description: Based on scattering results, Rutherford proposed that an atom consists of a massive, positively charged nucleus surrounded by electrons in circular orbits.

concepts.concept_title: Bohr’s postulates for the hydrogen atom
concepts.description: Bohr introduced quantized circular orbits where electrons can exist without radiating energy, each orbit having a fixed energy and angular momentum.

concepts.concept_title: Energy and radius of Bohr orbits
concepts.description: Bohr derived formulas for the energy and radius of an electron in a given orbit, allowing calculation of spectral lines of hydrogen.
The Bohr model cannot explain spectra of multi‑electron atoms and ignores the wave nature of electrons, necessitating quantum mechanical treatment.
Electrons exhibit wave‑particle duality; their wave nature is essential for describing atomic structure beyond the Bohr model.
It states that the exact position and exact momentum (or velocity) of an electron cannot be simultaneously known, imposing a fundamental limit on measurement precision.
A wave equation that yields the allowed energy levels and wave functions (ψ) of an electron in a hydrogen atom, incorporating wave‑particle duality and complying with the uncertainty principle.
Solutions of the Schrödinger equation introduce three quantum numbers—principal (n), azimuthal (l), and magnetic (m)—with specific allowed values: n = 1,2,… ; l = 0 to n‑1 ; m = –l to +l.
In multi‑electron atoms, orbital energies increase with the sum of principal and azimuthal quantum numbers; for equal (n + l) values, the orbital with lower n is lower in energy.
No two electrons in an atom can have an identical set of four quantum numbers; thus each orbital can hold a maximum of two electrons with opposite spins.
Electrons fill degenerate orbitals singly with parallel spins before pairing, maximizing the total spin multiplicity of the atom.
Electrons are organized into shells (defined by n), which contain subshells (defined by l) and orbitals (defined by m); this hierarchy determines the electronic structure of atoms.

concepts.concept_title: Threshold wavelength and Planck’s constant from photoelectric data
concepts.description: Using the linear relation between maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons and frequency of incident light, the threshold wavelength (λ₀) and Planck’s constant (h) can be obtained from experimental v vs λ data.

concepts.concept_title: Work function calculation from stopping potential
concepts.description: The work function (ϕ) of a metal is found from the stopping potential (V₀) required to halt photoelectrons, using ϕ = hν – eV₀, where ν is the frequency corresponding to the incident wavelength.

concepts.concept_title: Binding (ionization) energy of inner electrons from photon interaction
concepts.description: When a photon of known wavelength ejects an inner‑shell electron with a measured kinetic energy, the binding energy equals the photon energy minus the kinetic energy of the ejected electron.

concepts.concept_title: Rydberg formula for Paschen series and determination of quantum number n
concepts.description: The Rydberg relation for the Paschen series (final orbit n=3) allows calculation of the initial principal quantum number n from the observed wavelength, and identification of the corresponding spectral region (infrared).

concepts.concept_title: Wavelength of transition from orbital radii and series identification
concepts.description: Using the Bohr model relation between orbital radius and principal quantum number, the wavelength of a transition between two radii can be computed and the series (e.g., Balmer, Paschen) and its spectral region can be assigned.

concepts.concept_title: de Broglie wavelength of electrons in electron microscopy
concepts.description: The de Broglie wavelength λ = h / (mv) of electrons accelerated to a known velocity determines the resolution achievable in an electron microscope.

concepts.concept_title: Neutron velocity from given de Broglie wavelength
concepts.description: For neutrons, the velocity corresponding to a specified wavelength is obtained from v = h / (mₙλ), which is essential for neutron diffraction experiments.

concepts.concept_title: de Broglie wavelength of electron in Bohr’s first orbit
concepts.description: Applying the de Broglie relation to an electron moving in the ground state orbit of hydrogen provides its wavelength, illustrating wave‑particle duality in the Bohr model.

concepts.concept_title: de Broglie wavelength of macroscopic objects (hockey ball)
concepts.description: Even macroscopic bodies possess a de Broglie wavelength given by λ = h / (mv); for a 0.1 kg object moving at 4.37 × 10⁵ m s⁻¹ the wavelength is extremely small, highlighting classical limits of quantum effects.

concepts.concept_title: Heisenberg uncertainty principle for position and momentum
concepts.description: The product of uncertainties in position (Δx) and momentum (Δp) must satisfy Δx·Δp ≥ ħ/2; calculations using a measured position accuracy reveal the minimum allowable momentum uncertainty and test consistency with a given momentum value.

concepts.concept_title: Energy ordering of electron states using quantum numbers
concepts.description: Electronic energy in a hydrogen‑like atom depends primarily on the principal quantum number n; secondary splitting arises from l and spin, allowing arrangement of given (n, l, mₗ, mₛ) sets in increasing order of energy and identification of degenerate states.

concepts.concept_title: Effective nuclear charge experienced by p‑electrons in different shells
concepts.description: Within a multi‑electron atom, electrons in outer shells experience a higher effective nuclear charge (Z_eff) due to reduced shielding; comparing 2p, 3p, and 4p electrons in bromine reveals which electron is most strongly attracted to the nucleus.
The net positive charge experienced by an electron after accounting for shielding by inner electrons. It governs atomic size, ionisation energy and electron affinity.

concepts.concept_title: Trend of Z_eff with principal quantum number
concepts.description: For orbitals having the same azimuthal quantum number (l), Z_eff decreases as the principal quantum number (n) increases because electrons are farther from the nucleus and experience more shielding.

concepts.concept_title: Comparative Z_eff for specific orbital pairs
concepts.description: Among a given pair of orbitals, the one with the lower principal quantum number or lower orbital angular momentum generally experiences a larger effective nuclear charge.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Z_eff on unpaired electrons in the same subshell
concepts.description: Within the same principal shell, electrons in elements with higher nuclear charge experience a stronger Z_eff, pulling them closer and affecting their energy, but the number of unpaired electrons remains dictated by Hund’s rule.

concepts.concept_title: Determination of unpaired electrons in atoms
concepts.description: The number of unpaired electrons in an atom is obtained from its ground‑state electron configuration using Hund’s rule and the Pauli exclusion principle.

concepts.concept_title: Number of subshells for a given principal quantum number
concepts.description: For a principal quantum number n, the possible values of the azimuthal quantum number l are 0 to n‑1, giving n distinct subshells (s, p, d, f …).

concepts.concept_title: Electrons corresponding to a specific magnetic quantum number (m)
concepts.description: Each orbital (defined by n and l) contains two electrons with opposite spin; for a given magnetic quantum number m, the total electrons across all orbitals having that m value equal 2 times the number of such orbitals.
The periodic law states that the physical and chemical properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic numbers. This regularity forms the basis of the modern periodic table.

concepts.concept_title: Atomic Number as Basis of Classification
concepts.description: Atomic number, which equals the number of protons in the nucleus, determines the electronic configuration of an element and therefore its position and properties in the periodic table.
The distribution of electrons in atomic orbitals governs trends such as atomic size, ionization energy, electron affinity and electronegativity across periods and down groups.
Elements with atomic numbers greater than 100 are named systematically by IUPAC using Latin/Greek roots for the digits of the atomic number, followed by the suffix '-ium'.
Elements are grouped into s, p, d and f blocks according to the type of atomic orbital that receives the last electron; each block exhibits characteristic chemical behaviour.
Atomic radius decreases from left to right across a period due to increasing nuclear charge, and increases down a group as additional electron shells are added.
Ionization enthalpy generally increases across a period owing to stronger attraction between nucleus and electrons, and decreases down a group because of larger atomic size and shielding.
Electron affinity becomes more negative across a period, indicating a greater tendency to gain an electron, while it becomes less negative down a group due to increased distance from the nucleus.
Electronegativity increases across a period and decreases down a group, reflecting the ability of an atom to attract bonding electrons.
Metallic character decreases from left to right across a period and increases down a group, correlating inversely with ionization enthalpy and electronegativity.

concepts.concept_title: Reactivity Trends of Metals and Non‑metals
concepts.description: Reactivity of metals increases down a group (e.g., alkali metals) whereas reactivity of non‑metals generally decreases down a group; across a period, metal reactivity decreases and non‑metal reactivity increases.
Classifying elements provides a systematic framework to predict properties, understand chemical behaviour, and communicate scientific information efficiently.
Three elements form a triad when the atomic weight of the middle element is nearly the average of the other two and its chemical properties are intermediate between them.
When elements are arranged in order of increasing atomic weight, every eighth element exhibits properties similar to the first, a pattern that holds reliably up to calcium.
A three‑dimensional helical arrangement of elements on a cylinder where elements with similar properties recur at regular intervals of atomic weight.
Elements arranged by increasing atomic weight show periodic recurrence of physical and chemical properties; gaps in the table were used to predict undiscovered elements.
A table based on atomic weight that correlates physical properties such as atomic volume, melting point, and boiling point, demonstrating periodic variation across the elements.
Both chemical and physical properties of elements repeat at regular intervals when the elements are ordered by atomic weight, forming the basis of the periodic system.

concepts.concept_title: Atomic Weight as Basis for Early Periodicity
concepts.description: The early classification schemes (Dobereiner, Newlands, de Chancourtois, Meyer, Mendeleev) relied primarily on the sequential increase of atomic weights to reveal recurring patterns in element properties.
Mendeleev formulated that the physical and chemical properties of elements vary periodically with their atomic weights, leading to the arrangement of elements in a tabular form.
The modern periodic law states that the properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic numbers, which resolves the exceptions observed with atomic weight ordering.

concepts.concept_title: Groups and Periods in the Periodic Table
concepts.description: Elements placed in the same vertical column (group) exhibit similar chemical behaviour, while horizontal rows (periods) correspond to increasing atomic number.

concepts.concept_title: Arrangement of Elements by Increasing Atomic Weight
concepts.description: Mendeleev ordered the elements in the periodic table according to their increasing atomic weights, which revealed recurring patterns in properties.

concepts.concept_title: Eka‑Elements and Prediction of Undiscovered Elements
concepts.description: Mendeleev introduced placeholders like eka‑aluminium and eka‑silicon for unknown elements, predicting their existence and properties before experimental discovery.

concepts.concept_title: Prediction of Physical Properties of Unknown Elements
concepts.description: Using periodic trends, Mendeleev estimated atomic weight, density, and melting point of yet‑to‑be‑found elements such as gallium and germanium.

concepts.concept_title: Prediction of Chemical Formulas of Oxides and Halides
concepts.description: Based on the valency inferred from known elements, Mendeleev predicted the empirical formulas of oxides and chlorides for eka‑elements (e.g., Ga₂O₃, GeCl₄).

concepts.concept_title: Use of Empirical Formulas in Element Classification
concepts.description: Mendeleev relied on the similarities in empirical formulas of compounds formed by elements to group them and establish periodic relationships.
The successful experimental discovery of predicted elements and their properties provided strong evidence for the reliability of the periodic table.
The modern periodic law states that the physical and chemical properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers. Atomic number is a more fundamental property than atomic mass.

Moseley's law establishes a linear relationship between the square root of the frequency of characteristic X‑ray lines and the atomic number of an element, confirming atomic number as the basis of the periodic table.
In the modern periodic table, vertical columns are called groups and are numbered from 1 to 18, replacing the older IA‑VIIIA, IB‑VIIB notation.
Each horizontal row (period) corresponds to a specific principal quantum number (n) of the outermost electron shell, and the length of periods follows the sequence 2, 8, 8, 18, 18, 32.
The lanthanide (4f) and actinide (5f) series are placed separately as two rows below the main body of the periodic table to maintain its rectangular shape.
The atomic number (Z) of an element equals the number of protons in its nucleus and, for a neutral atom, also equals the number of electrons.
The electronic configuration of elements, governed by quantum numbers, underlies the recurring patterns (periodicity) observed in chemical and physical properties across the periodic table.
The maximum number of elements in a period is determined by the electron capacity of the corresponding subshells: 2 (s), 8 (p), 18 (d), and 32 (f).
The seventh period of the periodic table is currently incomplete; it is expected to accommodate up to 32 elements, extending the f‑block to include superheavy elements (Z > 118).

The periodic law states that the physical and chemical properties of elements repeat periodically as a function of their atomic numbers, which is a direct consequence of the periodic variation in electronic configurations.

Discovery of transuranium elements (Z = 94 to 102) led to the re‑configuration of the periodic table, positioning the actinoid series beneath the lanthanoid series to reflect their similar f‑electron filling patterns.
Glenn T. Seaborg discovered plutonium and several transuranium elements, earned the Nobel Prize in Chemistry (1951), and was honored by naming element 106 as Seaborgium (Sg).

concepts.concept_title: IUPAC systematic nomenclature for superheavy elements (Z>100)
concepts.description: Until a new element is officially recognized, IUPAC assigns a temporary name derived from its atomic number using Latin/Greek numerical roots (e.g., un‑nil‑un‑ium for Z = 101).
After resolving naming disputes (Rutherfordium vs. Kurchatovium), IUPAC approved the name Rutherfordium (Rf) for element 104, illustrating the process of official element naming post‑verification.

concepts.concept_title: Synthesis and characterization challenges of elements Z>100
concepts.description: Elements with atomic numbers above 100 are produced in minute quantities using sophisticated, high‑cost equipment, making their synthesis and experimental characterization highly challenging.

concepts.concept_title: Periodicity in properties across groups and periods
concepts.description: Elements in the same group exhibit similar chemical behavior, while moving across a period shows systematic changes in atomic radius, ionization energy, and electronegativity, reflecting periodic trends.

concepts.concept_title: Classification into s, p, d, f blocks
concepts.description: Elements are grouped into four blocks of the periodic table based on the type of atomic orbital (s, p, d, f) that is being filled with electrons in their ground state.
For a given principal quantum number, orbitals are filled in increasing order of (n + l) values; when equal, the orbital with lower n fills first. Example: 5s < 4d < 5p.
The lanthanoid series comprises elements from cerium (Z=58) to lutetium (Z=71) where electrons are added to the 4f subshell, giving characteristic +3 oxidation states and similar chemical behaviour.
The actinoid series includes elements from actinium (Z=89) onward, where electrons fill the 5f subshell, leading to radioactive properties and a wide range of oxidation states.

concepts.concept_title: Period number equals principal quantum number (n) of valence shell
concepts.description: The period in which an element appears indicates the principal quantum number (n) of the outermost (valence) electron shell that is being filled.

concepts.concept_title: Number of elements in a period equals twice the number of available orbitals
concepts.description: A period contains 2 × (total number of orbitals in that principal shell) elements, because each orbital can accommodate two electrons. Example: 5th period has 9 orbitals → 18 elements.

concepts.concept_title: Helium as an exception in block placement
concepts.description: Although helium has an s‑electron configuration (1s²), it is placed in the p‑block of the periodic table to keep it with the other noble gases.

concepts.concept_title: Hydrogen as an exception in block placement
concepts.description: Hydrogen possesses only one electron (1s¹) and is placed in the s‑block, but its unique chemical behavior often leads to its separate representation in the periodic table.

concepts.concept_title: Aufbau principle and electronic configuration notation
concepts.description: Electrons fill atomic orbitals in order of increasing energy (1s, 2s, 2p, …). The configuration can be written using the noble gas core in square brackets, e.g., [He]2s1 for lithium.

concepts.concept_title: General electronic configuration of s‑block elements
concepts.description: Elements of the s‑block have their outermost electrons in the ns¹ (Group 1) or ns² (Group 2) subshells, which dictates their typical +1 or +2 oxidation states.
Group 1 elements possess an ns¹ configuration, exhibit low ionization enthalpy, high metallic character, and form +1 ions; their reactivity and metallic character increase down the group.

concepts.concept_title: Alkaline earth metals: properties and trends
concepts.description: Group 2 elements have an ns² configuration, form +2 ions, have higher ionization enthalpy than alkali metals, and show decreasing reactivity down the group.

Across the s‑block, ionization enthalpy increases from left to right, while metallic character and atomic radius decrease; down the groups, atomic size and metallic character increase, ionization enthalpy decreases.

d‑block elements possess (n‑1)d electrons, display variable oxidation states, paramagnetism, and often act as catalysts; Zn, Cd and Hg have a filled d¹⁰ ns² configuration and behave like post‑transition metals.
Compounds formed by s‑block elements with non‑metals are predominantly ionic due to the low electronegativity and high electropositivity of the s‑block metals.

concepts.concept_title: Group‑wise electronic configurations and chemical similarity
concepts.description: Elements belonging to the same group have identical valence‑shell electron configurations, which leads to analogous chemical properties and reactivity patterns.

concepts.concept_title: Special placement of hydrogen in the periodic table
concepts.description: Hydrogen, with an electronic configuration 1s¹, can be placed above Group 1 or Group 17 because it can lose an electron to form H⁺ or gain one to form H⁻, exhibiting both alkali‑metal and halogen‑like behavior.

Lanthanides and actinides involve the filling of 4f and 5f orbitals respectively; their configurations are typically (n‑2)f¹⁻¹⁴ (n‑1)d⁰‑¹ ns², influencing their unique chemical and magnetic properties.
Lanthanoids (Ce to Lu, Z = 58‑71) and actinoids (Th to Lr, Z = 90‑103) form the two rows at the bottom of the periodic table, known as the f‑block.

concepts.concept_title: General electronic configuration of f‑block elements
concepts.description: The outer electronic configuration of f‑block elements is (n‑2)f¹‑¹⁴ (n‑1)d⁰⁻¹ ns², where the last electron added fills an f‑orbital.

concepts.concept_title: Low chemical reactivity of inner transition elements
concepts.description: Because the valence shell of inner transition elements attains a noble‑gas configuration (ns²np⁶), they exhibit very low chemical reactivity.
Early actinoids display a larger number of possible oxidation states than the corresponding lanthanoids, due to the involvement of 5f, 6d and 7s electrons.
All actinoid elements are radioactive; many of them are synthetic and exist only in nanogram quantities.
Elements with atomic numbers greater than 92 (uranium) are called transuranium elements; they are produced in nuclear reactions and are not fully studied.

concepts.concept_title: Halogens and chalcogens – high electron‑gain enthalpy
concepts.description: Group 17 halogens and Group 16 chalcogens have highly negative electron‑gain enthalpies and readily gain one or two electrons to achieve a noble‑gas configuration.

concepts.concept_title: Periodic trends of metallic and non‑metallic character
concepts.description: Non‑metallic character increases from left to right across a period, whereas metallic character increases down a group.
Elements of groups 3 to 12, situated in the centre of the periodic table, are called d‑block or transition elements because their inner d orbitals are being filled.

concepts.concept_title: General electronic configuration of d‑block elements
concepts.description: The outer electronic configuration of d‑block elements is (n‑1)d¹‑¹⁰ ns⁰⁻², with the exception of palladium.
Palladium (Pd) deviates from the general pattern; its configuration is 4d¹⁰ 5s⁰ rather than having electrons in the 5s orbital.
Transition elements commonly form coloured ions and exhibit variable oxidation states due to the comparable energies of (n‑1)d and ns electrons.

concepts.concept_title: Group and electronic configuration of element Z = 117
concepts.description: Element 117 belongs to Group 17 (halogens) and its electronic configuration is [Rn] 5f¹⁴ 6d¹⁰ 7s² 7p⁵.

concepts.concept_title: Group and electronic configuration of element Z = 120
concepts.description: Element 120 is placed in Group 2 (alkaline earth metals) with the configuration [Uuo] 8s².
Metallic character increases down a group and decreases across a period from left to right. This trend explains the gradual change from metallic to non‑metallic behavior in the periodic table.
Elements are classified into metals, non‑metals and metalloids based on their physical and chemical properties such as conductivity, state at room temperature, and reactivity.

concepts.concept_title: Physical property trends across periods and groups
concepts.description: Melting point, boiling point, heats of fusion and vaporisation show characteristic trends when moving across a period or down a group, reflecting changes in bonding and atomic structure.

concepts.concept_title: Conductivity differences between metals and non‑metals
concepts.description: Metals are good conductors of heat and electricity due to delocalised electrons, whereas non‑metals are poor conductors and act as insulators.

concepts.concept_title: Malleability and ductility of metals vs brittleness of non‑metals
concepts.description: Metals can be hammered into sheets (malleable) and drawn into wires (ductile) because of metallic bonding; non‑metals are generally brittle and lack such deformability.
Mercury, gallium and caesium are metals with unusually low melting points, while boron and carbon are non‑metals with high melting points, illustrating exceptions to general periodic trends.

concepts.concept_title: Chemical reactivity trends of alkali metals and halogens
concepts.description: Reactivity of Group 1 alkali metals increases down the group, whereas reactivity of Group 17 halogens decreases down the group, reflecting changes in ionisation energy and electron affinity.

concepts.concept_title: Atomic structure basis of periodic trends
concepts.description: Periodic variations in physical and chemical properties arise from the number of electrons and the principal energy levels occupied by atoms in each element.
The distance from the nucleus to the outermost electron cloud of an atom, expressed in picometers or ångströms. It is a comparative measure of the size of different atoms.

concepts.concept_title: Trend of atomic radius across a period
concepts.description: Atomic radius decreases from left to right within a period because the effective nuclear charge increases while the principal quantum number remains constant.

concepts.concept_title: Trend of atomic radius down a group
concepts.description: Atomic radius increases down a group as the principal quantum number (n) of the valence shell increases, outweighing the increase in nuclear charge.
The net positive charge experienced by valence electrons after accounting for shielding by inner‑shell electrons; a higher Z_eff pulls electrons closer, reducing atomic size.
Half the internuclear distance in a homonuclear covalent bond; used to estimate the size of non‑metallic atoms.
Half the distance between the nuclei of two adjacent metal atoms in a metallic crystal; employed to describe the size of metallic elements.
Ionization enthalpy generally increases across a period due to increasing Z_eff and decreases down a group because electrons are farther from the nucleus and more shielded.
Electron gain enthalpy becomes more negative across a period (except for group 2 and 13 elements) and less negative down a group, reflecting the ease of adding an electron.
Electronegativity increases from left to right across a period and decreases down a group, mirroring the variations in Z_eff and atomic radius.
Metallic character decreases across a period and increases down a group; elements like Si, Be, Mg, Na, P can be ordered based on this trend.

concepts.concept_title: Relation between principal quantum number and atomic size
concepts.description: A higher principal quantum number (n) indicates valence electrons are in shells farther from the nucleus, leading to larger atomic radii.
Inner‑shell electrons partially neutralize the nuclear charge felt by valence electrons; greater shielding reduces Z_eff and expands the atomic radius.

concepts.concept_title: Atomic radius trend across a period
concepts.description: Atomic radius decreases from left to right across a period because the effective nuclear charge increases while the principal quantum number remains unchanged.

concepts.concept_title: Atomic radius trend down a group
concepts.description: Atomic radius increases down a group due to the addition of successive electron shells, which outweighs the increase in nuclear charge.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of ionic charge on ionic radius
concepts.description: Cations are smaller than their parent atoms and the radius decreases with higher positive charge; anions are larger and the radius increases with higher negative charge because of electron‑electron repulsion.
For species having the same number of electrons, the one with the larger nuclear charge possesses the smaller radius.
Atomic radii are obtained from X‑ray diffraction of crystals or from spectroscopic measurements for isolated atoms; van der Waals radii refer to non‑bonded distances.

concepts.concept_title: Covalent vs van der Waals radii
concepts.description: Covalent radius is half the distance between two identical atoms bonded together, whereas van der Waals radius is half the distance between non‑bonded atoms in a crystal lattice.

concepts.concept_title: Estimating ionic radii from crystal structures
concepts.description: Ionic radii are derived by measuring interionic distances in ionic crystals and subtracting the known radius of one ion to obtain the radius of the other.
Ionization enthalpy is the energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom in its ground state, reflecting the atom’s tendency to lose electrons.
The first ionization enthalpy is the enthalpy change when a gaseous atom loses one electron to form a singly charged cation. Represented by the reaction X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻.
The second ionization enthalpy is the energy required to remove the second electron from a singly charged gaseous cation, given by X⁺(g) → X²⁺(g) + e⁻.
Each successive ionization enthalpy is higher than the preceding one because removing an electron from a positively charged ion requires more energy.
First ionization enthalpy generally increases from left to right across a period due to increasing nuclear charge with little change in shielding.
First ionization enthalpy decreases down a group because the valence electrons are farther from the nucleus and experience greater shielding.

concepts.concept_title: Maxima and minima in ionization enthalpy
concepts.description: Ionization enthalpy shows maxima at noble gases (closed shells) and minima at alkali metals (single valence electron), reflecting stability of electron configurations.
Two main factors govern ionization enthalpy: (i) the attraction between nucleus and valence electron (effective nuclear charge) and (ii) repulsion among electrons.
Effective nuclear charge is the net positive charge experienced by a valence electron, calculated as the actual nuclear charge minus the shielding contributed by inner electrons.
Shielding or screening refers to the reduction of nuclear attraction on outer electrons due to the presence of inner‑shell electrons, lowering ionization enthalpy.
Isoelectronic species are two or more atoms/ions that have the same number of electrons, identical valence electron count and similar structure, irrespective of the elements involved.

concepts.concept_title: Correlation between ionization enthalpy and atomic radius
concepts.description: Ionization enthalpy and atomic radius are inversely related; as atomic radius increases, the distance between nucleus and valence electron grows, reducing ionization enthalpy.
Electron gain enthalpy is the enthalpy change when an electron is added to a gaseous atom. A negative value indicates the process is exothermic, while a positive value indicates it is endothermic.

concepts.concept_title: Trend of electron gain enthalpy across a period
concepts.description: Across a period, electron gain enthalpy generally becomes more negative from left to right due to increasing effective nuclear charge and smaller atomic size, making it easier to add an electron.

concepts.concept_title: Exception of noble gases in electron gain enthalpy trend
concepts.description: Noble gases exhibit positive or less negative electron gain enthalpies because an added electron must occupy the next higher principal quantum level, leading to an unstable electronic configuration.

concepts.concept_title: Trend of electron gain enthalpy down a group
concepts.description: Within a group, electron gain enthalpy becomes less negative (or more positive) down the group as atomic radius increases, reducing the attractive force between the nucleus and the added electron.

concepts.concept_title: High negative electron gain enthalpy of halogens
concepts.description: Halogens possess large negative electron gain enthalpies because gaining one electron completes their valence shell, attaining a stable noble gas configuration.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of orbital type on electron gain enthalpy
concepts.description: Adding an electron to a 2p orbital results in greater electron-electron repulsion compared to a 3p orbital, influencing the magnitude of electron gain enthalpy for elements in the same period.

concepts.concept_title: Most negative electron gain enthalpy element
concepts.description: Among the main group elements, chlorine exhibits the most negative electron gain enthalpy, reflecting its strong tendency to accept an electron.

concepts.concept_title: Least negative electron gain enthalpy element
concepts.description: Phosphorus shows the least negative (or most positive) electron gain enthalpy among the elements listed, indicating a weaker tendency to gain an electron.
Electronegativity is a qualitative measure of an atom's ability to attract shared electrons in a chemical bond.

concepts.concept_title: Trend of electronegativity across a period
concepts.description: Electronegativity increases from left to right across a period as the effective nuclear charge rises and atomic radius decreases, enhancing the atom's pull on bonding electrons.

concepts.concept_title: Trend of electronegativity down a group
concepts.description: Electronegativity decreases down a group because the increase in atomic size places the valence electrons farther from the nucleus, reducing the atom's ability to attract electrons.

concepts.concept_title: Comparison between ionization enthalpy and electron gain enthalpy
concepts.description: While ionization enthalpy refers to the energy required to remove an electron from an atom, electron gain enthalpy pertains to the energy change when an electron is added; their trends are generally opposite across the periodic table.

concepts.concept_title: Electron affinity definition and sign convention
concepts.description: Electron affinity (A) is the energy change when a neutral atom gains an electron. In NEET convention, a released energy makes A positive, while energy required makes A negative.

concepts.concept_title: Electron affinity as negative of electron gain enthalpy
concepts.description: The electron affinity is numerically the negative of the enthalpy change for the electron‑gain process: ΔH = –A – (5/2)RT, linking thermodynamics with EA.

concepts.concept_title: Anomalous electron affinity of O and F
concepts.description: Oxygen and fluorine have less negative (or even positive) electron affinities because the added electron enters a smaller n=2 level and experiences strong electron‑electron repulsion.
Linus Pauling assigned fluorine a value of 4.0 and derived electronegativity values for other elements based on bond energies, making it the most widely used scale.

Alternative quantitative scales, Mulliken‑Jaffe (based on ionization energy and electron affinity) and Allred‑Rochow (based on effective nuclear charge), provide different numerical electronegativity values.

concepts.concept_title: Periodic trend of electronegativity across a period
concepts.description: Electronegativity increases from left to right across a period due to decreasing atomic radius and increasing effective nuclear charge, enhancing attraction for bonding electrons.

concepts.concept_title: Periodic trend of electronegativity down a group
concepts.description: Electronegativity decreases down a group because atomic radius increases, reducing the nucleus's pull on valence electrons despite a constant nuclear charge.

concepts.concept_title: Correlation between electronegativity, atomic radius and ionization enthalpy
concepts.description: Higher electronegativity corresponds to smaller atomic radius and higher ionization enthalpy, reflecting stronger nuclear attraction for valence electrons.

concepts.concept_title: Use of electronegativity to predict bond character
concepts.description: The difference in electronegativity between two atoms determines bond type: non‑polar covalent, polar covalent, or ionic, aiding prediction of molecular properties.
Within a given period, electronegativity values rise from left to right as atomic size decreases and effective nuclear charge increases.
Moving down a group, electronegativity values fall because of the addition of electron shells, which increase distance from the nucleus and shield the valence electrons.

concepts.concept_title: Fluorine: highest electronegativity and oxidation state –1
concepts.description: Fluorine possesses the maximum electronegativity on the Pauling scale and therefore always adopts an oxidation state of –1 in its compounds.
Oxidation state is the hypothetical charge an atom would have if all bonds to it were completely ionic, reflecting the electron loss or gain relative to the neutral atom.
Oxidation numbers are assigned by giving the more electronegative element a negative oxidation state; examples include Na₂O (O –2) and OF₂ (O +2, because fluorine is more electronegative).
Higher electronegativity corresponds to stronger non‑metallic behaviour, whereas lower electronegativity is associated with metallic properties.

concepts.concept_title: Periodicity of valence (oxidation) across the second period
concepts.description: Elements of the second period show a systematic change in valence from 1 (Li) to 4 (C) and then decrease, reflecting the filling of the 2p subshell.

concepts.concept_title: Typical valence of group 14 elements and halogens
concepts.description: Group 14 elements (e.g., Si) usually exhibit a valence of 4, whereas halogens (e.g., Br) commonly show a valence of 1 in their compounds.
The formula of a binary compound can be derived by balancing the valences of the constituent elements, such as Si (valence 4) with Br (valence 1) yielding SiBr₄.

concepts.concept_title: Exceptional oxidation state of oxygen in OF₂
concepts.description: Oxygen generally shows an oxidation state of –2, but in OF₂ it assumes +2 because fluorine’s electronegativity is higher, forcing oxygen to lose electrons.
Oxidation state is frequently employed as a measure of the valence of an element. It indicates the number of electrons an atom gains, loses or shares when forming compounds.
The valence of an element can be calculated as eight minus the number of electrons in its outermost shell, reflecting the tendency to achieve a noble‑gas configuration.

concepts.concept_title: Periodic trend in valence from hydride formulas
concepts.description: The stoichiometry of binary hydrides (e.g., LiH, NaH, AlH₃) across groups shows a systematic change in valence, which follows the group number of the metal.

concepts.concept_title: Periodic trend in valence from oxide formulas
concepts.description: Binary oxides (e.g., Li₂O, MgO, Al₂O₃) display a regular variation in the metal‑oxygen ratio, reflecting the oxidation state (valence) of the metal in each group.

concepts.concept_title: Electronegativity order among F, O and Na
concepts.description: Within the second period, fluorine is the most electronegative element, followed by oxygen and then sodium, a trend that influences bond polarity in their compounds.

concepts.concept_title: Diagonal relationship in the periodic table
concepts.description: Elements positioned diagonally (e.g., Li‑Mg and Be‑Al) exhibit similar chemical behaviour owing to comparable ionic sizes and charge densities, despite belonging to different groups.
Lithium shows higher ionisation energy, smaller atomic radius and greater covalent character compared to other alkali metals, owing to its small size and high charge‑to‑radius ratio.
Beryllium differs from other alkaline earth metals by having a high ionisation energy, strong tendency to form covalent compounds, and a limited ability to expand its coordination number.
The first element of each group (e.g., B, C, N, O, F) can form a maximum number of covalent bonds equal to its group number, limited by the availability of valence orbitals (2s and 2p).
Transition metals possess partially filled d‑orbitals, allowing them to exhibit several oxidation states in different compounds.
Actinoid series elements display a wide range of oxidation states due to the involvement of 5f, 6d and 7s orbitals in bonding.

concepts.concept_title: Influence of charge density on bonding character
concepts.description: A small ionic radius combined with a high charge (high charge density) leads to increased polarising power of a cation, resulting in more covalent character in its compounds.

concepts.concept_title: Increase in valence orbitals from second to third period
concepts.description: Elements of the third period have nine valence orbitals (3s, 3p, 3d) compared to the eight of the second period (2s, 2p), allowing higher coordination numbers and greater covalency.

concepts.concept_title: Metallic vs non‑metallic character across a period
concepts.description: Metallic character decreases and non‑metallic character increases while moving from left to right across a period due to changes in atomic size, ionization energy and electron affinity.

concepts.concept_title: Oxidation state and covalency as metallic character indicators
concepts.description: Higher oxidation states and higher covalency are associated with greater metallic character; for example, Al shows +3 oxidation state and covalency of 6, reflecting its metallic nature.

concepts.concept_title: Reactivity of elements with oxygen and oxide formation
concepts.description: Elements at the extreme left and right of a period readily combine with oxygen to form oxides, illustrating the influence of metallic and non‑metallic character on chemical reactivity.
Oxides are classified as basic, acidic, amphoteric or neutral based on their reaction with water or acids/bases; examples include Na2O (basic), Cl2O7 (acidic), Al2O3 (amphoteric), CO (neutral).

concepts.concept_title: Atomic and ionic radii trend across a period
concepts.description: Both atomic and ionic radii decrease steadily from left to right across a period because of increasing nuclear charge pulling electrons closer to the nucleus.

concepts.concept_title: Ionization enthalpy trend across a period
concepts.description: Ionization enthalpy generally increases across a period as electrons are held more tightly by the increasing effective nuclear charge, with few exceptions.

concepts.concept_title: Electron gain enthalpy trend across a period
concepts.description: Electron gain enthalpy becomes more negative (more exothermic) from left to right across a period, reflecting the greater tendency to accept electrons.

concepts.concept_title: Correlation of oxide type with position in period
concepts.description: Oxides of elements on the left side of a period are predominantly basic, whereas those of elements on the right side are acidic; amphoteric oxides occur for elements with intermediate character.

concepts.concept_title: Reaction of basic oxides with water
concepts.description: Basic oxides react with water to produce strong bases, e.g., Na2O + H2O → 2 NaOH, illustrating their alkaline nature.

concepts.concept_title: Reaction of acidic oxides with water
concepts.description: Acidic oxides react with water to form acids, for instance, Cl2O7 + H2O → 2 HClO4, demonstrating their acidic behavior.

Elements at the extreme left (alkali metals) lose electrons easily, while those at the extreme right (halogens) gain electrons readily. This gives maximum reactivity at both ends and minimum in the middle of a period.

concepts.concept_title: Ionization enthalpy variation across a period
concepts.description: Ionization enthalpy generally increases from left to right in a period due to increasing nuclear charge, but the increase is smaller for transition metals compared to s‑ and p‑block elements.

concepts.concept_title: Atomic radius trend in the periodic table
concepts.description: Atomic radius decreases across a period because of stronger effective nuclear charge, while it increases down a group due to addition of electron shells.
Metallic character diminishes from left to right across a period and enhances down a group, reflecting the change from electropositive to electronegative behavior.

concepts.concept_title: Classification of elements into s, p, d, f blocks
concepts.description: Elements are grouped into s‑block, p‑block, d‑block and f‑block based on the type of atomic orbital that receives the differentiating electron in their ground‑state configuration.
Mendeleev arranged elements by increasing atomic mass, whereas the modern periodic table orders them by atomic number, leading to correct periodicity of properties.

concepts.concept_title: Valence‑shell electronic configuration and group similarity
concepts.description: Elements belonging to the same group possess identical valence‑shell electron configurations, which accounts for their similar chemical and physical properties.
Metalloids (e.g., Si, Ge, As) occupy the border region between metals and non‑metals and display intermediate properties such as semiconductivity.

concepts.concept_title: Reactivity of transition metals compared to s‑ and p‑block elements
concepts.description: Transition metals, especially those of the 3d and 4f series, exhibit lower reactivity than the highly reactive s‑block alkali metals and p‑block halogens, due to their higher ionization enthalpies and smaller changes in atomic size.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship between ionization enthalpy and redox behaviour
concepts.description: Elements with low ionization enthalpy tend to act as reducing agents, whereas those with high ionization enthalpy behave as oxidizing agents, influencing their position in redox series.

concepts.concept_title: Periodicity of electronegativity across a period
concepts.description: Electronegativity increases steadily from left to right within a period due to increasing effective nuclear charge and decreasing atomic radius. This trend helps predict bond polarity and reactivity of elements.

concepts.concept_title: Periodicity of electronegativity down a group
concepts.description: Electronegativity decreases down a group because of the addition of electron shells, which increase atomic size and shield the valence electrons from the nucleus. The trend influences acid strength and oxidation‑reduction behavior.

concepts.concept_title: Relative electronegativity order of F, O, Cl, N
concepts.description: Among the elements fluorine, oxygen, chlorine and nitrogen, the electronegativity follows the order F > O > Cl > N. This ordering is useful for comparing bond polarity and predicting the direction of electron flow in reactions.

The Kössel-Lewis approach explains the formation of chemical bonds in terms of electrons. It involves calculating the number of valence electrons to determine the common or group valence of an element.
The octet rule states that atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve a full outer shell with eight electrons. This rule helps explain the formation of chemical bonds.
Lewis structures are diagrams that show the arrangement of electrons in an atom or molecule. They are used to represent the bonding between atoms in a molecule.
There are several types of chemical bonds, including ionic, covalent, and metallic bonds. Each type of bond has distinct characteristics and properties.
The Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion (VSEPR) theory explains the shapes of molecules based on the repulsion between electron pairs in the valence shell.
The valence bond approach explains the formation of covalent bonds in terms of the overlap of atomic orbitals.
Hybridisation involves the mixing of atomic orbitals to form new hybrid orbitals that are suitable for bonding.
The molecular orbital theory explains the formation of molecules in terms of the combination of atomic orbitals to form molecular orbitals.
A hydrogen bond is a type of intermolecular force that arises between molecules with a hydrogen atom bonded to a highly electronegative atom.

concepts.concept_title: Kössel and Lewis Explanation of Valence
concepts.description: Kössel and Lewis provided a logical explanation of valence based on the inertness of noble gases. They explained that atoms achieve a stable octet of electrons when linked by chemical bonds.
Lewis pictured the atom as a positively charged 'Kernel' (nucleus plus inner electrons) and an outer shell that can accommodate a maximum of eight electrons.
The octet rule states that atoms achieve a stable octet of electrons when linked by chemical bonds, which is a particularly stable electronic arrangement.
The formation of negative and positive ions from atoms is associated with the gain and loss of electrons, resulting in a stable noble gas electronic configuration.
The electrostatic attraction between positively and negatively charged ions stabilizes the ions and forms a chemical bond.
Lewis symbols are simple notations used to represent valence electrons in an atom, which take part in chemical combination.
The number of dots around the symbol in Lewis symbols represents the number of valence electrons, which is significant in understanding chemical bonding.

The octet rule states that atoms can combine by gaining, losing, or sharing valence electrons to have an octet in their valence shells. This results in a stable electronic configuration similar to that of the nearest noble gas.

Electrovalence is the number of unit charges on an ion. It is equal to the number of electrons gained or lost by an atom to form an ion. For example, calcium has an electrovalence of +2, while chlorine has an electrovalence of -1.
Lewis dot structures are diagrams that represent the valence electrons of atoms as dots. They are used to visualize the formation of chemical bonds between atoms.
A covalent bond is formed when two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons to achieve a stable electronic configuration. This results in a chemical bond between the atoms.

Multiple bonds are formed when two atoms share more than one pair of electrons. Examples include double bonds, where two pairs of electrons are shared, and triple bonds, where three pairs of electrons are shared.
Lewis dot structures provide a picture of bonding in molecules and ions in terms of shared pairs of electrons and the octet rule.
The total number of electrons required for writing the structures are obtained by adding the valence electrons of the combining atoms.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Charges on Valence Electrons
concepts.description: For anions, each negative charge would mean addition of one electron. For cations, each positive charge would result in subtraction of one electron from the total.
In general, the least electronegative atom occupies the central position in the molecule/ion.
Each bonded atom gets an octet of electrons.
The Lewis dot structures can be written by adopting the steps: counting valence electrons, determining central atom, and distributing electrons as bonding pairs and lone pairs.
Lewis dot structures are diagrams that show the bonding between atoms in a molecule. They are drawn by representing the valence electrons as dots around the atoms.
Formal charge is the difference between the number of valence electrons of an atom in its free state and the number of electrons assigned to it in a Lewis structure.

The formal charge of an atom in a Lewis structure is calculated as: Formal charge = total number of valence electrons in the free atom - total number of non-bonding electrons - (1/2) bonding electrons.
The octet rule is not universal and has exceptions, especially for elements with less than four valence electrons. Some compounds have incomplete octets or expanded octets.
In some compounds, the central atom has less than eight electrons in its valence shell, especially for elements with less than four valence electrons.
Molecules with an odd number of electrons do not satisfy the octet rule for all atoms. Examples include nitric oxide (NO) and nitrogen dioxide (NO2).
Elements in and beyond the third period have available 3d orbitals for bonding, leading to more than eight valence electrons around the central atom. Examples include PF5, SF6, and H2SO4.
Formal charges help in keeping track of valence electrons in a molecule and selecting the lowest energy Lewis structure. They do not indicate real charge separation within the molecule.

The octet rule has limitations, such as not accounting for the shape of molecules and not explaining the relative stability of molecules. It also does not apply to molecules with an odd number of electrons or expanded octets.
Ionic bonds are formed between elements with comparatively low ionization enthalpies and elements with comparatively high negative electron gain enthalpy.

concepts.concept_title: Exceptions to octet rule in noble gases
concepts.description: Some noble gases, such as xenon and krypton, form compounds with oxygen and fluorine, exceptions to the octet rule.
The formation of ionic compounds depends on the ease of formation of positive and negative ions from neutral atoms and the arrangement of ions in the solid.
The energy required to remove an electron from a neutral atom to form a positive ion.
The enthalpy change when a gas phase atom gains an electron.
The energy required to completely separate one mole of a solid ionic compound into gaseous constituent ions.
A measure of the stability of an ionic compound, provided by its enthalpy of lattice formation.
Bond length is the equilibrium distance between the nuclei of two bonded atoms in a molecule. It is measured by spectroscopic, X-ray diffraction, and electron-diffraction techniques.
Covalent radius is half of the distance between two similar atoms joined by a covalent bond. It is approximately the radius of an atom's core in contact with the core of an adjacent atom.
Van der Waals radius is half of the distance between two similar atoms in separate molecules in a solid.
Bond angle is the angle between the orbitals containing bonding electron pairs around the central atom in a molecule/complex ion.
Bond enthalpy is the amount of energy required to break one mole of bonds of a particular type between two atoms in a gaseous state.
The energy required to break a chemical bond in a molecule. It is an indicator of bond strength.
The number of bonds between two atoms in a molecule. It is a measure of bond strength.
The average energy required to break a bond in a polyatomic molecule.
The concept used to describe molecules that cannot be accurately represented by a single Lewis structure.

concepts.concept_title: Correlation between Bond Order and Stability
concepts.description: Molecules with higher bond order tend to be more stable.
A single Lewis structure may not accurately represent a molecule. Resonance structures are used to describe a molecule's structure when a single Lewis structure is inadequate.
The canonical forms have no real existence. The molecule does not exist for a certain fraction of time in one canonical form and for other fractions of time in other forms.
Resonance stabilizes the molecule as the energy of the resonance hybrid is less than the energy of any single canonical structure.
Resonance averages the bond characteristics as a whole.
The existence of a hundred percent ionic or covalent bond represents an ideal situation.
Bond enthalpy increases and bond length decreases. Non-bonding pairs of electrons affect bond enthalpy and length.
A covalent bond formed between two identical atoms, where the shared pair of electrons is equally attracted by the two atoms.
A covalent bond formed between two different atoms, where the shared electron pair is displaced more towards the atom with higher electronegativity.

A measure of the separation of positive and negative charges in a molecule, represented by the product of the magnitude of the charge and the distance between the centres of positive and negative charge.
The net dipole moment of a molecule is the vector sum of the dipole moments of various bonds in the molecule.
A set of Lewis structures that describe the delocalization of electrons in a molecule, used to represent the hybrid structure of the molecule.
The concept that a molecule's structure is a hybrid of different resonance structures, used to explain the molecule's properties.

Dipole moment is a measure of the separation of positive and negative charges within a molecule. It is a vector quantity that depends on the magnitude of the charge and the distance between the charges. The direction of the dipole moment is from the positive charge to the negative charge.

The dipole moment of a molecule is affected by the electronegativity of the atoms, the shape of the molecule, and the presence of lone pairs of electrons. The dipole moment is also affected by the size of the cation and anion in ionic bonds.
Ionic bonds have partial covalent character due to the polarization of the anion by the cation. The covalent character of an ionic bond depends on the size and charge of the cation and anion.
Fajans' rules state that the covalent character of an ionic bond increases with the increase in charge on the cation, decrease in size of the cation, and increase in size of the anion.

The polarizing power of a cation is its ability to polarize an anion, while the polarisability of an anion is its ability to be polarized. These factors determine the covalent character of an ionic bond.
The Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion (VSEPR) theory provides a simple procedure to predict the shapes of covalent molecules based on the repulsion between electron pairs.

VSEPR theory proposes that the shape of a molecule depends on the number of valence shell electron pairs around the central atom. It states that electron pairs in the valence shell repel each other and tend to occupy positions in space that minimize repulsion.

The main postulates of VSEPR theory are: (1) shape of a molecule depends on the number of valence shell electron pairs, (2) electron pairs in the valence shell repel each other, (3) electron pairs occupy positions that minimize repulsion, and (4) valence shell is taken as a sphere with electron pairs localizing on the surface.

The arrangement of electron pairs around a central atom can be: linear, trigonal planar, tetrahedral, trigonal-bipyramidal, and octahedral. These geometries correspond to the compounds of AB2, AB3, AB4, AB5, and AB6, respectively.

concepts.concept_title: Treatment of Multiple Bonds in VSEPR Theory
concepts.description: In VSEPR theory, a multiple bond is treated as if it is a single electron pair. The two or three electron pairs of a multiple bond are treated as a single super pair.

concepts.concept_title: Lone Pair and Bond Pair Repulsions
concepts.description: The repulsive interaction of electron pairs decreases in the order: lone pair (lp) – lone pair (lp) > lone pair (lp) – bond pair (bp) > bond pair (bp) – bond pair (bp).
key_terms: lone pair and bond pair repulsions

concepts.concept_title: Refinement of VSEPR Model by Nyholm and Gillespie
concepts.description: Nyholm and Gillespie refined the VSEPR model by explaining the important difference between lone pairs and bonding pairs.
VSEPR theory states that electron pairs in a molecule repel each other and move as far apart as possible. This theory helps in determining the geometry of molecules.

concepts.concept_title: Lone Pair and Bond Pair Repulsions
concepts.description: Lone pair electrons occupy more space and cause greater repulsion compared to bond pair electrons. This affects the shape of molecules.

concepts.concept_title: Geometry of Molecules with No Lone Pair
concepts.description: Molecules with no lone pair on the central atom have specific geometries, such as linear, trigonal planar, and tetrahedral.

concepts.concept_title: Geometry of Molecules with One or More Lone Pairs
concepts.description: Molecules with one or more lone pairs on the central atom have specific geometries, such as bent, trigonal pyramidal, and see-saw.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Lone Pair on Molecular Shape
concepts.description: The presence of lone pairs affects the shape of molecules by causing greater repulsion and changing the bond angles.
The stability of molecular arrangements depends on the position of lone pairs and bond pairs, with equatorial positions being more stable.
The Lewis approach fails to explain the formation of chemical bonds and doesn't give reasons for differences in bond dissociation enthalpies and bond lengths.
The valence bond theory explains chemical bond formation using atomic orbitals, electronic configurations, overlap criteria, and hybridization.
The formation of a hydrogen molecule involves the sharing of an electron pair between two hydrogen atoms, resulting in a stable molecule with a bond length of 74 pm.
Attractive forces between nuclei and electrons of different atoms and repulsive forces between electrons and nuclei of different atoms influence bond formation.
The energy released when a bond is formed is called bond enthalpy, which corresponds to the minimum energy in the potential energy curve.
The formation of a covalent bond between two atoms results by pairing of electrons present in the valence shell having opposite spins. The extent of overlap decides the strength of a covalent bond.
The covalent bond is formed by overlapping of atomic orbitals. The geometry of polyatomic molecules like CH4, NH3, and H2O is important in addition to bond formation.

When orbitals of two atoms come close to form a bond, their overlap may be positive, negative, or zero depending upon the sign (phase) and direction of orientation of amplitude of orbital wave function in space.
Orbitals forming a bond should have the same sign (phase) and orientation in space. This is called positive overlap.
The potential energy curve for the formation of a molecule as a function of internuclear distance between atoms. The minimum in the curve corresponds to the most stable state of the molecule.
Atomic orbitals overlap to form covalent bonds. The direction of the bond is determined by the orientation of the overlapping orbitals.
There are two types of overlapping: sigma (σ) and pi (π). Sigma bonds are formed by end-to-end overlap, while pi bonds are formed by side-by-side overlap.
A sigma bond is formed by the end-to-end overlap of bonding orbitals along the internuclear axis. It can be formed by s-s, s-p, or p-p overlapping.

concepts.concept_title: Strength of Sigma and Pi Bonds
concepts.description: The strength of a bond depends on the extent of overlapping. Sigma bonds are stronger than pi bonds due to greater overlapping.
Hybridization is the process of combining atomic orbitals to form new hybrid orbitals that explain the characteristic geometrical shapes of polyatomic molecules.
The process of intermixing of atomic orbitals of slightly different energies to form new set of equivalent orbitals known as hybrid orbitals.
The number of hybrid orbitals equals the number of atomic orbitals that get hybridised. Hybridised orbitals are equivalent in energy and shape.
There are various types of hybridisation involving s, p and d orbitals.
Involves the mixing of one s and one p-orbital to form two equivalent sp hybrid orbitals.
Involves the mixing of one s and two p-orbitals to form three equivalent sp2 hybrid orbitals.
Orbitals present in the valence shell of the atom are hybridised. Orbitals undergoing hybridisation should have almost equal energy.
Type of overlap that takes place between half-filled p-orbitals of two approaching atoms.
Type of bond formed by sidewise overlapping of atomic orbitals.

sp hybridisation involves mixing of one s and one p orbital, resulting in two equivalent sp hybrid orbitals with 50% s-character and 50% p-character. This type of hybridisation is also known as diagonal hybridisation.
sp2 hybridisation involves mixing of one s and two p orbitals, resulting in three equivalent sp2 hybrid orbitals. These orbitals are oriented in a trigonal planar arrangement.
sp3 hybridisation involves mixing of one s and three p orbitals, resulting in four equivalent sp3 hybrid orbitals. These orbitals are directed towards the four corners of a tetrahedron.
