A molecule with sp hybridisation has a linear geometry, with the central atom linked directly to two other atoms.
A molecule with sp2 hybridisation has a trigonal planar geometry, with the central atom linked to three other atoms.
A molecule with sp3 hybridisation has a tetrahedral geometry, with the central atom linked to four other atoms.
A molecule with sp3 hybridisation and a lone pair of electrons has a pyramidal geometry.
sp3 hybridisation occurs when one s and three p orbitals combine to form four equivalent hybrid orbitals. This results in a tetrahedral geometry.
The presence of lone pairs in a molecule with sp3 hybridisation results in a V-shape or angular geometry. The bond angle is reduced from 109.5°.
sp2 hybridisation occurs when one s and two p orbitals combine to form three equivalent hybrid orbitals. This results in a trigonal planar geometry.
The sidewise overlap of unhybridised p orbitals results in the formation of a pi (π) bond.
sp hybridisation occurs when one s and one p orbital combine to form two equivalent hybrid orbitals. This results in a linear geometry.

concepts.concept_title: Hybridisation of Elements Involving d Orbitals
concepts.description: Elements in the third period and beyond can exhibit hybridisation involving d orbitals, resulting in expanded octets.

concepts.concept_title: Hybridization involving s, p and d orbitals
concepts.description: Hybridization schemes involving s, p and d orbitals, such as dsp2, sp3d, sp3d2, and d2sp3.
Relationship between hybridization and shape of molecules/ions, such as square planar, trigonal bipyramidal, square pyramidal, and octahedral.
sp3d hybridisation in PCl5, resulting in a trigonal bipyramidal geometry.
sp3d2 hybridisation in SF6, resulting in an octahedral geometry.
Basic principles of MO theory, including the presence of electrons in molecular orbitals.
The central sulphur atom in SF6 has six orbitals (one s, three p, and two d) that hybridise to form six new sp3d2 hybrid orbitals, projected towards the corners of a regular octahedron.
Molecular orbitals are formed by combining atomic orbitals of comparable energies and proper symmetry. The number of molecular orbitals formed is equal to the number of combining atomic orbitals.
Molecular orbitals can be described by the linear combination of atomic orbitals that can take place by addition and subtraction of wave functions of individual atomic orbitals.

The combination of two atomic orbitals forms two molecular orbitals: one bonding (σ) and one antibonding (σ*). The bonding molecular orbital has lower energy and greater stability than the antibonding molecular orbital.
Molecular orbitals are filled in accordance with the Aufbau principle, obeying the Pauli's exclusion principle and the Hund's rule.

Molecular orbitals are formed by linear combination of atomic orbitals (LCAO). The formation of molecular orbitals can be understood in terms of constructive or destructive interference of electron waves of the combining atoms.

In a bonding molecular orbital, the electron density is located between the nuclei of the bonded atoms. In an antibonding molecular orbital, most of the electron density is located away from the space between the nuclei.
The combining atomic orbitals must have same or nearly same energy and same symmetry. The combining atomic orbitals must overlap to the maximum extent.
Molecular orbitals of diatomic molecules are designated as σ (sigma), π (pi), δ (delta), etc. Sigma (σ) molecular orbitals are symmetrical around the bond-axis.
Molecular orbitals are formed by combining atomic orbitals of two atoms. The total energy of two molecular orbitals remains the same as that of two original atomic orbitals.
The combining atomic orbitals must have the same or nearly the same energy and the same symmetry about the molecular axis.
Sigma molecular orbitals are symmetrical around the bond axis, while pi molecular orbitals are not symmetrical due to the presence of positive and negative lobes.

concepts.concept_title: Energy Level Diagram for Molecular Orbitals
concepts.description: The energy levels of molecular orbitals are determined experimentally and can be represented in an energy level diagram.
A positive bond order indicates a stable molecule, while a negative or zero bond order indicates an unstable molecule.
Bond order is defined as one half the difference between the number of electrons present in the bonding and the antibonding orbitals. It is an approximate measure of the bond length.
The increasing order of energies of various molecular orbitals is: σ1s < σ*1s < σ2s < σ*2s < (π2px = π2py) < σ2pz < (π*2px = π*2py) < σ*2pz
If all the molecular orbitals in a molecule are doubly occupied, the substance is diamagnetic. If one or more molecular orbitals are singly occupied, it is paramagnetic.
A molecule is stable if the number of electrons occupying bonding orbitals is greater than the number occupying antibonding orbitals.
The distribution of electrons among various molecular orbitals is called the electronic configuration of the molecule.
The bond length decreases as bond order increases.
The electronic configuration of He2 molecule is (σ1s)2 (σ*1s)2. The bond order of He2 is 0, making it unstable and nonexistent.
The electronic configuration of O2 molecule is (σ1s)2 (σ*1s)2 (σ2s)2 (σ*2s)2 (σ2pz)2 (π2px2 = π2py2) (π*2px1 ≡ π*2py1). The bond order of O2 is 2, and it has two unpaired electrons.
The electronic configuration of Li2 molecule is (σ1s)2 (σ*1s)2 (σ2s)2. The bond order of Li2 is 1, making it stable and diamagnetic.
The electronic configuration of C2 molecule is (σ1s)2 (σ*1s)2 (σ2s)2 (σ*2s)2 (π2px2 = π2py2).
Bond order determines the stability of a molecule. A positive bond order indicates a stable molecule, while a zero or negative bond order indicates instability.
Molecules with unpaired electrons are paramagnetic, while those with paired electrons are diamagnetic.
Bond order is calculated as half the difference between the number of electrons in bonding and antibonding orbitals. It is a measure of bond strength.
Diamagnetism is a property of molecules with paired electrons, resulting in no net magnetic moment. Diamagnetic molecules are weakly repelled by magnetic fields.
Hydrogen bonding is a type of intermolecular force that arises between molecules with a hydrogen atom bonded to a highly electronegative atom (F, O, or N).
Intermolecular hydrogen bonding occurs between two different molecules of the same or different compounds.
Intramolecular hydrogen bonding occurs within a single molecule, where a hydrogen atom is bonded to two highly electronegative atoms.
Electrovalency is the concept of electrostatic attraction between ions, leading to their stability, and is related to the attainment of noble gas configurations.
Covalent bonding involves the sharing of electron pairs between atoms, leading to the attainment of noble gas configurations.
Lewis dot symbols and structures show the number of valence electrons and pictorial representations of bonding in molecules.
An ionic compound is pictured as a three-dimensional aggregation of positive and negative ions in an ordered arrangement called the crystal lattice, stabilized by the enthalpy of lattice formation.
Multiple bonds result from the sharing of two or three electron pairs, and some bonded atoms have additional pairs of electrons not involved in bonding, called lone-pairs.
Important parameters associated with chemical bonds include bond length, bond angle, bond enthalpy, bond order, and bond polarity.

A number of molecules and polyatomic ions cannot be described accurately by a single Lewis structure, and a number of descriptions based on the same skeletal structure are written, taken together to represent the molecule or ion.
The VSEPR model predicts the geometrical shapes of molecules based on the assumption that electron pairs repel each other and tend to remain as far apart as possible.
The valence bond approach to covalent bonding discusses bond formation in terms of overlap of orbitals.
A chemical bond is formed when the potential energy of the system gets lowered as atoms come near to each other. The energy touches a minimum at the equilibrium inter-nuclear distance.
Orbital overlap leads to an increase in electron density between the nuclei, helping to bring them closer. This concept explains the formation of chemical bonds.
Hybridization of atomic orbitals explains the characteristic shapes of polyatomic molecules. sp, sp2, sp3 hybridizations are used to explain molecular shapes.
The molecular orbital theory describes bonding in terms of the combination and arrangement of atomic orbitals to form molecular orbitals.
Bonding molecular orbitals increase electron density between the nuclei, while antibonding molecular orbitals have a region of zero electron density.
Molecules are stable if the number of electrons in bonding molecular orbitals is greater than that in antibonding molecular orbitals.
Hydrogen bonds are formed when a hydrogen atom is between two highly electronegative atoms. They can be intermolecular or intramolecular.
The VSEPR model is used to discuss the shape of molecules. It states that electron pairs around a central atom arrange themselves to minimize repulsions.
The octet rule states that atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve a full outer energy level with eight electrons.
Bond order is a measure of bond strength, expressed as the number of electrons involved in bonds between two atoms.
Bond length is the average distance between the nuclei of two bonded atoms.
Resonance occurs when a molecule or ion has multiple valid Lewis structures, leading to a hybrid structure.
Lewis symbols represent the valence electrons of an atom as dots, used to show electron transfer and bond formation.
Dipole moment measures the separation of charge in a molecule, influencing its shape and polarity.
Electronegativity is the ability of an atom to attract electrons in a covalent bond.
A polar covalent bond is a bond between two atoms with different electronegativities, leading to unequal electron sharing.
Hybridization is the mixing of atomic orbitals to form new hybrid orbitals suitable for bonding.
Sigma bonds are formed by end-to-end overlap, while pi bonds are formed by side-by-side overlap of atomic orbitals.
Molecular geometry refers to the three-dimensional arrangement of atoms in a molecule, influenced by hybridization and bond pairs.
Lone pairs are pairs of electrons not involved in bonding, while bond pairs are pairs of electrons involved in bonding.
Conditions required for linear combination of atomic orbitals to form molecular orbitals.
Be molecule does not exist according to molecular orbital theory.
Comparing stability of species and their magnetic properties.
Significance of plus and minus signs in representing orbitals.
Hybridization in PCl5 and bond length comparison.
Definition and strength of hydrogen bond compared to van der Waals forces.
Definition and calculation of bond order for various molecules.

A system is a part of the universe where changes are observed, and the surroundings are the remaining part of the universe. The boundary is the surface that separates the system from the surroundings.

There are three types of systems: open, closed, and isolated. Open systems can exchange matter and energy with the surroundings. Closed systems can exchange energy but not matter. Isolated systems cannot exchange matter or energy.
Internal energy (U) is the total energy of the system, including kinetic energy, potential energy, and other forms of energy.

Work (w) is the transfer of energy from the surroundings to the system through the boundary, and heat (q) is the transfer of energy from the surroundings to the system due to a temperature difference.
The first law of thermodynamics states that the change in internal energy (∆U) of a system is equal to the heat added to the system (q) minus the work done by the system (w): ∆U = q - w.
State functions are properties of a system that depend only on the current state of the system, not on the path taken to reach that state. Examples include internal energy (U) and enthalpy (H).
Enthalpy (H) is a state function defined as H = U + pV, where p is the pressure and V is the volume of the system.
∆U and ∆H are related by the equation ∆H = ∆U + ∆(pV). For reactions at constant pressure, ∆H = ∆U + p∆V.
Standard states are defined conditions for a system, such as 1 atm pressure and 298 K temperature, used as a reference point for measuring thermodynamic properties.
Hess's law states that the total enthalpy change (∆H) for a reaction is the same regardless of the number of steps involved in the reaction.
Extensive properties depend on the size of the system, such as volume and mass. Intensive properties do not depend on the size of the system, such as temperature and pressure.
Spontaneous processes occur on their own without external input, while non-spontaneous processes require external input to occur.
Entropy (S) is a thermodynamic state function that measures the disorder or randomness of a system.
Gibbs energy change (∆G) is a measure of the energy change of a system at constant temperature and pressure, and is related to the spontaneity of a process.
The system is the part of the universe where observations are made, and the surroundings constitute the remaining universe. The system and surroundings together form the universe.
There are three types of systems: open, closed, and isolated. These are classified based on the exchange of matter and energy between the system and surroundings.
An open system allows exchange of both matter and energy with the surroundings.
A closed system allows exchange of energy but not matter with the surroundings.
An isolated system does not allow exchange of either matter or energy with the surroundings.
key_terms: no exchange of matter or energy

concepts.description: Internal energy is the total energy of the system, which can be chemical, electrical, mechanical, or any other type of energy.
The state of a thermodynamic system is described by its measurable or macroscopic properties. These properties include pressure (p), volume (V), temperature (T), and amount (n).
Variables like p, V, T are called state variables or state functions because their values depend only on the state of the system and not on how it is reached.
An adiabatic process is a process in which there is no transfer of heat between the system and surroundings. This type of system is called adiabatic.

concepts.concept_title: Internal Energy as a State Function
concepts.description: The internal energy of a system is a state function, which means its value depends only on the state of the system and not on how it is reached.
In thermodynamics, work is done when a system changes its state, for example, by rotating a set of small paddles.
A state function is a property of a system that depends only on its current state, not on the path by which it reached that state. Examples include internal energy, volume, pressure, and temperature.

Internal energy is a state function that represents the total energy of a system. It includes both kinetic energy and potential energy. Change in internal energy (∆U) is equal to the adiabatic work done on the system.
Change in internal energy (∆U) is equal to the difference between the internal energy of the final state and the initial state. ∆U = U₂ - U₁ = w (adiabatic work done on the system).
Heat (q) is a form of energy that is transferred from one body to another due to a temperature difference. It is a measure of the energy transferred as a result of a temperature difference.

concepts.concept_title: Sign Convention for Heat and Work
concepts.description: According to IUPAC conventions, heat absorbed by the system (q) and work done on the system (w) are positive. Heat evolved by the system and work done by the system are negative.
Heat can be measured in terms of temperature difference. When no work is done at constant volume, ∆U = q.
The energy of an isolated system is constant. Energy can neither be created nor be destroyed. The change in internal energy of a system is given by ∆U = q + w.
The change in internal energy (∆U) of a system is given by ∆U = q + w, where q is the heat added to the system and w is the work done on the system.
A state function is a property of a system that depends only on the initial and final state of the system, not on the path followed. Internal energy (U) is a state function.
Work done (w) is a form of energy transfer that can be done on or by a system. In thermodynamics, work done is defined as the product of the external pressure and the change in volume.
The sign convention for heat and work done in thermodynamics. Heat added to the system is positive, heat removed is negative. Work done on the system is negative, work done by the system is positive.

concepts.concept_title: Work done by an ideal gas
concepts.description: Work done on an ideal gas in a cylinder when compressed by a constant external pressure. Work done is equal to the shaded area.
A reversible process proceeds infinitely slowly by a series of equilibrium states. A process is reversible if it can be reversed by an infinitesimal change.

concepts.concept_title: Work done in a reversible process
concepts.description: Work done on the gas in a reversible process is given by the integral of p dV. The external pressure is always infinitesimally greater than the pressure of the gas.
Work done on the gas is represented by the shaded area in a pV-plot. The plot can be used to calculate work done in a reversible or irreversible process.
Isothermal and free expansion of an ideal gas. These processes have specific characteristics and equations.

concepts.concept_title: Isothermal Irreversible Expansion of Ideal Gas
concepts.description: For isothermal irreversible expansion of an ideal gas into vacuum, work done (w) is 0 and ∆U = 0. The equation for this process is q = – w = p (V – V ).

concepts.concept_title: Isothermal Reversible Expansion of Ideal Gas
concepts.description: For isothermal reversible expansion of an ideal gas, work done (w) is given by w = – ∫p dV = – nRT ln (V / V ).
Free expansion is the expansion of a gas in vacuum (p = 0). No work is done during free expansion of an ideal gas, whether the process is reversible or irreversible.
For adiabatic change, q = 0 and ∆U = w.

concepts.concept_title: Enthalpy (H) and Heat at Constant Pressure
concepts.description: Enthalpy (H) is a state function defined as H = U + pV. At constant pressure, ∆H = q, where q is the heat absorbed by the system.
At constant volume, ∆U = q. At constant pressure, ∆H = ∆U + ∆(pV) = ∆U + p∆V.

concepts.concept_title: Enthalpy (H) and its relation to internal energy (U)
concepts.description: Enthalpy (H) is a thermodynamic function defined as H = U + pV. The change in enthalpy (∆H) is related to the change in internal energy (∆U) by the equation ∆H = ∆U + ∆pV or ∆H = ∆U + p∆V at constant pressure.
The difference between ∆H and ∆U is given by ∆H - ∆U = ∆nRT or p∆V, where ∆n is the change in the number of moles of gases. This difference is significant for reactions involving gases.

concepts.concept_title: Calculation of ∆H from ∆U and vice versa
concepts.description: The equation ∆H = ∆U + ∆nRT is useful for calculating ∆H from ∆U and vice versa. This equation helps in determining the enthalpy change for reactions involving gases.

At constant pressure, the heat absorbed by the system is equal to the change in enthalpy (∆H). This is a measure of the total energy change in a system, including the energy associated with the pressure and volume of a system.
Properties whose value depends on the quantity or size of matter present in the system. Examples: mass, volume, internal energy, enthalpy, heat capacity.
Properties which do not depend on the quantity or size of matter present. Examples: temperature, density, pressure.
The value of an extensive property for 1 mol of a substance. Examples: molar volume, molar heat capacity.
The coefficient of heat transferred to a system, denoted by C. It is directly proportional to the amount of substance.
The quantity of heat required to raise the temperature of one unit mass of a substance by one degree celsius (or one kelvin).

concepts.concept_title: Measurement of Heat at Constant Volume
concepts.description: Heat absorbed at constant volume is measured in a bomb calorimeter.
The relationship between heat capacities at constant pressure and constant volume.
An ideal gas is a hypothetical gas that obeys the ideal gas equation.
Heat capacity is the amount of heat required to change the temperature of a substance by 1 degree.
Cp - Cv = R, where R is the gas constant.
Calorimetry is an experimental technique used to measure energy changes associated with chemical or physical processes.
∆U can be measured at constant volume using a bomb calorimeter.
∆H can be measured at constant pressure using a calorimeter.
Enthalpy of reaction, ∆H, is the heat absorbed or evolved at constant pressure.
Enthalpy change (∆H) is the energy change associated with a chemical reaction at constant pressure. It is defined as ∆H = (sum of enthalpies of products) – (sum of enthalpies of reactants).

The standard enthalpy of reaction is the enthalpy change for a reaction when all participating substances are in their standard states. Standard conditions are denoted by adding the superscript  to the symbol ∆H.
Phase transformations involve energy changes. The enthalpy change that accompanies melting of one mole of a solid substance in its standard state is called the standard enthalpy of fusion.
For a reaction, ∆H = ∆U + ∆nRT, but when ∆n = 0, ∆H = ∆U. This relationship can be used to calculate ∆H from ∆U.

The standard enthalpy of fusion is the change in enthalpy when one mole of a solid substance melts at a constant temperature and under standard pressure (1bar). It is denoted by ∆H°fus. Melting of a solid is endothermic, so all enthalpies of fusion are positive.

The standard enthalpy of vaporization is the change in enthalpy when one mole of a liquid substance vaporizes at a constant temperature and under standard pressure (1bar). It is denoted by ∆H°vap. Evaporation of a liquid is endothermic, so all enthalpies of vaporization are positive.
The standard enthalpy of sublimation is the change in enthalpy when one mole of a solid substance sublimes at a constant temperature and under standard pressure (1bar). It is denoted by ∆H°sub.

The relationship between ∆H and ∆U is given by ∆U = ∆H - ∆nRT, where ∆n is the change in the number of moles of gas. This equation is used to calculate the internal energy change (∆U) from the enthalpy change (∆H).

The magnitude of the enthalpy change during phase transformations depends on the strength of the intermolecular interactions in the substance undergoing the phase transformation. Stronger intermolecular interactions require more energy to overcome, resulting in larger enthalpy changes.
The standard enthalpy change for the formation of one mole of a compound from its elements in their most stable states of aggregation. It is denoted by ∆H˚f.
The most stable state of aggregation of an element at 25°C and 1 bar pressure. For example, the reference state of dihydrogen is H2 gas.

concepts.concept_title: Calculation of Enthalpy Change using Standard Enthalpy of Formation
concepts.description: The enthalpy change for a reaction can be calculated using the equation: ∆H˚r = ∑a∆H˚f(products) – ∑b∆H˚f(reactants).

concepts.concept_title: Internal Energy Change (∆U) and Enthalpy Change (∆H) Relationship
concepts.description: For a process with negligible change in volume, ∆H = ∆U.
The standard enthalpy change for the vaporization of one mole of a substance.
A balanced chemical equation with its enthalpy change is called a thermochemical equation. It specifies the physical state of substances and coefficients refer to moles.
The enthalpy change for formation of one mole of a substance from its elements in their standard states is called standard enthalpy of formation.
Coefficients refer to moles, not molecules. ∆H values refer to the number of moles specified in the equation.

concepts.concept_title: Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation
concepts.description: The enthalpy change for a reaction is the same whether it occurs in one step or in a series of steps.

concepts.concept_title: Calculation of Enthalpy Change using Hess's Law
concepts.description: Enthalpy change for a reaction can be calculated using standard enthalpies of formation and Hess's Law.
Enthalpy change for a reaction is defined as the enthalpy change when the reactants are converted to products. It is represented by ∆H and its unit is kJ mol–1.
Enthalpy is an extensive quantity, meaning its value depends on the amount of substance. Intensive properties are independent of the amount of substance.
When a chemical equation is reversed, the sign of ∆H is changed. For example, if ∆H = –x kJ mol–1 for a reaction, then ∆H = +x kJ mol–1 for the reversed reaction.
Hess's Law states that the total enthalpy change for a reaction is the same regardless of the number of steps involved. It can be represented as ∆H = ∆H1 + ∆H2 + ∆H3 ...
The standard enthalpy of formation is the enthalpy change when one mole of a compound is formed from its constituent elements in their standard states.
The enthalpy change per mole of a substance when it undergoes combustion and all reactants and products are in their standard states at a specified temperature.
The enthalpy change when one mole of a compound is formed from its constituent elements in their standard states.
The enthalpy change when one mole of a substance is converted into its constituent atoms in their standard states.
The enthalpy change when one mole of covalent bonds of a gaseous covalent compound is broken.
The average enthalpy change associated with a particular type of bond in different compounds.
A general term that encompasses bond dissociation enthalpy and mean bond enthalpy.
The enthalpy change on breaking one mole of bonds completely to obtain atoms in the gas phase. It is the enthalpy change for the formation of atoms from a molecule in the gas phase.
The enthalpy change required to break a particular bond in a molecule. It is the same as the enthalpy of atomization for diatomic molecules.
The average enthalpy change required to break a particular type of bond in different molecules. It is used for polyatomic molecules where different bonds of the same type have similar energies.
The enthalpy change for a reaction under standard conditions. It can be calculated using bond enthalpies of reactants and products.
A law stating that the total enthalpy change in a reaction is the same regardless of the number of steps involved.
Bond dissociation enthalpy is the energy required to break a particular bond in a molecule. It is an important concept in thermodynamics and is used to calculate the enthalpy changes in reactions.
Mean bond enthalpy is the average energy required to break a particular type of bond in different molecules. It is used to estimate the enthalpy changes in reactions.

Lattice enthalpy is the enthalpy change that occurs when one mole of an ionic compound dissociates into its ions in the gaseous state. It is an important concept in thermodynamics and is used to calculate the enthalpy changes in reactions.
Born-Haber cycle is a thermodynamic cycle used to calculate the lattice enthalpy of an ionic compound. It involves a series of steps, including sublimation, ionization, and electron gain.

Ionization enthalpy is the energy required to remove an electron from an atom or ion in the gaseous state. It is an important concept in thermodynamics and is used to calculate the enthalpy changes in reactions.

Electron gain enthalpy is the energy change that occurs when an electron is added to an atom or ion in the gaseous state. It is an important concept in thermodynamics and is used to calculate the enthalpy changes in reactions.

Lattice enthalpy is the enthalpy change associated with the formation of a crystal lattice from its constituent ions in the gaseous state. It is a measure of the strength of the ionic bonds in the crystal lattice.

The Born-Haber cycle is a series of enthalpy changes that occur when an ionic compound is formed from its constituent elements. It is a useful tool for calculating the lattice enthalpy of an ionic compound.

The enthalpy of solution is the enthalpy change associated with the dissolution of a substance in a specified amount of solvent. It is a measure of the energy change that occurs when a substance dissolves in a solvent.

The enthalpy of hydration is the enthalpy change associated with the solvation of ions or molecules by water. It is a measure of the energy change that occurs when ions or molecules interact with water.
The enthalpy of dilution is the enthalpy change associated with the addition of a specified amount of solute to a specified amount of solvent at a constant temperature and pressure.
The enthalpy of solution is the change in enthalpy when a substance is dissolved in a solvent. It depends on the amount of solvent used and the concentration of the solution.
The enthalpy of dilution is the change in enthalpy when a solution is diluted by adding more solvent. It depends on the original concentration of the solution and the amount of solvent added.
A spontaneous process is one that can proceed on its own without external assistance. It does not imply that the process occurs rapidly, but rather that it has the potential to occur.
A decrease in enthalpy is not the only criterion for spontaneity. However, for exothermic reactions, a decrease in energy can contribute to spontaneity.
A spontaneous process is irreversible and can only be reversed by an external agency.
A spontaneous reaction is one that occurs on its own without external influence. It may not necessarily occur immediately.
Decrease in enthalpy may contribute to spontaneity, but it is not the only factor. Some endothermic reactions are spontaneous.
Entropy is a measure of disorder or chaos. In an isolated system, there is a tendency for energy to become more disordered or chaotic, which can drive spontaneity.
A criterion for spontaneous change is the tendency of a system to become more disordered or chaotic.
Entropy is a measure of the degree of randomness or disorder in a system. It is a state function and is a thermodynamic property.
The change in entropy (∆S) is given by ∆S = q / T for a reversible process. ∆S is independent of path and is a state property.
The total entropy change (∆S_total) for a spontaneous process is given by ∆S_total = ∆S_system + ∆S_surroundings > 0.
The entropy of a substance depends on its structure. The crystalline solid state has the lowest entropy, while the gaseous state has the highest entropy.
The entropy of a system increases with temperature. Heat added to a system increases molecular motions, causing increased randomness.
Entropy change can be predicted in different processes, such as crystallization, change in temperature, and chemical reactions.
Entropy is a measure of randomness or disorder in a system. Higher temperature leads to greater randomness and thus higher entropy.
Entropy change is inversely proportional to temperature. Heat added at lower temperature causes greater randomness than at higher temperature.
Gibbs energy change determines the spontaneity of a reaction. A reaction is spontaneous if Gibbs energy change is negative.
Gibbs energy change = enthalpy change - temperature × entropy change. This equation is used to determine the spontaneity of a reaction.

concepts.concept_title: Entropy Change and Number of Particles
concepts.description: Increase in number of particles leads to more disordered state and higher entropy.
At 0 K, constituent particles are static and entropy is minimum.

Gibbs free energy (∆G) is a measure of the net energy available to do useful work in a system at constant pressure and temperature. It is defined as ∆G = ∆H - T∆S, where ∆H is the enthalpy change and ∆S is the entropy change.
A process is spontaneous if ∆G is negative (< 0). ∆G gives a criteria for spontaneity at constant pressure and temperature.
Entropy (∆S) is a measure of disorder or randomness in a system. Increase in entropy leads to increase in disorder.
The second law of thermodynamics states that the total entropy of an isolated system always increases over time. It explains why spontaneous exothermic reactions are common.
The third law of thermodynamics states that the entropy of a pure crystalline substance approaches zero as the temperature approaches absolute zero.
Absolute entropy is a measure of the total entropy of a system. Molecules have translational, rotational, and vibrational motions that contribute to entropy.

The third law of thermodynamics states that the entropy of a pure crystalline solid at absolute zero is zero. This law permits the calculation of absolute values of entropy of pure substances from thermal data alone.
A process is spontaneous if ∆G is negative (< 0). If ∆G is positive (> 0), the process is non-spontaneous.
The criterion for equilibrium is ∆G = 0. ∆G is related to the equilibrium constant of the reaction as ∆G  = – RT ln K.
Standard entropies can be used to calculate standard entropy changes by a Hess’s law type of calculation.
∆G  = – 2.303 RT log K. For strongly endothermic reactions, the value of K will be much smaller than 1.

The spontaneity of a reaction is affected by temperature, depending on the signs of ∆H° and ∆S°. A reaction can be spontaneous at low temperatures but non-spontaneous at high temperatures, or vice versa.

concepts.concept_title: Gibbs Free Energy Change (∆G°) and Equilibrium Constant (K)
concepts.description: ∆G° is related to the equilibrium constant K by the equation ∆G° = –2.303 RT log K. This relationship allows us to calculate K at any temperature if ∆G° is known.
∆G° can be calculated using the equation ∆G° = ∆H° – T∆S°. This allows us to estimate ∆G° from measurements of ∆H° and ∆S°.
A reaction is spontaneous if ∆G° is negative, and non-spontaneous if ∆G° is positive. The value of ∆G° determines the feasibility of a reaction.
Thermodynamic parameters such as ∆G°, ∆H°, and ∆S° are related to the equilibrium constant K and can be used to predict the behavior of a system at equilibrium.
State functions are quantities that depend only on the state of a system, not on the path by which the system is obtained. Examples include internal energy (U) and enthalpy (H).

concepts.concept_title: Sign Conventions for q and w
concepts.description: The sign conventions for heat (q) and work (w) are defined such that positive values are given when these quantities are added to the system.
Heat capacity (C) is the amount of heat required to change the temperature of a substance by a given amount. It is related to the heat absorbed or evolved by the equation q = C∆T.
Work can be measured by the equation w = –p ∆V, which applies to the expansion of gases.
Enthalpy change (∆H) is a state function that can be calculated from the heat changes at constant pressure. It is related to internal energy change (∆U) by the equation ∆H = ∆U + ∆nRT.
Hess's law states that the total enthalpy change in a reaction is the same regardless of the number of steps involved. It can be used to calculate enthalpy changes for chemical reactions.
Entropy (S) is a state function that measures the disorder or randomness of a system. For a spontaneous change, the total entropy change is positive.
Gibbs energy (G) is a state function that is related to entropy and enthalpy changes by the equation ∆G = ∆H – T ∆S. For a spontaneous change, ∆G < 0.
Standard Gibbs energy change (∆G°) is related to the equilibrium constant (K) by the equation ∆G° = – RT ln K.
State functions are independent of the path taken to reach a particular state. This concept is crucial in understanding thermodynamic properties.
Adiabatic conditions refer to a process where no heat is transferred between the system and its surroundings. This is represented by q = 0.

The standard enthalpy of formation is the enthalpy change when one mole of a compound is formed from its constituent elements in their standard states. The enthalpies of all elements in their standard states are zero.
The relationship between ∆U and ∆H is given by ∆H = ∆U + ∆nRT, where ∆n is the change in the number of moles of gas. This relationship is crucial in converting between ∆U and ∆H.
The enthalpy of formation of a compound can be calculated using the enthalpies of combustion of the reactants and products.
A reaction is spontaneous if ∆G is negative. Entropy change (∆S) is an important factor in determining spontaneity. A positive entropy change favors spontaneity.
The first law of thermodynamics states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another. This is represented by ∆U = q + w.
Enthalpy change can be calculated using the formula ∆H = ∆U + ∆nRT. This concept is used to calculate enthalpy changes in various reactions.
Heat capacity is the amount of heat required to change the temperature of a substance by a certain amount. Calorimetry is the measurement of heat changes in a reaction.
Bond enthalpy is the energy required to break a particular bond in a molecule. It is an important concept in understanding the energetics of chemical reactions.
A reaction is spontaneous if ∆G is negative. ∆G = ∆H - T∆S. A reaction becomes spontaneous when ∆H and ∆S are constant over a temperature range.
∆G = ∆H - T∆S. This equation relates the change in Gibbs free energy to the change in enthalpy and entropy.
The signs of ∆H and ∆S can be used to predict the spontaneity of a reaction. ∆H and ∆S can be positive or negative.
∆G = ∆U - T∆S. This equation relates the change in Gibbs free energy to the change in internal energy and entropy.
∆G = -RT ln(K). This equation relates the change in Gibbs free energy to the equilibrium constant.
The thermodynamic stability of a compound can be determined from the ∆H values of its formation reactions.
The entropy change in surroundings can be calculated from the ∆H value of a reaction.
Equilibrium is a dynamic state where rates of forward and reverse reactions are equal. It's not a static state, but rather a balance between two opposing processes.
The law of equilibrium states that at equilibrium, the rates of forward and reverse reactions are equal.
Equilibrium constants (K) are expressions that describe the ratio of product concentrations to reactant concentrations at equilibrium.
Equilibrium is characterized by no net change in concentrations of reactants and products. It's a state of balance between two opposing processes.
Various factors can affect the equilibrium state of a reaction, such as concentration, temperature, and pressure.

concepts.concept_title: Arrhenius Concept of Acids and Bases
concepts.description: The Arrhenius concept defines acids as substances that increase H+ ion concentration and bases as substances that increase OH- ion concentration.

concepts.concept_title: Bronsted-Lowry Concept of Acids and Bases
concepts.description: The Bronsted-Lowry concept defines acids as proton donors and bases as proton acceptors.

concepts.concept_title: Lewis Concept of Acids and Bases
concepts.description: The Lewis concept defines acids as electron pair acceptors and bases as electron pair donors.
Ionization constants (Ka, Kb) describe the strength of acids and bases in terms of their ionization.
The pH scale is used to represent hydrogen ion concentration in a solution.
Water can act as both an acid and a base, and its ionization is described by the ionic product (Kw).
Buffer solutions are used to resist changes in pH when acids or bases are added.
The solubility product constant (Ksp) describes the solubility of a salt in a solution.
A state of equilibrium where the rates of forward and reverse processes are equal, and there is no net change in the concentrations of species.
At equilibrium, there is no change in the concentrations of various species, and the system has characteristic features.

Reactions can be classified into three types based on the extent of reaction: (i) reactions that proceed nearly to completion, (ii) reactions with small amounts of products, and (iii) reactions with comparable concentrations of reactants and products.
The temperature at which the solid and liquid phases of a substance are in equilibrium at atmospheric pressure.
A state of equilibrium between a liquid and its vapour, where the rates of evaporation and condensation are equal.
The importance of optimising operational conditions to favour equilibrium in the direction of the desired product.
Equilibrium between a solid and a liquid at a given temperature and pressure. The system shows no net change in composition.
The pressure exerted by a vapour in equilibrium with its liquid phase at a given temperature. Increases with temperature.
At equilibrium, the rate of evaporation equals the rate of condensation. No net change in composition.
The temperature at which a liquid and its vapour are in equilibrium at 1.013 bar pressure.
Boiling point depends on atmospheric pressure. Decreases with decrease in pressure.
Equilibrium between a solid and its vapour phase. The solid sublimes to vapour.
A liquid with higher vapour pressure is more volatile and has a lower boiling point.
A solution that cannot dissolve more solute at a given temperature. Dynamic equilibrium exists between solute molecules in solid state and solution.
The solubility of a solid in a liquid is constant at a given temperature. It depends on the nature of the solid and liquid.
A state where the rates of forward and reverse processes are equal. Illustrated by sugar (solution) ⇌ sugar (solid).

concepts.concept_title: Equilibrium Involving Dissolution of Gases in Liquids
concepts.description: The concentration of a gas in a liquid decreases with increase in temperature. Henry's law is related to this concept.
The vapour pressure of a liquid is constant at a given temperature. It is a characteristic property of the liquid.
Physical equilibrium is a state where the rates of forward and reverse physical processes are equal, and there is no net change in the system.

Physical equilibria have characteristics such as: (i) occur in a closed system, (ii) dynamic but stable condition, (iii) measurable properties remain constant, and (iv) one parameter has a constant value at a given temperature.
Henry's Law states that the mass of a gas dissolved in a solvent at a given temperature is proportional to the pressure of the gas.
Chemical reactions can attain a state of equilibrium where the rates of forward and reverse reactions are equal, and there is no net change in the concentrations of reactants and products.
Chemical equilibrium is dynamic, meaning that the reactants and products are constantly being converted into each other, but there is no net change in their concentrations.
A state where the concentrations of reactants and products remain constant. This is a dynamic equilibrium, where forward and reverse reactions occur at the same rate.
The equilibrium state is dynamic, meaning that reactants and products are constantly being converted into each other, but the concentrations remain constant.
A reaction that can proceed in both forward and reverse directions, leading to a state of equilibrium.
Not explicitly mentioned but implied: the ratio of concentrations of products to reactants at equilibrium remains constant.
The concentrations of reactants and products remain constant, and the reaction is dynamic in nature.

The process of transfer of reactants to products continues even after the constancy of level, indicating a dynamic nature of the process. This means that at equilibrium, the rates of forward and reverse reactions are equal.
A mixture of reactants and products in the equilibrium state is called an equilibrium mixture. The composition of this mixture is constant at equilibrium.
The law of chemical equilibrium states that at equilibrium, the rates of forward and reverse reactions are equal, and there is no net change in composition.
The equilibrium constant is a value that describes the ratio of the concentrations of products to reactants at equilibrium.

The equilibrium constant expression is given by Kc = [C][D] / [A][B]. The equilibrium constant Kc is a value that describes the ratio of product concentrations to reactant concentrations at equilibrium.

The law of mass action states that the concentrations of reactants and products in an equilibrium mixture are related by the equilibrium constant expression. This law was proposed by Cato Maximillian Guldberg and Peter Waage.
Equilibrium concentrations are the concentrations of reactants and products at equilibrium. These concentrations can be used to calculate the equilibrium constant Kc.
A reversible reaction is a reaction that can proceed in both forward and reverse directions. Reversible reactions can attain equilibrium.

Equilibrium can be attained from both sides of a reaction, i.e., from reactants or products. The composition of an equilibrium mixture can be altered by changing the initial concentrations of reactants or products.
The equilibrium constant expression is given by K = [C]^c[D]^d / [A]^a[B]^b, where [A], [B], [C], and [D] are the equilibrium concentrations of the reactants and products.

The law states that at a given temperature, the product of concentrations of the reaction products raised to the respective stoichiometric coefficient in the balanced chemical equation divided by the product of the concentrations of the reactants raised to their individual stoichiometric coefficients has a constant value.
The equilibrium constant for the reverse reaction is the inverse of the equilibrium constant for the reaction in the forward direction, i.e., K' = 1/K.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Stoichiometric Coefficients on Equilibrium Constant
concepts.description: If the stoichiometric coefficients in a chemical equation are multiplied by a factor, the expression for the equilibrium constant must also reflect that change.

concepts.concept_title: Equilibrium Constant in Terms of Concentrations
concepts.description: The equilibrium constant is expressed in terms of concentrations of mol L^-1, and the symbol for phases (s, l, g) are generally ignored.

The equilibrium constant expression for a reaction is given by K = [C]^c[D]^d / [A]^a[B]^b. The equilibrium constant is a value that describes the ratio of the concentrations of the products to the concentrations of the reactants at equilibrium.
If a reaction is multiplied by a factor, its equilibrium constant is raised to the power of that factor. For example, if a reaction is multiplied by 2, its equilibrium constant is squared.
In a homogeneous system, all the reactants and products are in the same phase. Examples include gaseous reactions and reactions in solution.
For reactions involving gases, it is often more convenient to express the equilibrium constant in terms of partial pressures rather than concentrations.

The equilibrium constant in terms of partial pressures (Kp) is related to the equilibrium constant in terms of concentrations (Kc) by the equation Kp = Kc(RT)^(Δn), where Δn is the difference in the number of moles of gas between the products and reactants.
The law of mass action states that the rate of a chemical reaction is proportional to the product of the concentrations of the reactants.
The equilibrium constant Kc is defined as the ratio of the product of the concentrations of the products to the product of the concentrations of the reactants.
The equilibrium constant Kp is defined as the ratio of the product of the partial pressures of the products to the product of the partial pressures of the reactants.
key_terms: partial pressures of products and reactants

concepts.description: Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn, where Δn is the difference in the number of moles of gases between products and reactants.
Equilibrium in a system having more than one phase is called heterogeneous equilibrium.
The concentrations of the reactants and products at equilibrium can be calculated using the equilibrium constant expression.

Heterogeneous equilibria involve different phases, such as solid and liquid or solid and gas. The molar concentration of a pure solid or liquid is constant and is not included in the equilibrium expression.

concepts.concept_title: Equilibrium Constant Expression for Heterogeneous Equilibria
concepts.description: For heterogeneous equilibria, the concentrations of pure solids or liquids are constant and are not included in the equilibrium expression. The equilibrium constant expression is written in terms of the concentrations of species in other phases.

The units of the equilibrium constant depend on the units of concentration or pressure used. If the units of concentration or pressure in the numerator and denominator are the same, the equilibrium constant is unitless.
Examples of heterogeneous equilibria include the thermal dissociation of calcium carbonate and the equilibrium between nickel, carbon monoxide, and nickel carbonyl.
The equilibrium constant expression is a mathematical representation of the equilibrium state of a reaction. It is expressed as a ratio of the concentrations of products to reactants.
The units of the equilibrium constant depend on the standard state chosen for the reactants and products. If the standard state is 1 bar for gases, Kc and Kp are dimensionless.
Kc and Kp are related but have different numerical values due to different standard states. The equilibrium constant Kp is related to Kc by the equation Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn.

concepts.concept_title: Predicting the Extent of a Reaction
concepts.description: The numerical value of the equilibrium constant indicates the extent of the reaction. A high value of K suggests a high concentration of products.
The equilibrium constant can be used to predict the direction of a reaction. If Q < K, the reaction proceeds in the forward direction.
The equilibrium constant can be used to calculate the equilibrium concentrations of reactants and products.
If K > 10^3, products predominate over reactants. The magnitude of K is directly proportional to the concentrations of products and inversely proportional to the concentrations of reactants.

The equilibrium constant is a value that describes the ratio of concentrations of products to reactants at equilibrium. It is temperature-dependent and has a unique value for a particular reaction at a given temperature.

If Kc is very large, the reaction proceeds nearly to completion. If Kc is very small, the reaction proceeds rarely. If Kc is in the range of 10–3 to 103, appreciable concentrations of both reactants and products are present.
The equilibrium constant for the reverse reaction is equal to the inverse of the equilibrium constant for the forward reaction.
The reaction quotient, Qc, is used to predict the direction of reaction by comparing the values of Qc and Kc. If Qc = Kc, the reaction mixture is already at equilibrium.
The equilibrium constant helps in predicting the direction in which a given reaction will proceed at any stage. This is done by comparing the values of Qc and Kc.
The reaction quotient, Q, is defined as the ratio of the concentrations of products to reactants, raised to their stoichiometric coefficients, at any point in the reaction.
If Q < K, the reaction proceeds in the forward direction (towards products). If Q > K, the reaction proceeds in the reverse direction (towards reactants). If Q = K, no net reaction occurs.

To calculate equilibrium concentrations, follow three steps: (1) write the balanced equation, (2) make a table of initial concentrations, changes in concentrations, and equilibrium concentrations, and (3) substitute equilibrium concentrations into the equilibrium equation and solve for x.
Kp = Kc(RT)∆n, where ∆n is the difference in the number of moles of gas between products and reactants.
The equilibrium constant expression is a mathematical expression that describes the ratio of the concentrations of products to reactants at equilibrium.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship between Kc and Gibbs Energy
concepts.description: The equilibrium constant Kc is related to the standard Gibbs energy change (∆G°) of a reaction.
A reaction is spontaneous if ∆G° < 0, and non-spontaneous if ∆G° > 0.
The reaction quotient (Q) is used to determine the direction of a reaction, and the equilibrium constant (Kc) is used to describe the equilibrium state.
∆G° can be calculated from the equilibrium constant Kc using the equation ∆G° = -RT ln Kc.
Kc can be calculated from ∆G° using the equation Kc = e^(-∆G°/RT).

The equilibrium constant K is a value that describes the ratio of the concentrations of the products to the concentrations of the reactants at equilibrium. It is independent of initial concentrations.

Le Chatelier's principle states that when a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change in concentration, temperature, or pressure, the system will adjust to a new equilibrium that counteracts the effect of the change.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Concentration Change on Equilibrium
concepts.description: When the concentration of a reactant or product is changed, the equilibrium shifts in a direction that tends to restore the original equilibrium concentrations.
At equilibrium, ∆G = 0 and Q = K. The equation ∆G = ∆G° + RT ln Q can be used to relate ∆G to equilibrium constant K.
∆G° = –RT ln K. This equation relates the standard Gibbs free energy change to the equilibrium constant.
When a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change in concentration, temperature, or pressure, the equilibrium shifts in a direction that tends to counteract the effect of the change.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Concentration Change on Equilibrium
concepts.description: An increase in concentration of a reactant causes the equilibrium to shift in the forward direction, while a decrease causes it to shift in the reverse direction. The opposite effect is observed for products.

The reaction quotient, Q, is the ratio of the concentrations of products to reactants at any given time. When Q < Kc, the reaction shifts in the forward direction, and when Q > Kc, it shifts in the reverse direction.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Pressure Change on Equilibrium
concepts.description: A change in pressure can affect the equilibrium of a reaction involving gases. If the total number of moles of gaseous reactants and products are different, a change in pressure will cause the equilibrium to shift.
In a heterogeneous equilibrium, the effect of pressure changes on solids and liquids can be ignored because their volumes and concentrations are nearly independent of pressure.

Le Chatelier's principle states that when a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change in concentration, temperature, or pressure, the equilibrium shifts in a direction that tends to counteract the effect of the change.

The equilibrium shifts in the direction that tends to reduce the concentration of the added reactant or product. Adding a reactant or product causes the equilibrium to shift in the opposite direction.

The equilibrium shifts in the direction that tends to reduce the pressure. If the number of moles of gas is more on the product side, an increase in pressure shifts the equilibrium to the reactant side.

For an exothermic reaction, an increase in temperature shifts the equilibrium to the reactant side, and for an endothermic reaction, an increase in temperature shifts the equilibrium to the product side.
Adding an inert gas at constant volume does not change the partial pressures or molar concentrations, so the equilibrium remains undisturbed.
The reaction quotient (Qc) is the ratio of the concentrations of products to reactants at any point in the reaction. If Qc < Kc, the reaction proceeds in the forward direction.

The temperature dependence of the equilibrium constant depends on the sign of ∆H for the reaction. For exothermic reactions, K decreases with increasing temperature, while for endothermic reactions, K increases with increasing temperature.

Whenever an equilibrium is disturbed by a change in concentration, pressure, or volume, the composition of the equilibrium mixture changes because the reaction quotient, Q, no longer equals the equilibrium constant, K.
A catalyst increases the rate of the chemical reaction by making available a new low-energy pathway for the conversion of reactants to products. It does not affect the equilibrium constant.
The equilibrium constant for an exothermic reaction decreases as the temperature increases, while the equilibrium constant for an endothermic reaction increases as the temperature increases.
A catalyst is used to increase the rate of a reaction. If a reaction has an exceedingly small K, a catalyst would be of little help.

Substances that conduct electricity in their aqueous solutions are called electrolytes, while those that do not are called non-electrolytes. Electrolytes are further classified into strong and weak electrolytes.

Strong electrolytes are ionized almost completely in aqueous solution, while weak electrolytes are only partially dissociated. Examples include sodium chloride (strong electrolyte) and acetic acid (weak electrolyte).
In weak electrolytes, equilibrium is established between ions and the unionized molecules. This type of equilibrium involving ions in aqueous solution is called ionic equilibrium.
A catalyst lowers the activation energy for the forward and reverse reactions by exactly the same amount and does not affect the equilibrium composition of a reaction mixture.

The equilibrium constant decreases with an increase in temperature. At low temperatures, the rate decreases, and it takes a long time to reach equilibrium, whereas high temperatures give satisfactory rates but poor yields.
Electrolytes are charged species that can conduct electricity. They can be classified into strong and weak electrolytes based on their ability to ionize in a solution.

concepts.concept_title: Dielectric constant and its effect on electrostatic interactions
concepts.description: The dielectric constant of a medium is a measure of its ability to reduce electrostatic interactions between charges. A higher dielectric constant reduces electrostatic interactions.

concepts.concept_title: Hydration of ions in a solution
concepts.description: When ions are dissolved in water, they get hydrated by polar water molecules. This hydration helps to stabilize the ions in the solution.
Acids taste sour, turn blue litmus paper into red, and liberate dihydrogen on reacting with some metals.
Bases taste bitter, turn red litmus paper blue, and feel soapy.
When acids and bases are mixed in the right proportion, they react with each other to give salts.

concepts.concept_title: Arrhenius Concept of Acids and Bases
concepts.description: According to Arrhenius theory, acids are substances that dissociate in water to give hydrogen ions H+(aq) and bases are substances that produce hydroxyl ions OH–(aq).

Ionization corresponds to a process in which a neutral molecule splits into charged ions in the solution. Dissociation refers to the process of separation of ions in water already existing as such in the solid state of the solute.
The hydronium ion, H3O+, is formed when a hydrogen ion bonds to the oxygen atom of a solvent water molecule. The hydroxyl ion, OH–, exists in hydrated form in aqueous solutions.

concepts.concept_title: Brönsted-Lowry Concept of Acids and Bases
concepts.description: According to Brönsted-Lowry theory, acid is a substance that is capable of donating a hydrogen ion H+ and bases are substances capable of accepting a hydrogen ion, H+.
The extent to which ionization occurs depends upon the strength of the bond and the extent of solvation of ions produced.

concepts.concept_title: Arrhenius Concept of Acid and Base
concepts.description: The Arrhenius concept states that acids are substances that increase the concentration of hydrogen ions (H+) in aqueous solutions, while bases increase the concentration of hydroxide ions (OH-).
The Arrhenius concept has limitations, as it only applies to aqueous solutions and does not account for the basicity of substances like ammonia, which do not possess a hydroxyl group.

concepts.concept_title: Lowry-Brönsted Concept of Acid and Base
concepts.description: The Lowry-Brönsted concept defines acids as proton donors and bases as proton acceptors. This concept is more general and applies to non-aqueous solutions as well.
A conjugate acid-base pair is a pair of species that differ only by one proton. The acid loses a proton to form its conjugate base, and the base gains a proton to form its conjugate acid.

concepts.concept_title: Strength of Acid and Its Conjugate Base
concepts.description: If a Brönsted acid is strong, its conjugate base is weak, and vice versa.

concepts.concept_title: Identification of Conjugate Acids and Bases
concepts.description: To identify the conjugate acid or base of a given species, add or remove a proton, respectively.
A conjugate acid-base pair consists of a species that donates a proton (acid) and the species that accepts the proton (base). For example, HCl and Cl- are conjugate acid-base pairs.
Water can act as both an acid and a base. It can accept a proton to form H3O+ (acidic nature) or donate a proton to form OH- (basic nature).

A Lewis acid is a species that accepts an electron pair, while a Lewis base is a species that donates an electron pair. This concept is broader than the Bronsted-Lowry concept and includes species without protons.

Acids and bases can be classified as strong or weak based on their ability to dissociate in water. Strong acids and bases are almost completely dissociated, while weak acids and bases are only partially dissociated.

concepts.concept_title: Bronsted-Lowry Concept of Acids and Bases
concepts.description: The Bronsted-Lowry concept defines an acid as a proton donor and a base as a proton acceptor. This concept is useful for understanding acid-base reactions in aqueous solutions.

concepts.concept_title: Electron Deficient Species as Lewis Acids
concepts.description: Electron deficient species, such as BF3 and AlCl3, can act as Lewis acids by accepting electron pairs.

concepts.concept_title: Species that Can Donate Electron Pairs as Lewis Bases
concepts.description: Species that can donate electron pairs, such as H2O, NH3, and OH-, can act as Lewis bases.

concepts.concept_title: Arrhenius Concept of Acids and Bases
concepts.description: The Arrhenius concept defines acids as substances that increase the concentration of hydrogen ions (H+) in aqueous solutions, and bases as substances that increase the concentration of hydroxide ions (OH-) in aqueous solutions.
A Lewis acid is a substance that can accept a pair of electrons, while a Lewis base is a substance that can donate a pair of electrons. Examples include BCl3 as a Lewis acid and :OH- as a Lewis base.
A proton (H+ ion) can act as a Lewis acid by accepting an electron pair. A species like :OH- can act as a Lewis base by donating an electron pair to a proton.

The strength of an acid or base is determined by its ability to donate or accept a proton. A stronger acid is one that donates a proton more easily, while a stronger base is one that accepts a proton more easily.

When an acid donates a proton, it forms a conjugate base. Similarly, when a base accepts a proton, it forms a conjugate acid. The conjugate base of a strong acid is weak, and the conjugate acid of a strong base is weak.

concepts.concept_title: Ionization of Acids and Bases in Water
concepts.description: The ionization of acids and bases in water leads to the formation of H+ and OH- ions. The concentration of these ions determines the pH of the solution.
The dissociation constant of water (Kw) is a measure of the concentration of H+ and OH- ions in water. It is defined as Kw = [H+][OH-].
The ionization constant of water (Kw) is the equilibrium constant for the dissociation of water into hydrogen and hydroxide ions. It is expressed as Kw = [H+][OH-].
The pH scale is a logarithmic scale used to express the concentration of hydrogen ions in a solution. It is defined as pH = -log[H+].
Acidic solutions have [H+] > [OH-], neutral solutions have [H+] = [OH-], and basic solutions have [H+] < [OH-].
The molarity of pure water can be calculated using its density and molar mass. It is given as 55.55 M.
The value of Kw is temperature dependent, being an equilibrium constant.

concepts.concept_title: pH Paper and Measurement of pH
concepts.description: The pH of a solution can be found roughly with the help of pH paper. Measurement of pH is essential in biological and cosmetic applications.
The pH scale is used to measure the acidity or basicity of a solution. It is defined as the negative logarithm of the concentration of hydrogen ions in a solution.

concepts.concept_title: pH of Acidic, Basic, and Neutral Solutions
concepts.description: Acidic solutions have a pH < 7, basic solutions have a pH > 7, and neutral solutions have a pH = 7.
pH paper is used to determine the pH of a solution with an accuracy of ~0.5, while pH meters are used for greater accuracy and can measure pH within 0.001 precision.
The pKw is the negative logarithm of the water dissociation constant (Kw) and is equal to 14 at 25°C. The relationship between pH and pOH is given by pH + pOH = 14.
The pH of a solution can be calculated using the formula pH = -log[H+].
The ionization constant of a weak acid (Ka) is a measure of its strength. It is defined as the ratio of the concentrations of the ions produced to the concentration of the undissociated acid.
The pH scale is a measure of the hydrogen ion concentration. pKa is the negative logarithm of the ionization constant of an acid (Ka).

concepts.concept_title: Extent of Ionization and Degree of Ionization
concepts.description: The extent of ionization (α) is a measure of the degree to which an acid ionizes in a solution. The degree of ionization is related to the ionization constant and the initial concentration of the acid.
The equilibrium concentrations of all species in a solution can be calculated using the ionization constant and the initial concentration of the acid.
Brönsted-Lowry acids and bases are defined as species that donate or accept protons (H+ ions).

concepts.concept_title: Step-wise Approach to Evaluate pH of Weak Electrolytes
concepts.description: A step-wise approach can be used to evaluate the pH of weak electrolytes by identifying the species present, writing balanced equations, and identifying the primary and subsidiary reactions.
The degree of dissociation (α) is the extent to which a molecule dissociates into its constituent particles. It is a measure of the strength of an acid or base.
The ionization constant of an acid (Ka) is a measure of the strength of an acid. It is expressed as the ratio of the concentrations of the products to the reactants at equilibrium.
The ionization constant of a base (Kb) is a measure of the strength of a base. It is expressed as the ratio of the concentrations of the products to the reactants at equilibrium.
Percent dissociation is a measure of the strength of an acid or base. It is calculated as the ratio of the concentration of dissociated species to the initial concentration.
The equilibrium concentration is the concentration of a species at equilibrium. It can be calculated using the ionization constant and the initial concentration.
The pH of a solution can be calculated using the concentration of hydrogen ions. It is a measure of the acidity or basicity of a solution.
pKa and pKb are the negative logarithms of the ionization constants of an acid and base, respectively. They are measures of the strength of an acid or base.

concepts.concept_title: Percent Dissociation of a Weak Acid
concepts.description: The percent dissociation of a weak acid can be calculated using the equilibrium constant expression. For example, given the equilibrium constant of HOCl, we can determine its percent dissociation.
The ionization constants of weak bases, such as dimethylamine, triethylamine, and ammonia, are given. These constants represent the strength of a base in aqueous solution.
The ionization constants of an acid (Ka) and its conjugate base (Kb) are related. If one is known, the other can be deduced using the ionic product of water.
The pKb and pKa values are related to the ionization constants of a base and an acid, respectively. These relationships can be used to calculate the strength of an acid or a base.
The ionization constant of a weak base, such as hydrazine, can be calculated from its pH and concentration.
The equilibrium constant expression for a weak acid or base can be written in terms of the concentrations of the species involved.
The ionization constant of a base (Kb) is a measure of the strength of a base in solution. It is defined as the equilibrium constant for the dissociation of a base in water.
For a conjugate acid-base pair, the product of the acid dissociation constant (Ka) and the base dissociation constant (Kb) is equal to the water dissociation constant (Kw).
The pH of a solution can be calculated using the concentrations of the acid and its conjugate base, and the acid dissociation constant (Ka) or base dissociation constant (Kb).
Some acids have more than one replaceable hydrogen atom and are called di- or polybasic acids. They can donate more than one proton (H+ ion) per molecule.
Some bases can accept more than one proton (H+ ion) per molecule and are called polyacidic bases.
Polyprotic acids are acids that can donate more than one proton (H+ ion) per molecule. Examples include dibasic acids like H2X and tribasic acids like H3PO4.
Polyprotic acids have multiple ionization constants, denoted as Ka1, Ka2, etc. These constants represent the acid's ability to donate protons in a stepwise manner.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship Between Bond Strength and Acid Strength
concepts.description: The strength of an acid is influenced by the strength of the H-A bond. A weaker H-A bond results in a stronger acid. Down a group in the periodic table, acid strength increases as H-A bond strength decreases.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship Between Bond Polarity and Acid Strength
concepts.description: The polarity of the H-A bond also affects acid strength. Across a row in the periodic table, acid strength increases as the electronegativity of A increases, leading to greater bond polarity.

concepts.concept_title: Common Ion Effect in Acids and Bases
concepts.description: The common ion effect influences the ionization of acids and bases. In a solution containing a mixture of acids, the primary reaction involves the dissociation of the strongest acid.
The dissociation of an acid in water is represented by an equilibrium equation, e.g., CH3COOH(aq) ⇌ H+(aq) + CH3COO–(aq). The equilibrium constant (Ka) is given by Ka = [H+][Ac–] / [HAc].

The common ion effect is a shift in equilibrium on adding a substance that provides more of an ionic species already present in the dissociation equilibrium. This phenomenon is based on the Le Chatelier's principle.

The extent of dissociation of an acid depends on the strength and polarity of the H-A bond. The acid strength is influenced by the stability of the conjugate base and the ability of the acid to donate a proton.
The pH of a solution can be calculated using the concentration of hydrogen ions [H+]. The pH is given by pH = –log[H+].
The equilibrium constant expression for a dissociation reaction is given by Ka = [H+][Ac–] / [HAc]. For weak acids, the expression can be simplified as Ka = [H+]^2 / [HAc].
Salts formed by reactions between acids and bases undergo ionization in water, affecting pH.
Salts can be classified into four types based on hydrolysis: (i) weak acid and strong base, (ii) strong acid and weak base, (iii) weak acid and weak base, and (iv) strong acid and strong base.
The pH of salt solutions depends on the nature of the salt and can be calculated using pKa and pKb values.

concepts.concept_title: Hydrolysis of Salts of Weak Acid and Strong Base
concepts.description: Salts of weak acids and strong bases undergo hydrolysis, resulting in a basic solution.

concepts.concept_title: Hydrolysis of Salts of Strong Acid and Weak Base
concepts.description: Salts of strong acids and weak bases undergo hydrolysis, resulting in an acidic solution.

concepts.concept_title: Hydrolysis of Salts of Weak Acid and Weak Base
concepts.description: Salts of weak acids and weak bases undergo hydrolysis, and the pH depends on the difference between pKa and pKb values.
The pH of salt solutions can be calculated using the equation pH = 7 + ½ (pKa - pKb) for salts of weak acids and weak bases.

Buffer solutions resist change in pH on dilution or with the addition of small amounts of acid or alkali. They are prepared from a weak acid and its salt with a strong base or a weak base and its salt with a strong acid.
The pH of a buffer solution can be calculated using the equation pH = pKa + log([Salt]/[Acid]) or pH = pKb + log([Salt]/[Base]).

Buffer solutions of known pH can be prepared using the knowledge of pKa, pKb, and equilibrium constant. The ratio of salt and acid or salt and base concentrations can be controlled to achieve a specific pH.

Acidic buffer solutions are prepared using a weak acid and its salt with a strong base. The pH of the buffer solution is equal to the pKa of the acid when the concentrations of acid and salt are equal.
Salts of weak acids and strong bases or weak bases and strong acids undergo hydrolysis, leading to a change in pH. This is due to the formation of H+ or OH- ions.
The pH of a salt solution can be calculated using the equation pH = 7 + 1/2(pKa - pKb) for salts of weak acids and strong bases or pH = 7 - 1/2(pKa - pKb) for salts of weak bases and strong acids.

The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation is used to calculate the pH of a buffer solution. It is given by pH = pKa + log([conjugate base]/[acid]). This equation helps in understanding the relationship between the pH of a buffer solution and the concentrations of the acid and its conjugate base.

The pH of a buffer solution can be calculated using the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation. If the molar concentrations of the base and its conjugate acid are the same, then the pH of the buffer solution will be the same as the pKa of the acid.

The solubility product constant (Ksp) is a measure of the solubility of a sparingly soluble salt. It is defined as the product of the concentrations of the ions in a saturated solution. For example, for BaSO4, Ksp = [Ba2+][SO42-].
Sparingly soluble salts establish an equilibrium between the undissolved solid and the ions in a saturated solution. This equilibrium is characterized by the solubility product constant (Ksp).
The solubility product constant is the product of the concentrations of ions in a saturated solution. It is a measure of the solubility of a salt.
Solvation enthalpy is the energy released when ions interact with a solvent. It depends on the nature of the solvent.
Lattice enthalpy is the energy required to overcome the forces of attraction between ions in a solid.

concepts.concept_title: Classification of Salts based on Solubility
concepts.description: Salts are classified into three categories: soluble, slightly soluble, and sparingly soluble based on their solubility.
The common ion effect states that if the concentration of one of the ions is increased, it will combine with the ion of its opposite charge and reduce the solubility of the salt.

concepts.concept_title: Solubility of Salts of Weak Acids
concepts.description: The solubility of salts of weak acids is affected by the concentration of the ions.

The common ion effect states that if a solution is at equilibrium, and one of the ions in the solution is also introduced through another compound, the equilibrium will shift to consume some of the added ion. This effect is used to precipitate ions as sparingly soluble salts.
The solubility product constant (Ksp) is a measure of the equilibrium between a solid ionic compound and its ions in a solution. It represents the levels of the ions in a saturated solution.
The solubility of a salt can be affected by the pH of the solution. A change in pH can change the concentration of ions, which in turn affects the solubility of the salt.
Ions can be precipitated out of a solution by adding a compound that forms a sparingly soluble salt with the ion. This is a common method used in gravimetric analysis.
The solubility of sparingly soluble salts can be calculated using the solubility product constant (Ksp). The solubility of these salts is typically very low.

Le Chatelier's principle states that a change in any factor such as temperature, pressure, concentration, etc. will cause the equilibrium to shift in such a direction so as to reduce or counteract the effect of the change.
The reaction quotient Q is equal to K at equilibrium. Q is used to predict the direction of a reaction.
Substances that conduct electricity in aqueous solutions are called electrolytes. Electrolytes can be strong or weak, depending on their degree of dissociation.

concepts.concept_title: Arrhenius Definition of Acids and Bases
concepts.description: According to Arrhenius, acids give hydrogen ions while bases produce hydroxyl ions in their aqueous solutions.

concepts.concept_title: Brönsted-Lowry Definition of Acids and Bases
concepts.description: A Brönsted-Lowry acid is a proton donor and a base is a proton acceptor. A conjugate pair of acid-base differs only by one proton.

concepts.concept_title: Lewis Definition of Acids and Bases
concepts.description: A Lewis acid is an electron pair acceptor and a base is an electron pair donor.

concepts.concept_title: Ionization Constants of Weak Acids and Bases
concepts.description: The expressions for ionization (equilibrium) constants of weak acids (Ka) and weak bases (Kb) are developed using Arrhenius definition.
The pH scale (pH = –log[H+]) is used to express the hydrogen ion concentration. pOH, pKa, and pKb are also defined.
Salts of strong acid and weak base, weak acid and strong base, and weak acid and weak base undergo hydrolysis in aqueous solution.
Buffer solutions are defined and their importance is discussed briefly.

concepts.concept_title: Solubility Equilibrium and Solubility Product Constant (Ksp)
concepts.description: The solubility equilibrium of sparingly soluble salts is discussed and the equilibrium constant is introduced as solubility product constant (Ksp).

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Change in Volume on Vapour Pressure
concepts.description: The vapour pressure of a liquid in equilibrium with its vapour in a sealed container is affected by a change in volume. When the volume of the container is suddenly increased, the vapour pressure decreases initially.

The rates of evaporation and condensation are affected by a change in volume. Initially, the rate of evaporation increases and the rate of condensation decreases when the volume of the container is suddenly increased.

The equilibrium constant expression is written in terms of the concentrations of the reactants and products. For a reaction, the equilibrium constant expression is given by K = [C]^c[D]^d / [A]^a[B]^b.
For heterogeneous equilibria, the concentrations of pure solids and liquids are ignored while writing the equilibrium constant expression.
