The relationship between Kc and Kp is given by Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn, where Δn is the difference in the number of moles of gases between products and reactants.
The equilibrium concentrations of reactants and products can be calculated using the equilibrium constant expression and the initial concentrations of reactants and products.
The common ion effect is the effect of a common ion on the solubility of a sparingly soluble salt.
The equilibrium constant expression is a mathematical expression that describes the ratio of the concentrations of products to reactants at equilibrium.
The law of mass action states that the equilibrium constant is equal to the ratio of the product of the concentrations of the products to the product of the concentrations of the reactants.
Kc is the equilibrium constant in terms of concentration, while Kp is the equilibrium constant in terms of partial pressure.
Homogeneous equilibria involve reactants and products in the same phase, while heterogeneous equilibria involve reactants and products in different phases.
To calculate equilibrium concentrations, we can use the equilibrium constant expression and the given initial concentrations.
The reaction quotient (Q) is used to determine if a reaction is at equilibrium, and the equilibrium constant (K) is used to describe the ratio of products to reactants at equilibrium.
The equilibrium constant is dependent on temperature, and a change in temperature can affect the value of the equilibrium constant.
The equilibrium constant expression is a mathematical expression that describes the ratio of the concentrations of products to reactants at equilibrium.
The equilibrium constant can be calculated using the concentrations of reactants and products at equilibrium.
The equilibrium partial pressures of gases can be calculated using the equilibrium constant expression and the initial partial pressures.
The reaction quotient is a measure of the composition of a reaction mixture at a given time.
The direction of a reaction can be determined by comparing the reaction quotient to the equilibrium constant.
Changes in concentration can affect the equilibrium position of a reaction.
Changes in pressure can affect the equilibrium position of a reaction.
Changes in temperature can affect the equilibrium constant and position of a reaction.
The Gibbs free energy change is related to the equilibrium constant.

The equilibrium constant expression for a reaction is written as the product of the concentrations of the products raised to their stoichiometric coefficients divided by the product of the concentrations of the reactants raised to their stoichiometric coefficients.
The equilibrium constant for a reverse reaction is the reciprocal of the equilibrium constant for the forward reaction.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Concentration Changes on Equilibrium
concepts.description: If the concentration of a reactant or product is changed, the equilibrium will shift in a direction that tends to restore the equilibrium constant.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Pressure Changes on Equilibrium
concepts.description: If the pressure is increased, the equilibrium will shift in a direction that tends to reduce the pressure, i.e., to the side with fewer moles of gas.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Temperature Changes on Equilibrium
concepts.description: If the temperature is increased, the equilibrium will shift in a direction that tends to absorb heat, i.e., to the endothermic direction.
A conjugate acid-base pair consists of two species that differ by one proton.
A Lewis acid is a species that can accept a pair of electrons, while a Lewis base is a species that can donate a pair of electrons.
A Brönsted acid is a species that can donate a proton, while a Brönsted base is a species that can accept a proton.
The pH of a solution is a measure of its hydrogen ion concentration, given by pH = -log[H+].
The ionization constant of a weak acid is a measure of its strength, given by Ka = [H+][A-]/[HA].
The ionization constant of a conjugate base is related to the ionization constant of its conjugate acid.
The ionization constant of a weak acid is a measure of its strength. It is denoted by Ka and is used to calculate the concentration of ions in a solution.
The concentration of ions in a solution can be calculated using the ionization constant and the initial concentration of the acid.
The degree of ionization is the ratio of the concentration of ions to the initial concentration of the acid. It is a measure of the extent of ionization.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Common Ion on Ionization
concepts.description: The presence of a common ion in a solution can affect the ionization of a weak acid. This is known as the common ion effect.
The pH of a solution can be calculated using the concentration of hydrogen ions. It is a measure of the acidity or basicity of a solution.
The pKa of an acid is related to its ionization constant. It is a measure of the strength of an acid.
The hydrogen ion concentration of a solution can be calculated from its pH. It is a measure of the acidity of a solution.
The solubility of salts can be used to calculate the concentration of ions in a solution.
The ionization constant of a weak acid is a measure of its strength. It is calculated using the formula Ka = [H+][A-]/[HA].
The degree of ionization of an acid is the ratio of the concentration of ionized acid to the total concentration of acid. It is calculated using the formula α = [H+]/C.
The pH of a weak acid solution can be calculated using the ionization constant and the degree of ionization.
The ionization constant of a weak base is a measure of its strength. It is calculated using the formula Kb = [OH-][BH+)/[B].
The hydrolysis of salts is a reaction in which the salt reacts with water to form an acid and a base. The degree of hydrolysis can be calculated using the ionization constant.
The solubility product constant is a measure of the solubility of a sparingly soluble salt. It is calculated using the formula Ksp = [M+][X-].
The solubility of a sparingly soluble salt can be calculated using the solubility product constant.
The common ion effect is a phenomenon in which the solubility of a sparingly soluble salt is reduced by the presence of a common ion.
The pH of a salt solution can be calculated using the ionization constant and the degree of hydrolysis.
The ionic product of water is a measure of the concentration of hydrogen and hydroxide ions in water. It is calculated using the formula Kw = [H+][OH-].
Redox reactions involve simultaneous oxidation and reduction. These reactions are a class of reactions where one substance is oxidized while another is reduced.
Oxidation is the addition of oxygen or removal of hydrogen/electropositive element from a substance. Reduction is the removal of oxygen or addition of hydrogen/electropositive element to a substance.
An oxidant (oxidising agent) is a substance that causes oxidation, while a reductant (reducing agent) is a substance that causes reduction.
Redox reactions occur through the transfer of electrons. Oxidation involves the loss of electrons, while reduction involves the gain of electrons.
The oxidation number is a number assigned to an element in a compound that represents the number of electrons lost or gained by that element.
Redox reactions can be classified into combination (synthesis), decomposition, displacement, and disproportionation reactions.
Chemical equations can be balanced using the oxidation number method or the half-reaction method.
Oxidation is the addition of oxygen or electronegative element to a substance, or removal of hydrogen or electropositive element from a substance.
Reduction is the removal of oxygen from a compound, or addition of hydrogen or electropositive element to a substance.
Oxidation and reduction always occur simultaneously, hence, the word 'redox' was coined for this class of chemical reactions.
Electronegative elements are those that gain electrons, while electropositive elements are those that lose electrons.

Redox reactions involve the transfer of electrons from one species to another, resulting in a change in oxidation state. These reactions can be broken down into two half-reactions: oxidation (loss of electrons) and reduction (gain of electrons).
Oxidation is the loss of electrons by a species, while reduction is the gain of electrons by a species. These processes occur simultaneously in a redox reaction.
An oxidising agent is a species that accepts electrons (causes oxidation), while a reducing agent is a species that donates electrons (causes reduction).
Half-reactions are the individual oxidation and reduction reactions that occur in a redox reaction. They explicitly show the involvement of electrons.
Redox reactions involve the transfer of electrons from one species to another, resulting in a change in oxidation state. These reactions can be split into two half-reactions: oxidation and reduction.
Oxidation involves the loss of electrons, while reduction involves the gain of electrons. Oxidation and reduction occur simultaneously in a redox reaction.
Half-reactions are the oxidation and reduction reactions that occur in a redox reaction. They can be written separately to understand the electron transfer process.
In competitive electron transfer reactions, different species compete for electrons. The reaction that occurs depends on the relative tendencies of the species to lose or gain electrons.
Redox reactions can reach equilibrium, where the rates of forward and reverse reactions are equal. The equilibrium constant can be used to predict the extent of the reaction.

Oxidation number denotes the oxidation state of an element in a compound. It is ascertained according to a set of rules formulated on the basis that electron pair in a covalent bond is shared unequally.

Electron transfer is assumed to occur from a less electronegative atom to a more electronegative atom in a covalent compound. This assumption helps in keeping track of electron shifts in chemical reactions.
Oxidation involves the loss of electrons, while reduction involves the gain of electrons. This can be understood in terms of electron transfer from one species to another.

The tendency of metals to release electrons can be compared and listed in a metal activity series or electrochemical series. This series helps in understanding the competition for electrons between various metals.
Galvanic cells are a class of cells where chemical reactions become the source of electrical energy. The competition for electrons between various metals helps in designing these cells.
Oxidation number is a number assigned to an element in a compound or ion. It represents the number of electrons gained or lost by an atom.
There are rules to determine the oxidation number of an element in a compound or ion. These rules help in calculating the oxidation number.

concepts.concept_title: Oxidation Number of Elements in Free State
concepts.description: In elements, in the free or uncombined state, each atom bears an oxidation number of zero.
For ions composed of only one atom, the oxidation number is equal to the charge on the ion.
The oxidation number of oxygen in most compounds is –2. However, it is different in peroxides and superoxides.
The oxidation number of hydrogen is +1, except when it is bonded to metals in binary compounds.
Fluorine has an oxidation number of –1 in all its compounds. Other halogens have an oxidation number of –1 when they occur as halide ions.

concepts.concept_title: Sum of Oxidation Numbers in Compounds and Ions
concepts.description: The algebraic sum of the oxidation numbers of all the atoms in a compound must be zero. In polyatomic ions, the sum must equal the charge on the ion.
The oxidation number of an element in a compound is a number that represents the number of electrons lost or gained by an atom of that element in the compound.
Oxygen has different oxidation numbers in different compounds. It is -2 in most compounds, -1 in peroxides, -1/2 in superoxides, and +2 or +1 when bonded to fluorine.
Stock notation is a way of expressing the oxidation number of a metal in a compound using Roman numerals in parentheses after the symbol of the metal.
Oxidation is an increase in the oxidation number of an element, while reduction is a decrease in the oxidation number of an element.
An oxidising agent is a reagent that increases the oxidation number of an element, while a reducing agent is a reagent that decreases the oxidation number of an element.
Redox reactions are reactions that involve a change in oxidation number of the interacting elements.

Oxidation number is a number assigned to an element in a compound that represents the number of electrons lost or gained by the element in the compound. It helps in identifying the oxidation state of an element.

Redox reactions involve the transfer of electrons from one species to another, resulting in a change in oxidation state. These reactions can be classified into combination, decomposition, displacement, and disproportionation reactions.

There are several types of redox reactions, including combination reactions, decomposition reactions, displacement reactions, and disproportionation reactions. Each type involves a different mechanism of electron transfer.
Combination reactions involve the reaction of two or more elements to form a compound. These reactions are also known as synthesis reactions.
Decomposition reactions involve the breakdown of a compound into two or more simpler substances. These reactions can be redox or non-redox reactions.
Displacement reactions involve the replacement of an ion or atom in a compound by another ion or atom. These reactions can be metal displacement or non-metal displacement reactions.
An oxidant is a species that causes oxidation by accepting electrons, while a reductant is a species that causes reduction by donating electrons.
Decomposition reactions lead to the breakdown of a compound into two or more components, at least one of which must be in the elemental state.
A reducing metal is a better reducing agent than the one that is being reduced, showing more capability to lose electrons.
Non-metal displacement redox reactions include hydrogen displacement and rarely occurring oxygen displacement reactions.
All alkali metals and some alkaline earth metals are very good reductants and displace hydrogen from cold water.
The power of halogens as oxidising agents decreases as we move down from fluorine to iodine in group 17 of the periodic table.
Fluorine can replace chloride, bromide, and iodide ions in solution, but chlorine, bromine, and iodine have limited displacement reactions.
Many metals, including those which do not react with cold water, are capable of displacing hydrogen from acids.
Oxidation and reduction reactions involve the transfer of electrons, resulting in a change in oxidation state. Oxidation is the loss of electrons, while reduction is the gain of electrons.

Halogen displacement reactions involve the displacement of a less reactive halogen by a more reactive halogen. These reactions have industrial applications, such as the recovery of halogens from their halides.
The layer test is a laboratory test used to identify halide ions, such as bromide and iodide, based on their reactivity with halogens.
Disproportionation reactions involve the simultaneous oxidation and reduction of an element in one oxidation state, resulting in the formation of higher and lower oxidation states.
The reactivity series of metals reflects their ability to lose electrons and react with acids, such as hydrochloric acid, to produce hydrogen gas.
A disproportionation reaction is a type of redox reaction where a species is simultaneously oxidized and reduced to form two different products.
The oxidation state of an element in a compound is a measure of the number of electrons lost or gained by an atom of that element in the compound.
In some compounds, the oxidation number of an element can be a fraction, which can be confusing as it seems to imply a fraction of an electron.
Redox reactions can be classified into different types, such as combination, decomposition, displacement, and disproportionation reactions.

The concept of fractional oxidation state, which is an average oxidation state of an element in a compound, often revealed by structural parameters. This average oxidation state may not reflect the actual oxidation states of the element in the compound.
The oxidation number of an element in a compound, which can be a whole number or a fraction. It is used to keep track of electrons transferred during a reaction.
Compounds that contain an element in multiple oxidation states, often resulting in a fractional average oxidation state. Examples include Fe3O4, Mn3O4, and Pb3O4.
A method used to balance oxidation-reduction reactions by tracking changes in oxidation numbers of atoms involved in the reaction.

Oxidation number is a number assigned to an element in a chemical compound that represents the number of electrons lost or gained by the element in the compound. It is used to determine the oxidation state of an element.
Oxidation numbers are assigned to elements in a compound by following certain rules, such as the oxidation number of oxygen being -2 and that of hydrogen being +1.
A change in oxidation number indicates that an element has been oxidized or reduced. An increase in oxidation number indicates oxidation, while a decrease indicates reduction.
An oxidizing agent is a substance that causes another substance to be oxidized, while a reducing agent is a substance that causes another substance to be reduced.

Redox reactions can be balanced by using two methods: the oxidation number method and the half-reaction method. These methods involve calculating the change in oxidation number and splitting the reaction into two half-reactions.

The half-reaction method involves splitting the redox reaction into two half-reactions: one for oxidation and one for reduction. These half-reactions are then balanced and combined to form the overall balanced equation.

concepts.concept_title: Balancing Redox Reactions in Acidic and Basic Solutions
concepts.description: Redox reactions can be balanced in both acidic and basic solutions by adding H+ or OH- ions to the reaction. This ensures that the reaction is balanced in terms of both charge and mass.
key_terms: Balancing Redox Reactions in Acidic and Basic Solutions

concepts.description: Oxidation number is a number assigned to an element in a chemical compound that represents the number of electrons lost or gained by the element in the compound.

To assign oxidation numbers, we need to follow certain rules such as the oxidation number of a free element is zero, the oxidation number of a monatomic ion is equal to its charge, and the sum of the oxidation numbers of all atoms in a neutral compound is zero.

concepts.concept_title: Increase and Decrease in Oxidation Number
concepts.description: In a redox reaction, the oxidation number of an element increases (oxidation) or decreases (reduction). The increase in oxidation number is equal to the decrease in oxidation number in a redox reaction.
Redox equations can be balanced by using the oxidation number method or the half-reaction method. The goal is to make the increase in oxidation number equal to the decrease in oxidation number.
In the half-reaction method, the two half-equations (oxidation and reduction) are balanced separately and then added together to give the balanced equation.

concepts.concept_title: Balancing Equations in Acidic and Basic Medium
concepts.description: When balancing redox equations, we need to consider the medium in which the reaction occurs. In acidic medium, H+ ions are added to balance the equation, while in basic medium, OH- ions are added.

Redox equations can be balanced using the half-reaction method. This involves separating the equation into oxidation and reduction half-reactions, balancing atoms and charges, and then combining the half-reactions.
Oxidation half-reactions involve the loss of electrons, while reduction half-reactions involve the gain of electrons. These half-reactions are used to balance redox equations.

concepts.concept_title: Balancing Atoms and Charges in Half-Reactions
concepts.description: To balance half-reactions, atoms are balanced first, followed by charges. This is done by adding electrons, H2O, or H+ ions to the half-reaction.

To combine half-reactions, the number of electrons lost in the oxidation half-reaction must equal the number of electrons gained in the reduction half-reaction. This is done by multiplying one or both half-reactions by a suitable number.

concepts.concept_title: Balancing Redox Equations in Basic Medium
concepts.description: To balance redox equations in basic medium, the equation is first balanced as in acidic medium. Then, for each H+ ion, an equal number of OH- ions are added to both sides of the equation, and H+ and OH- ions are combined to form H2O.

Balancing redox equations involves several steps: writing half-reactions, balancing atoms and charges, and equalizing electrons. This process helps in understanding the stoichiometry of redox reactions.

There are different types of redox indicators, including self-indicators, which change color dramatically at the endpoint, and non-self-indicators, which require an additional substance to signal the endpoint.

Titration methods can be used to determine the strength of a reductant or oxidant using a redox-sensitive indicator. This involves reacting the substance with a known concentration of another redox substance until the endpoint is reached.
The concept of oxidation number has limitations, particularly in certain types of reactions where it may not be applicable or may lead to ambiguous results.

concepts.concept_title: Redox Reactions as Basis for Titrations
concepts.description: Redox reactions can be used as the basis for titrations, allowing for the determination of the strength of a reductant or oxidant. This involves using a redox-sensitive indicator to signal the endpoint.

Redox reactions involve the transfer of electrons from one species to another, resulting in a change in oxidation state. This process can be visualized as a decrease in electron density for oxidation and an increase in electron density for reduction.
Oxidation is the loss of electrons, while reduction is the gain of electrons. This results in a change in oxidation state for the species involved.
A redox couple consists of the oxidized and reduced forms of a substance taking part in an oxidation or reduction half reaction.
A redox reaction can be divided into two half reactions: oxidation and reduction. Each half reaction involves the transfer of electrons.
An electrochemical cell consists of two half cells, each containing a redox couple. The cell allows for the transfer of electrons between the half cells.
A salt bridge is a device used to connect two half cells in an electrochemical cell, allowing for the flow of ions between the cells.
The potential associated with each electrode is known as electrode potential. It is a measure of the relative tendency of the active species in the process to remain in the oxidised/reduced form.

The potential of each electrode is said to be the Standard Electrode Potential if the concentration of each species taking part in the electrode reaction is unity and the reaction is carried out at 298K.
A redox couple is a pair of oxidised and reduced forms of a species. The standard electrode potential of a redox couple is a measure of its tendency to remain in the oxidised/reduced form.

concepts.concept_title: Strength of Oxidising and Reducing Agents
concepts.description: A negative E means that the redox couple is a stronger reducing agent than the H+/H couple. A positive E means that the redox couple is a stronger oxidising agent.

Oxidation number is a number assigned to an element in a chemical compound that represents the number of electrons lost or gained by the element in the compound. It is used to keep track of the electrons during redox reactions.

There are a set of rules to assign oxidation numbers to elements in a compound. For example, the oxidation number of a free element is zero, and the oxidation number of a monatomic ion is equal to its charge.
Redox reactions are chemical reactions in which both oxidation and reduction occur simultaneously. Oxidation is the loss of electrons, while reduction is the gain of electrons.
Redox reactions can be classified into four categories: combination reactions, decomposition reactions, displacement reactions, and disproportionation reactions.
A redox couple is a pair of chemical species that differ by one electron. Redox couples are used to describe electrode processes and cells.
Electrode potentials are a measure of the tendency of a chemical species to gain or lose electrons. Standard electrode potentials are used to compare the reactivity of different chemical species.

A negative electrode potential indicates that the redox couple is a stronger reducing agent than the H+/H couple, while a positive electrode potential indicates that the redox couple is a weaker reducing agent than the H+/H couple.
A substance that causes oxidation is called an oxidising agent, while a substance that causes reduction is called a reducing agent. Oxidising agents gain electrons and reducing agents lose electrons.
Redox reactions can be classified based on the change in oxidation state of the elements involved. A reaction where a substance is both oxidised and reduced is called a disproportionation reaction.
The oxidation state of an element in a compound is a number that represents the number of electrons lost or gained by an atom of that element in the compound. It can be positive, negative, or zero.

Redox equations can be balanced by using the oxidation state method or the half-reaction method. The goal is to ensure that the number of electrons lost by the reducing agent equals the number of electrons gained by the oxidising agent.

concepts.concept_title: Strength of Oxidising and Reducing Agents
concepts.description: The strength of an oxidising agent or reducing agent depends on its ability to gain or lose electrons. A strong oxidising agent has a high tendency to gain electrons, while a strong reducing agent has a high tendency to lose electrons.

Fluorine is the best oxidant among halogens due to its high electronegativity and reactivity. Among hydrohalic compounds, hydroiodic acid is the best reductant due to the ease with which iodine can be oxidised.
Redox reactions are important in inorganic chemistry, where they are used to prepare various compounds and to understand the properties of elements and their compounds.
The path of redox reactions can be investigated using various techniques, including the use of radioactive isotopes and monitoring the change in concentration of reactants and products.
Redox reactions involve the transfer of electrons from one species to another, resulting in a change in oxidation state.
Oxidation number is a number assigned to an element in a chemical compound that represents the number of electrons lost or gained.
Balancing redox equations involves using the ion-electron method or oxidation number method to ensure that the number of electrons lost equals the number of electrons gained.
Types of redox reactions include disproportionation reactions, where a single element is both oxidized and reduced.
Electrochemical reactions involve the transfer of electrons through an external circuit, resulting in oxidation and reduction reactions.
Standard electrode potentials are used to predict the feasibility of electrochemical reactions.
The feasibility of redox reactions can be predicted using standard electrode potentials.
Disproportionation reactions involve the simultaneous oxidation and reduction of a single element.
The arrangement of metals in the order of their reactivity, with more reactive metals displacing less reactive ones from their salt solutions.
The measure of the tendency of a metal to lose or gain electrons, expressed as a voltage value.
The ability of a metal to donate electrons and act as a reducing agent, related to its standard electrode potential.
The depiction of a galvanic cell, including the electrodes, electrolyte, and direction of electron flow.
The individual chemical reactions occurring at the anode and cathode electrodes in a galvanic cell.
The particles responsible for carrying electric current in a galvanic cell.
The determination of which electrode is negatively charged in a galvanic cell.
Carbon has the unique property of forming covalent bonds with other carbon atoms, leading to the formation of long chains and rings.
Carbon has four valence electrons and can form four covalent bonds with other atoms, leading to a stable electronic configuration.
The shape of an organic molecule is determined by the arrangement of its atoms in space, which is influenced by the type of bonds and hybridization of the atoms.
Organic chemistry is the study of compounds that contain carbon and hydrogen, and is essential for understanding the properties and reactions of these compounds.
Organic compounds can be classified based on their structure, properties, and reactions.
The International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) system is used to name organic compounds in a systematic and consistent way.
Electronic displacements, such as inductive and resonance effects, can influence the structure and reactivity of organic compounds.
The study of the step-by-step process of an organic reaction, including the formation of intermediates and transition states.
Carbon has four valence electrons and forms four covalent bonds to achieve a stable octet configuration.
Carbon orbitals undergo hybridization to form bonds, resulting in different shapes and properties of molecules.
Carbon exhibits sp3, sp2, and sp hybridization, leading to tetrahedral, trigonal planar, and linear shapes, respectively.
Hybridization affects bond length and enthalpy, with sp hybrid orbitals forming shorter and stronger bonds.
The s-character of hybrid orbitals influences electronegativity, with greater s-character resulting in higher electronegativity.
π bonds form through parallel orientation of p orbitals on adjacent atoms, leading to sideways overlap.
π bonds are reactive centers, providing easy access to attacking reagents due to their electron charge cloud.

concepts.concept_title: Restriction of Rotation in C=C Bonds
concepts.description: The π bond in C=C bonds restricts rotation, resulting in a planar, trigonal shape.
Complete structural formulas show all the atoms and bonds in a molecule. They are also known as Lewis structures or dot structures, dash structures.

Condensed structural formulas are abbreviated forms of complete structural formulas. They omit some or all of the dashes representing covalent bonds and indicate the number of identical groups attached to an atom by a subscript.
Bond-line structural formulas use lines to represent carbon-carbon bonds. Carbon and hydrogen atoms are not shown, and only atoms like oxygen, chlorine, and nitrogen are specifically written.
Cyclic compounds can be represented using bond-line formulas, which show the ring structure and functional groups.
Condensed formulas can be expanded to show all atoms, including carbon and hydrogen. Bond-line formulas can also be converted to complete structural formulas.
Molecular models are physical devices used to visualize 3D shapes of organic molecules. There are three types: framework model, ball-and-stick model, and space filling model.
3D structure of organic molecules can be represented using solid and dashed wedge formulas. Solid wedge indicates a bond projecting out of the plane of paper towards the observer.
Framework model shows only bonds, ball-and-stick model shows atoms and bonds, and space filling model emphasizes relative size of atoms.
Organic compounds are broadly classified based on their structures into aliphatic and aromatic compounds.
Aliphatic compounds can be open-chain (acyclic) or closed-chain (cyclic or homocyclic).
Sometimes atoms other than carbon are present in the ring, known as heterocyclic compounds.
Organic compounds can be classified into acyclic (open chain) and cyclic (closed chain or ring) compounds.
Alicyclic compounds contain carbon atoms joined in the form of a ring.
Aromatic compounds are special types of compounds, including benzenoid and non-benzenoid compounds.
Heterocyclic aromatic compounds contain hetero atoms in the ring.
A functional group is an atom or a group of atoms joined to the carbon chain responsible for characteristic chemical properties.
A homologous series is a group of organic compounds each containing a characteristic functional group.
IUPAC system provides a systematic way of naming organic compounds.
IUPAC nomenclature is a systematic method of naming organic compounds. It correlates the name with the structure, allowing the reader to deduce the structure from the name.
In IUPAC nomenclature, the parent hydrocarbon and functional group(s) attached to it are identified to name the compound.

concepts.concept_title: Prefixes and Suffixes in IUPAC Nomenclature
concepts.description: Prefixes and suffixes are used to modify the parent name and obtain the actual name of the compound.
Compounds containing only carbon and hydrogen are called hydrocarbons. They can be saturated or unsaturated.
A hydrocarbon is saturated if it contains only carbon-carbon single bonds. Unsaturated hydrocarbons contain at least one carbon-carbon double or triple bond.
The IUPAC name for a homologous series of saturated hydrocarbons is alkane. They are also known as paraffins.
The names of straight-chain alkanes end with the suffix '-ane' and carry a prefix indicating the number of carbon atoms present in the chain.
An alkyl group is derived from a saturated hydrocarbon by removing a hydrogen atom from carbon. It is named by substituting 'yl' for 'ane' in the corresponding alkane.
In a branched chain compound, small chains of carbon atoms are attached at one or more carbon atoms of the parent chain.
The rules for naming branched chain alkanes include identifying the longest carbon chain, numbering the carbon atoms, and prefixing the names of alkyl groups attached as branches.
The longest carbon chain in the molecule is identified as the parent or root chain. This chain should have the maximum number of carbon atoms.
The carbon atoms of the parent chain are numbered to identify the parent alkane and to locate the positions of the carbon atoms at which branching takes place.
The names of alkyl groups attached as branches are prefixed to the name of the parent alkane. The position of the substituents is indicated by appropriate numbers.

If different alkyl groups are present, they are listed in alphabetical order. The prefixes such as di, tri, tetra, etc. are not considered while writing the name of the substituents in alphabetical order.
The branched alkyl groups can be named by following the above-mentioned procedures. The carbon atom of the branch that attaches to the root alkane is numbered 1.
If the two substituents are found in equivalent positions, the lower number is given to the one coming first in the alphabetical listing.

concepts.concept_title: IUPAC Nomenclature of Branched Chain Alkyl Groups
concepts.description: In IUPAC nomenclature, the name of a branched chain alkyl group is placed in parenthesis. The prefixes iso- and neo- are considered part of the fundamental name of the alkyl group.
A saturated monocyclic compound is named by prefixing 'cyclo' to the corresponding straight chain alkane. If side chains are present, the rules for nomenclature are applied.

concepts.concept_title: Selection of Parent Chain in Multi-Substituted Compounds
concepts.description: In multi-substituted compounds, the parent chain is selected based on the number of side chains. If two chains have an equal number of side chains, the chain with more side chains is selected.
The parent chain is numbered from the end closer to the substituent.

concepts.concept_title: Nomenclature of Organic Compounds with Functional Groups
concepts.description: A functional group is an atom or group of atoms bonded together in a unique manner, usually the site of chemical reactivity. Compounds with the same functional group undergo similar reactions.

concepts.concept_title: Use of Class Suffix in Nomenclature
concepts.description: The class suffix is used to indicate the functional group in a compound. The full name of the parent alkane is written before the class suffix.

IUPAC nomenclature is a systematic way of naming organic compounds. It involves identifying the functional group, determining the longest chain of carbon atoms, and numbering the chain to give the lowest possible number to the functional group.

The priority of functional groups is determined by their order of preference. The order of decreasing priority for some functional groups is: -COOH, -SO3H, -COOR, -COCl, -CONH2, -CN, >C=O, -OH, -NH2, >C=C<, -C≡C-.

The suffix of an organic compound is determined by the functional group present. The prefix is used to indicate the presence of substituents. The suffix and prefix are combined to form the IUPAC name of the compound.
In polyfunctional compounds, one functional group is chosen as the principal functional group. The remaining functional groups are named as substituents using prefixes.
The longest chain of carbon atoms containing the functional group is numbered to give the lowest possible number to the functional group.

IUPAC nomenclature is a systematic way of naming organic compounds. It involves identifying the longest continuous chain of carbon atoms and assigning suffixes and prefixes to indicate functional groups and substituents.
In IUPAC nomenclature, suffixes are used to indicate functional groups. For example, '-oic' acid for carboxylic acids, '-ol' for alcohols, and '-al' for aldehydes.
In IUPAC nomenclature, the carbon chain is numbered to give the lowest possible numbers to functional groups and substituents.
Substituted benzene compounds are named by placing the substituent as a prefix to the word benzene. The position of substituents is defined by numbering the carbon atoms of the ring.
In IUPAC nomenclature, substituents appear in the name in alphabetical order.
In IUPAC nomenclature, the functional group with the highest priority is given the lowest number in the carbon chain.
The terms ortho (o), meta (m), and para (p) are used as prefixes to indicate the relative positions 1,2; 1,3 and 1,4 respectively on a benzene ring.
For tri- or higher substituted benzene derivatives, the compounds are named by identifying substituent positions on the ring by following the lowest locant rule.

When a benzene ring is attached to an alkane with a functional group, it is considered as a substituent, instead of a parent. The name for benzene as a substituent is phenyl (C6H5-, also abbreviated as Ph).
When two or more compounds have similar molecular formulas but different carbon skeletons, these are referred to as chain isomers and the phenomenon is termed as chain isomerism.

When two or more compounds differ in the position of a substituent atom or functional group on the carbon skeleton, they are called position isomers and this phenomenon is termed as position isomerism.
Two or more compounds having the same molecular formula but different functional groups are called functional isomers and this phenomenon is termed as functional group isomerism.
Compounds having the same molecular formula but different structures are classified as structural isomers.
The phenomenon of existence of two or more molecular formulas but different properties is known as isomerism. Such compounds are called isomers.
Isomerism is a phenomenon where compounds have the same molecular formula but different structural or spatial arrangements.
Structural isomerism occurs when compounds have the same molecular formula but differ in the way their atoms are connected.
Stereoisomerism occurs when compounds have the same molecular formula and sequence of covalent bonds but differ in relative positions of their atoms or groups in space.
Metamerism is a type of isomerism that arises due to different alkyl chains on either side of the functional group in a molecule.
A covalent bond can be cleaved either by heterolytic cleavage or homolytic cleavage.
Heterolytic cleavage is a type of bond cleavage where the shared pair of electrons remains with one of the fragments.
Carbocations are highly unstable and reactive species that have a carbon atom with a sextet of electrons and a positive charge.
The stability order of carbocations is CH3+ < CH3CH2+ < (CH3)2CH+ < (CH3)3C+. This order is due to inductive and hyperconjugation effects.
Carbocations have a trigonal planar shape with the positively charged carbon being sp2 hybridized.
Free radicals are formed by homolytic cleavage of a covalent bond, resulting in a neutral species with an unpaired electron.
Free radicals are classified as primary, secondary, or tertiary based on the alkyl group attached to the radical carbon.
The stability of free radicals increases from primary to tertiary due to hyperconjugation effects.
Carbanions are formed by heterolytic cleavage of a covalent bond, resulting in a carbon species with a negative charge.
Carbanions have a distorted tetrahedral shape with the carbon being sp3 hybridized.
In organic reactions, one molecule is named as the substrate and the other as the reagent, based on which carbon is involved in new bond formation.
Homolytic cleavage is a type of bond breaking where one electron of the shared pair in a covalent bond goes with each of the bonded atoms. This results in the formation of free radicals.
Heterolytic cleavage is a type of bond breaking where one atom retains both electrons of the shared pair in a covalent bond, resulting in the formation of ions.
A nucleophile is a reagent that brings an electron pair to the reactive site of a substrate. It is a species that donates an electron pair to form a covalent bond.
An electrophile is a reagent that takes away an electron pair from the reactive site of a substrate. It is a species that accepts an electron pair to form a covalent bond.
A reactive site is a part of a substrate molecule that can either donate or accept an electron pair. It can be electron deficient or electron rich.
Curved-arrow notation is used to show the movement of an electron pair from a nucleophile to an electrophile.
Electrophilic centers are atoms that have a partial positive charge due to polarity of a bond, making them electron deficient and able to receive a pair of electrons from nucleophiles.
The movement of electrons in organic reactions can be shown by curved-arrow notation, which indicates how changes in bonding occur due to electronic redistribution during the reaction.
The inductive effect is the polarization of a σ-bond caused by the polarization of an adjacent covalent bond, resulting in a shift of electron density and a partial positive charge on an atom.

A polar covalent bond is a bond between two atoms of different electronegativity, resulting in a shift of electron density and a partial positive charge on one atom and a partial negative charge on the other.
Electron displacement effects in covalent bonds refer to the shift of electron density in a molecule due to the influence of an atom or group of atoms.
The inductive effect is a permanent polarization of a bond due to the influence of an atom or substituent group. It is related to the ability of substituents to withdraw or donate electron density.
Substituents can be classified as electron-withdrawing or electron-donating groups relative to hydrogen. Examples of electron-withdrawing groups include halogens, nitro, cyano, and carboxy groups.
The electromeric effect is a temporary displacement of electrons in a molecule when a reagent approaches to attack it. It is also known as the polarisability effect.

Resonance structures are hypothetical structures that contribute to the actual structure of a molecule. They are used to represent molecules that cannot be adequately described by a single Lewis structure.

A resonance hybrid is the actual structure of a molecule that is a combination of multiple resonance structures. The energy of the actual structure is lower than that of any individual resonance structure.
The polarity of a bond is determined by the electronegativity difference between the atoms involved. A bond is more polar if the electronegativity difference is greater.
Resonance structures are hypothetical structures that represent the delocalization of electrons in a molecule. They have the same positions of nuclei and the same number of unpaired electrons.
Resonance energy is the difference in energy between the actual structure of a molecule and its lowest energy resonance structure. It is a measure of the stability of a molecule due to resonance.

The rules for writing resonance structures include: (i) same positions of nuclei, (ii) same number of unpaired electrons, (iii) more covalent bonds, (iv) octet of electrons for atoms (except hydrogen), and (v) dispersal of charge.
The resonance effect is the polarity produced in a molecule by the interaction of two π-bonds or between a π-bond and a lone pair of electrons on an adjacent atom.

The positive resonance effect (+R effect) is the transfer of electrons away from an atom or substituent group attached to a conjugated system, resulting in high electron densities at certain positions.
The negative resonance effect (-R effect) is the transfer of electrons towards an atom or substituent group attached to a conjugated system, resulting in low electron densities at certain positions.
A conjugated system is a system with alternate single and double bonds in an open chain or cyclic system. These systems often show abnormal behaviour due to delocalisation of π-electrons.
The electromeric effect is a temporary effect that involves the complete transfer of a shared pair of π-electrons to one of the atoms joined by a multiple bond on the demand of an attacking reagent.
In the positive electromeric effect (+E effect), the π-electrons of the multiple bond are transferred to that atom to which the reagent gets attached.
In the negative electromeric effect (–E effect), the π-electrons of the multiple bond are not transferred to the atom to which the reagent gets attached.
Hyperconjugation is a permanent effect that involves the delocalisation of σ-electrons of a C—H bond into an adjacent empty p orbital or a p orbital with an unshared pair of electrons.
Resonance structures are used to describe the delocalisation of electrons in a conjugated system. These structures cannot be isolated and are contributing structures to the real structure.
The R effect refers to the effect of substituents on the electron density of a conjugated system. +R effect: electron-donating groups; -R effect: electron-withdrawing groups.
Hyperconjugation is a general stabilising interaction that involves delocalisation of σ electrons of C—H bond of an alkyl group directly attached to an atom of unsaturated system.
Organic reactions can be classified into substitution reactions, addition reactions, elimination reactions, and rearrangement reactions.
Sublimation is a method of purification of organic compounds where a solid changes directly to vapour without passing through the liquid phase.
Crystallisation is a method of purification of organic compounds where a solid is obtained from a solution in the form of crystals.
Distillation is a method of purification of organic compounds where a liquid is separated from a mixture based on differences in boiling points.
Differential extraction is a method of purification of organic compounds where a solid or liquid is separated from a mixture based on differences in solubility.
Chromatography is a method of purification and separation of organic compounds based on differences in interaction with a stationary phase and a mobile phase.
Electromeric effect is a type of resonance effect that involves the transfer of electrons through a conjugated system.
Inductive effect is a type of effect that involves the transfer of electrons through a σ-bonding system.
Sublimation is a purification technique used to separate sublimable compounds from non-sublimable impurities. It involves heating a solid substance to change it directly from solid to vapour state.
Crystallisation is a purification technique used to separate solid organic compounds based on their solubility differences in a suitable solvent.
Distillation is a purification technique used to separate volatile liquids from non-volatile impurities or liquids with different boiling points.
Fractional distillation is a technique used to separate liquids with small differences in boiling points using a fractionating column.

concepts.concept_title: Separation based on Solubility and Boiling Point
concepts.description: Impurities can be removed based on solubility and boiling point differences, using techniques like crystallisation and distillation.

Fractional distillation is a method used to separate a mixture of liquids with different boiling points. It involves the use of a fractionating column, which provides many surfaces for heat exchange between the ascending vapours and the descending condensed liquid.

Distillation under reduced pressure is a method used to purify liquids with very high boiling points or those that decompose at or below their boiling points. The pressure is reduced using a water pump or vacuum pump, allowing the liquid to boil at a lower temperature.

Steam distillation is a technique used to separate substances that are steam volatile and immiscible with water. Steam is passed through a heated flask containing the liquid to be distilled, and the mixture of steam and the volatile organic compound is condensed and collected.

Differential extraction is a method used to separate an organic compound from an aqueous medium. The compound is shaken with an organic solvent in which it is more soluble than in water, and the organic solvent is later removed by distillation or evaporation.
A technique used to separate substances based on difference in volatility. The mixture is heated with steam, and the vapours condense and collect separately.
A technique used to extract a compound from a mixture using a solvent repeatedly.
A technique used to separate mixtures into their components, purify compounds, and test the purity of compounds.
A type of chromatography based on the adsorption of compounds on an adsorbent to different degrees.
A type of chromatography based on the partition of compounds between two phases.
A technique used to separate a mixture over a column of adsorbent.
A technique used to separate a mixture using a thin layer of adsorbent.

A technique used to separate components of a mixture based on their degree of adsorption on an adsorbent. The mixture is placed on top of the adsorbent column and an eluant is allowed to flow down, separating the components.
A measure of the relative adsorption of a substance in chromatography, calculated as the ratio of the distance moved by the substance to the distance moved by the solvent.
A type of adsorption chromatography that involves separation of substances over a thin layer of an adsorbent coated on a glass plate.

concepts.concept_title: Detection of Colourless Compounds in TLC
concepts.description: Techniques used to detect colourless compounds in TLC, such as placing the plate under ultraviolet light or using a reagent that reacts with the compounds.
A type of chromatography based on continuous differential partitioning of components between stationary and mobile phases.
A type of partition chromatography that uses a special quality paper known as chromatography paper.

Lassaigne's test is used to detect the presence of elements like nitrogen, sulphur, halogens, and phosphorus in an organic compound. The compound is fused with sodium metal to convert these elements into ionic form.

The presence of nitrogen is confirmed by boiling the sodium fusion extract with iron(II) sulphate and then acidifying with concentrated sulphuric acid. Formation of Prussian blue colour indicates the presence of nitrogen.
Halogens are detected by adding silver nitrate to the sodium fusion extract. A yellow precipitate of AgX (X = Cl, Br, or I) confirms the presence of halogens.
Carbon and hydrogen are detected by heating the compound with copper(II) oxide. Carbon is oxidised to CO2 (tested with lime-water) and hydrogen to H2O (tested with anhydrous copper sulphate).

Paper chromatography is a technique used to separate coloured compounds based on their differing partition between two phases. A chromatogram is developed by allowing a solvent to flow over a spotted paper.
The presence of sulphur in a compound is tested by adding lead acetate to the sodium fusion extract, resulting in a black precipitate of lead sulphide.
The presence of phosphorus in a compound is tested by oxidizing it to phosphate and then treating it with ammonium molybdate, resulting in a yellow coloration or precipitate.
The presence of halogens in a compound is tested by acidifying the sodium fusion extract with nitric acid and then treating it with silver nitrate, resulting in a precipitate.
Quantitative analysis of organic compounds involves determining the mass percentage of elements present in a compound.
Carbon and hydrogen in an organic compound are estimated by burning the compound in excess oxygen and copper(II) oxide, and then measuring the mass of water and carbon dioxide produced.
Nitrogen in an organic compound is estimated using Dumas method or Kjeldahl's method.
The Dumas method involves heating a nitrogen-containing organic compound with copper oxide in an atmosphere of carbon dioxide to produce free nitrogen, which is then collected and measured.

concepts.concept_title: Calculation of Carbon and Hydrogen Percentage
concepts.description: The percentages of carbon and hydrogen in an organic compound can be calculated from the masses of water and carbon dioxide produced during combustion.

Kjeldahl's method involves heating a nitrogen-containing compound with concentrated sulfuric acid to convert nitrogen to ammonium sulfate, which is then heated with sodium hydroxide to liberate ammonia gas.
The volume of nitrogen collected at STP can be calculated using the ideal gas equation and the given conditions of temperature and pressure.
The percentage of nitrogen in an organic compound can be calculated from the volume of nitrogen collected at STP and the mass of the compound.

Kjeldahl method is used for estimating nitrogen in organic compounds. It involves treating the compound with concentrated H2SO4 to get ammonium sulphate, which liberates ammonia on treating with NaOH.
Kjeldahl method is not applicable to compounds containing nitrogen in nitro and azo groups and nitrogen present in the ring.
The percentage of nitrogen is calculated using the formula: 1.4×M×2(V−V/2) / m, where M is the molarity of H2SO4, V and V1 are the volumes of H2SO4 and NaOH used.
Carius method is used for estimating halogens in organic compounds. It involves heating the compound with fuming nitric acid and silver nitrate.
The percentage of halogen is calculated using the formula: (atomic mass of X × m) / (molecular mass of AgX × g), where X is the halogen.
The Carius method involves heating an organic compound with sodium peroxide or fuming nitric acid to oxidize sulphur to sulphuric acid, which is then precipitated as barium sulphate.
The percentage of sulphur in an organic compound can be calculated from the mass of barium sulphate formed using the relation: (32 × mass of BaSO4 × 100) / (233 × mass of organic compound).
The Carius method involves heating a halogen-containing organic compound with fuming nitric acid and silver nitrate to form a silver halide, which is then filtered, washed, dried, and weighed.

The percentage of halogen in an organic compound can be calculated from the mass of silver halide formed using the relation: (atomic mass of X × mass of AgX × 100) / (molecular mass of AgX × mass of organic compound).
Oxygen can be estimated directly by decomposing an organic compound and passing the gaseous products over red-hot coke to convert oxygen to carbon monoxide, which is then oxidized to carbon dioxide.

Phosphorus can be estimated by heating an organic compound with fuming nitric acid to oxidize phosphorus to phosphoric acid, which is then precipitated as ammonium phosphomolybdate or magnesium ammonium phosphate.

Phosphorus in an organic compound can be estimated by converting it into magnesium ammonium phosphate (MgNH4PO4) or magnesium pyrophosphate (Mg2P2O7). The percentage of phosphorus is calculated using the mass of the organic compound and the mass of the phosphate formed.

The percentage of oxygen in an organic compound can be estimated by difference between the total percentage of elements and the percentages of other elements. Oxygen can also be estimated by converting it into iodine and then calculating its percentage.

A CHN elemental analyser is an apparatus used to determine the elements carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen present in a compound. It requires only a small amount of the substance and displays the values on a screen within a short time.
The International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) has laid down a set of rules for naming organic compounds. IUPAC nomenclature correlates the names of compounds with their structures.

A functional group is an atom or group of atoms bonded together in a unique fashion that determines the physical and chemical properties of a compound. Organic compounds can be classified based on their functional groups.
A covalent bond may be cleaved in heterolytic or homolytic fashion. Heterolytic cleavage yields carbocations or carbanions, while homolytic cleavage gives free radicals as reactive intermediate.

Reactions proceeding through heterolytic cleavage involve the complimentary pairs of reactive species. These are electron pair donor known as nucleophile and an electron pair acceptor known as electrophile.

concepts.concept_title: Inductive, Resonance, Electromeric and Hyperconjugation Effects
concepts.description: The inductive, resonance, electromeric and hyperconjugation effects may help in the polarisation of a bond making certain carbon atom or other atom positions as places of low or high electron densities.
Organic reactions can be broadly classified into following types; substitution, addition, elimination and rearrangement reactions.

Purification, qualitative and quantitative analysis of organic compounds are carried out for determining their structures. The methods of purification namely : sublimation, distillation and differential extraction are based on the difference in one or more physical properties.
Chromatography is a useful technique of separation, identification and purification of compounds. It is classified into two categories : adsorption and partition chromatography.
Nitrogen, sulphur, halogens and phosphorus are detected by lassaigne’s test.
Carbon and hydrogen are estimated by determining the amounts of carbon dioxide and water produced. Nitrogen is estimated by dumas or Kjeldahl’s method and halogens by Carius method.
IUPAC nomenclature is a set of rules for naming organic compounds. It provides a systematic way of naming compounds to avoid confusion.
A homologous series is a series of compounds that have the same functional group and differ by a CH2 unit. Each member of the series has similar chemical properties.
Functional groups are specific groups of atoms within a molecule that determine its chemical properties. Examples include -OH, -CHO, and -COOH.

concepts.concept_title: Condensed and Bond Line Structural Formulas
concepts.description: Condensed formulas show the arrangement of atoms in a molecule using bonds and atoms. Bond line formulas show only the bonds and atoms at the ends of the bonds.
Electrophiles are electron-deficient species that accept a pair of electrons. Nucleophiles are electron-rich species that donate a pair of electrons.
Resonance structures are a set of Lewis structures that show the delocalization of electrons within a molecule.
Inductive effects refer to the transfer of electrons through sigma bonds. Electromeric effects refer to the transfer of electrons through pi bonds.
Homolysis is the cleavage of a bond where each atom retains one of the shared electrons. Heterolysis is the cleavage of a bond where one atom retains both shared electrons.
These are techniques used to separate and purify compounds based on their physical and chemical properties.
Isomerism is the phenomenon where compounds have the same molecular formula but different structural or spatial arrangements.

Lassaigne's test is used to detect the presence of nitrogen in an organic compound. The test involves fusing the compound with sodium metal, followed by the addition of iron(II) sulfate and acidification.
The Dumas method involves heating the organic compound with copper oxide to produce nitrogen gas, which is then measured.
Kjeldahl's method involves heating the organic compound with sulfuric acid to produce ammonia, which is then measured.

concepts.concept_title: Estimation of halogens, sulphur and phosphorus
concepts.description: The estimation of halogens, sulphur, and phosphorus involves various methods, including Carius method and Kjeldahl's method.
Paper chromatography is a technique used to separate and identify mixtures of substances based on their interactions with a stationary phase and a mobile phase.
The Carius method involves heating the organic compound with silver nitrate to produce a precipitate of silver halide, which is then measured.
Steam distillation is a technique used to separate mixtures of substances based on their boiling points and vapor pressures.
Sublimation is a technique used to separate mixtures of substances based on their ability to change directly from a solid to a gas.
Crystallisation is a technique used to separate mixtures of substances based on their solubility and crystal formation.
Nucleophilic substitution is a type of organic reaction where a nucleophile replaces a leaving group in a molecule.
Hybridisation of orbitals refers to the mixing of atomic orbitals to form new hybrid orbitals that are suitable for the pairing of electrons.
Carbocation stability refers to the stability of a positively charged carbon atom in a molecule.
Chromatography is a technique used to separate and identify mixtures of substances based on their interactions with a stationary phase and a mobile phase.
Hydrocarbons are compounds of carbon and hydrogen only. They are important sources of energy and play a key role in daily life.
Hydrocarbons can be classified into three main categories: saturated, unsaturated, and aromatic hydrocarbons.
Saturated hydrocarbons contain only carbon-carbon and carbon-hydrogen single bonds.
Hydrocarbons can be named according to the IUPAC system of nomenclature.
Isomers of alkanes, alkenes, alkynes, and aromatic hydrocarbons can be recognised and written.

concepts.concept_title: Physical and Chemical Properties of Hydrocarbons
concepts.description: Hydrocarbons can be distinguished based on their physical and chemical properties.
Various methods are used for the preparation of hydrocarbons.

concepts.concept_title: Role of Hydrocarbons as Energy Sources
concepts.description: Hydrocarbons are used as sources of energy and have various industrial applications.
Ethane has different conformations.

concepts.concept_title: Addition Products of Unsymmetrical Alkenes and Alkynes
concepts.description: The formation of addition products of unsymmetrical alkenes and alkynes can be predicted.
The structure of benzene and aromaticity can be understood.
The mechanism of electrophilic substitution reactions of benzene can be understood.

concepts.concept_title: Directive Influence of Substituents in Benzene Ring
concepts.description: The directive influence of substituents in a monosubstituted benzene ring can be predicted.
Alkanes are saturated open-chain hydrocarbons containing carbon-carbon single bonds. The general formula for alkanes is C_nH_(2n+2).

concepts.concept_title: Tetravalency of carbon and structure of methane
concepts.description: Carbon is tetravalent and has a tetrahedral structure, as seen in methane (CH4). The C-H bond angle is 109.5°.
C-C and C-H σ bonds in alkanes are formed by head-on overlapping of sp3 hybrid orbitals of carbon and 1s orbitals of hydrogen atoms.
Alkanes have specific nomenclature and can exhibit isomerism, where different structures can have the same molecular formula.
Alkanes can have straight-chain or branched-chain structures, leading to different isomers.
Alkanes are inert under normal conditions and do not react with acids, bases, and other reagents.
Chain isomers are structural isomers that differ in the chain of carbon atoms. They have the same molecular formula but different structures and properties.

concepts.concept_title: Primary, Secondary, Tertiary, and Quaternary Carbon Atoms
concepts.description: Carbon atoms can be classified based on the number of carbon atoms attached to them: primary (1°), secondary (2°), tertiary (3°), or quaternary (4°).
Alkyl groups are substituents derived from alkanes by removal of one hydrogen atom. They have the general formula C_nH_(2n+1).
The nomenclature of substituted alkanes involves applying general rules for naming compounds, considering the parent chain and substituents.
Isomers have the same molecular formula but differ in structure and properties. The difference in properties is due to the difference in their structures.

IUPAC nomenclature is a systematic way of naming organic compounds. It involves identifying the longest continuous chain of carbon atoms, numbering it, and then adding prefixes and suffixes to indicate substituents and functional groups.
Isomeric alkyl groups are alkyl groups with the same molecular formula but different structures. They can be obtained by attaching -OH groups at different carbons of the chain.
Alcohols are organic compounds that contain a hydroxyl (-OH) functional group attached to a carbon atom. They can be named by adding the suffix '-ol' to the parent alkane name.
The parent alkane is the longest continuous chain of carbon atoms in an organic compound. It serves as the basis for naming the compound.
Substituents are groups of atoms that replace hydrogen atoms in a parent alkane. They can be alkyl groups or other functional groups.
Alphabetical priority order is used to arrange substituents in alphabetical order when naming an organic compound.
IUPAC nomenclature involves identifying the longest continuous chain of carbon atoms and numbering it to give the lowest possible numbers to substituents.

concepts.concept_title: Writing Structural Formulas from IUPAC Names
concepts.description: To write structural formulas from IUPAC names, identify the parent chain and substituents, and arrange them according to the IUPAC name.
Hydrogenation involves adding dihydrogen gas to unsaturated hydrocarbons in the presence of catalysts like platinum, palladium, or nickel to form alkanes.

concepts.concept_title: Preparation of Alkanes from Alkyl Halides
concepts.description: Alkanes can be prepared from alkyl halides through reduction with zinc and dilute hydrochloric acid or through the Wurtz reaction with sodium metal in dry ether.
The Wurtz reaction involves treating alkyl halides with sodium metal in dry ether to form higher alkanes containing an even number of carbon atoms.

Decarboxylation is a process of elimination of carbon dioxide from a carboxylic acid. Sodium salts of carboxylic acids on heating with soda lime give alkanes containing one carbon atom less than the carboxylic acid.
Kolbe's electrolytic method involves the electrolysis of an aqueous solution of sodium or potassium salt of a carboxylic acid to give alkanes.
Alkanes are almost non-polar molecules due to the covalent nature of C-C and C-H bonds. They possess weak van der Waals forces, and their boiling points increase with molecular mass.
Alkanes are insoluble in water due to their non-polar nature. The principle 'like dissolves like' explains the solubility of substances in solvents.
One or more hydrogen atoms of alkanes can be replaced by halogens, nitro group, and sulphonic acid group through substitution reactions.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of branching on boiling point
concepts.description: As the number of branched chains increases, the molecule attains a spherical shape, resulting in smaller area of contact and weaker intermolecular forces, which are overcome at relatively lower temperatures.
Alkanes react with halogens in the presence of light or heat to form halogenated products through a free radical chain mechanism.

The mechanism involves three steps: initiation, propagation, and termination. Initiation involves homolysis of the halogen molecule, propagation involves the formation of free radicals, and termination involves the consumption of free radicals.
The rate of reaction of alkanes with halogens is F > Cl > Br > I.
The rate of replacement of hydrogens of alkanes is 3° > 2° > 1°.
Alkanes on heating in the presence of air or dioxygen are completely oxidized to carbon dioxide and water with the evolution of large amount of heat.
The general combustion equation for any alkane is: C n H 2n+2 + (3n+1)/2 O 2 → nCO 2 +(n+1)H 2 O
During incomplete combustion of alkanes with insufficient amount of air or dioxygen, carbon black is formed.
Alkanes on heating with a regulated supply of dioxygen or air at high pressure and in the presence of suitable catalysts give a variety of oxidation products.
Methane reacts with steam at 1273 K in the presence of nickel catalyst to form carbon monoxide and dihydrogen.
Higher alkanes on heating to higher temperature decompose into lower alkanes, alkenes etc.
The C-H bond in alkanes can undergo homolysis to generate free radicals.
The chlorination of alkanes involves a free radical chain reaction mechanism.
Pyrolysis or cracking is the process of breaking down organic compounds by applying heat. This process is used to prepare oil gas or petrol gas from kerosene oil or petrol.
Isomerisation is the process of converting n-alkanes to branched chain alkanes in the presence of anhydrous aluminium chloride and hydrogen chloride gas.
Aromatization is the process of converting n-alkanes having six or more carbon atoms to aromatic compounds on heating to 773K at 10-20 atmospheric pressure.
Conformations are spatial arrangements of atoms in space that can change into one another by rotation around a C-C single bond.
Sawhorse projections are a way of representing molecules by viewing them along the molecular axis and projecting them onto a plane.
Newman projections are a way of representing molecules by viewing them head-on along the C-C bond.
Torsional strain is a type of repulsive interaction between adjacent bonds that hinders rotation around a C-C single bond.
Eclipsed and staggered conformations are two types of conformations that occur in molecules due to rotation around a C-C single bond.

Conformational isomers are spatial arrangements of atoms in a molecule that can be converted into each other by rotation around a single bond. These isomers have the same molecular formula and bond sequence but differ in their three-dimensional arrangement.

Eclipsed conformation: Hydrogen atoms attached to two carbons are as close together as possible. Staggered conformation: Hydrogens are as far apart as possible. These are two extreme cases of conformations.
Newman projections are a way to represent the conformations of a molecule, particularly useful for representing eclipsed and staggered forms.
Torsional strain refers to the repulsive interaction between electron clouds of atoms that affects the stability of a conformation. It depends on the angle of rotation about a single bond.
A carbon-carbon double bond consists of one strong sigma (σ) bond and one weak pi (π) bond. The double bond is shorter than a single bond due to the presence of the pi bond.
Sawhorse projections are another way to represent the conformations of a molecule, particularly useful for representing eclipsed and staggered forms.

Torsional strain is the energy associated with the rotation around a single bond. The staggered conformation has the least torsional strain, while the eclipsed conformation has the maximum torsional strain.

Conformational isomers are different arrangements of atoms in space due to rotation around a single bond. However, it is not possible to separate and isolate different conformational isomers of ethane.
Rotation around a carbon-carbon single bond is almost free for all practical purposes, with an energy barrier of 12.5 kJ mol–1. This energy barrier can be overcome through intermolecular collisions.
Alkenes are unsaturated hydrocarbons containing at least one double bond, with the general formula CnH2n. They are also known as olefins.
In the IUPAC system, the longest chain of carbon atoms containing the double bond is selected, and the suffix 'ene' replaces 'ane' of alkanes.
Electrophilic reagents are compounds that are in search of electrons and can easily attack alkenes due to the presence of a weaker π-bond.
Alkenes are unstable molecules compared to alkanes due to the presence of a weaker π-bond, and they can be converted into single bond compounds by combining with electrophilic reagents.
IUPAC rules for naming alkenes, including identifying the longest continuous chain and numbering it to give the lowest possible numbers to the double bonds.
Alkenes can exhibit structural isomerism, where molecules with the same molecular formula have different structures.
Alkenes can exhibit geometrical isomerism due to restricted rotation around the double bond, resulting in cis and trans isomers.
Cis isomers have identical atoms or groups on the same side of the double bond, while trans isomers have them on opposite sides.
Alkenes can exhibit chain isomerism, where molecules with the same molecular formula have different carbon chain arrangements.
Alkenes can exhibit position isomerism, where molecules with the same molecular formula have different positions of the double bond.
Alkenes contain sigma (σ) and pi (π) bonds, with σ bonds formed by end-to-end overlap and π bonds formed by side-by-side overlap.
Geometrical isomerism occurs in alkenes with different arrangements of atoms or groups in space, leading to different properties.
Cis and trans isomers are types of geometrical isomers with different arrangements of atoms or groups in space.
Cis isomers are more polar than trans isomers due to the arrangement of atoms or groups, affecting their dipole moment.
Alkynes can be partially reduced to form alkenes with cis geometry using Lindlar's catalyst.

concepts.concept_title: Preparation of Alkenes from Alkyl Halides
concepts.description: Alkyl halides can be heated with alcoholic potash to eliminate a molecule of halogen acid and form alkenes.
Geometrical isomerism occurs in alkenes of the types XYC = CXZ and XYC = CZW.
Removal of halogen acid from a molecule, an example of β-elimination reaction.
Alkenes resemble alkanes in physical properties, with regular increase in boiling point with size.
Removal of halogen atoms from vicinal dihalides using zinc metal.
Alcohols form alkenes with elimination of water molecule in presence of acid.
Alkenes add one molecule of dihydrogen gas in presence of catalyst to form alkanes.
Halogens like bromine or chlorine add up to alkene to form vicinal dihalides.
A reaction in which an electrophile adds to an unsaturated compound, resulting in the formation of a new bond.
A rule that states the negative part of the addendum gets attached to the carbon atom with fewer hydrogen atoms.
A reaction in which halogens add to alkenes to form a cyclic halonium ion.
A reaction in which hydrogen halides add to alkenes to form alkyl halides.
A reaction in which the addition of HBr to unsymmetrical alkenes takes place contrary to the Markovnikov rule in the presence of peroxide.
The stability of carbocations increases with the number of alkyl groups attached to the positively charged carbon atom.
A mechanism in which the addition of HBr to unsymmetrical alkenes takes place through the formation of free radicals.
The peroxide effect is a phenomenon where the addition of HBr to an alkene results in an anti-Markovnikov product in the presence of peroxides.
The Markovnikov rule states that in the addition of a polar molecule to an alkene, the more electropositive part of the polar molecule adds to the carbon atom with more hydrogen atoms.
A free radical chain mechanism involves the formation of free radicals that propagate a chain reaction, resulting in the addition of a molecule to an alkene.
The addition of sulphuric acid to an alkene results in the formation of an alkyl hydrogen sulphate, following the Markovnikov rule.
Ozonolysis involves the addition of ozone to an alkene, resulting in the cleavage of the ozonide to form smaller molecules, useful in detecting the position of the double bond.
The addition of water to an alkene results in the formation of an alcohol, following the Markovnikov rule, in the presence of an acid catalyst.
Alkenes can be oxidized using potassium permanganate (Baeyer's reagent) to form vicinal glycols, with decolorization of the KMnO4 solution indicating unsaturation.
Polymerization involves the combination of large numbers of monomer molecules to form a polymer, such as polythene, under high temperature, pressure, and in the presence of a catalyst.
Alkynes are unsaturated hydrocarbons containing at least one triple bond between two carbon atoms. They have the general formula CnH2n-2.
Position isomers of alkynes differ in the position of the triple bond. For example, but-1-yne and but-2-yne are position isomers.
Chain isomers of alkynes have different carbon chain arrangements. For example, pent-1-yne and 3-methylbut-1-yne are chain isomers.
Alkynes can be named using the common system (derivatives of acetylene) or the IUPAC system.
The general formula of alkynes is CnH2n-2.
Ethyne, also known as acetylene, is the first stable member of the alkyne series. It is used for arc welding purposes.

Ethyne has a linear structure with a C≡C bond formed by head-on overlapping of two sp hybridised orbitals. Each carbon atom has two unhybridised p orbitals perpendicular to each other and the plane of the C-C sigma bond.
The C≡C bond is stronger than C=C and C-C bonds with a bond enthalpy of 823 kJ/mol. The bond length of C≡C (120 pm) is shorter than C=C (133 pm) and C-C (154 pm).

concepts.concept_title: Acidity of Hydrogen Atoms in Ethyne
concepts.description: The hydrogen atoms attached to the triply bonded carbon atoms in ethyne are acidic in nature due to the high electronegativity of the sp hybridised carbon atoms.
Ethyne can be prepared by treating calcium carbide with water or by dehydrohalogenation of vicinal dihalides.
The physical properties of alkynes follow the same trend as alkenes and alkanes, with the first three members being gases, the next eight being liquids, and so on.
