Alkynes are colourless, weakly polar, and lighter than water. They are immiscible with water but soluble in organic solvents. Their melting point, boiling point, and density increase with increase in molar mass.
Alkynes show acidic nature due to the hybridization of carbon atoms. They react with strong bases like sodium metal and sodamide to form acetylides.
Alkynes undergo addition reactions due to the presence of a triple bond. They add molecules of dihydrogen, halogen, hydrogen halides, etc.
In unsymmetrical alkynes, addition reactions take place according to Markovnikov's rule.
Alkynes undergo polymerization reactions to form high molecular weight compounds like polyacetylene.
The decolorization of bromine in carbon tetrachloride is used as a test for unsaturation.
Aromatic hydrocarbons are also known as 'arenes'. They possess pleasant odour and most of them contain a benzene ring.
Benzene ring is highly unsaturated and is the starting molecule for the preparation of derivatives of benzene, dyes, drugs and other organic compounds.
The nomenclature and isomerism of aromatic hydrocarbons have already been discussed. All six hydrogen atoms in benzene are equivalent, leading to one type of monosubstituted product.

concepts.concept_title: Ortho, Meta, and Para Disubstituted Compounds
concepts.description: When two hydrogen atoms in benzene are replaced by two similar or different monovalent atoms or groups, three different position isomers are possible: ortho (o-), meta (m-), and para (p-).
Ethyne on passing through a red hot iron tube at 873K undergoes cyclic polymerization to form benzene.
Aromatic compounds containing a benzene ring are known as benzenoids, while those not containing a benzene ring are known as non-benzenoids.
Benzene has a cyclic arrangement of six carbon atoms with alternate single and double bonds and one hydrogen atom attached to each carbon atom.

concepts.concept_title: Identity of Carbon and Hydrogen Atoms in Benzene
concepts.description: All six carbon and six hydrogen atoms of benzene are identical, which led to the proposal of Kekulé's structure.

concepts.concept_title: Oscillating Nature of Double Bonds in Benzene
concepts.description: Kekulé suggested that the double bonds in benzene oscillate, which explains the formation of only one ortho disubstituted product.

concepts.concept_title: Unhybridised p Orbital and π Bond Formation in Benzene
concepts.description: The unhybridised p orbital of carbon atoms in benzene form a π bond by lateral overlap, leading to the stability of the molecule.
Kekulenes are compounds having polybenzenoid structures, named after Kekulé's centenary celebrations.
Benzene is a hybrid of various resonating structures, with two main contributing structures given by Kekulé. The hybrid structure is represented by a circle or dotted circle in the hexagon.

All six carbon atoms in benzene are sp2 hybridized, with two sp2 hybrid orbitals overlapping to form six C—C sigma bonds and one sp2 hybrid orbital overlapping with s orbital of hydrogen to form C—H sigma bonds.
The six π electrons in benzene are delocalised and can move freely about the six carbon nuclei, making benzene more stable than hypothetical cyclohexatriene.
A compound is aromatic if it has (4n + 2) π electrons in the ring, where n is an integer (n = 0, 1, 2, . . .).
Benzene is a planar molecule, as revealed by X-ray diffraction data.
Aromaticity refers to the characteristics of ring systems that possess a planar, ring-shaped arrangement of atoms with alternating double and single bonds, exhibiting unusual stability.
Electrophilic substitution reactions involve the replacement of a substituent in an aromatic ring with an electrophile, resulting in the formation of a new compound.
Nitration of benzene involves the introduction of a nitro group into the benzene ring using a mixture of concentrated nitric acid and sulfuric acid.
Halogenation of arenes involves the reaction of arenes with halogens in the presence of a Lewis acid to form haloarenes.
Sulphonation of benzene involves the replacement of a hydrogen atom by a sulphonic acid group using fuming sulfuric acid.
Friedel-Crafts alkylation involves the reaction of benzene with an alkyl halide in the presence of anhydrous aluminum chloride to form alkylbenzene.
Friedel-Crafts acylation involves the reaction of benzene with an acyl halide or acid anhydride in the presence of Lewis acids to form acylbenzene.

The generation of electrophile involves the formation of a highly reactive species that attacks the benzene ring. This can be achieved through the use of Lewis acids like anhydrous AlCl3, which helps in generating the electrophile.

The arenium ion, also known as the σ-complex, is formed when the electrophile attacks the benzene ring. This results in one of the carbon atoms becoming sp3 hybridized, leading to a loss of aromatic character.
To restore the aromatic character, the σ-complex releases a proton from the sp3 hybridized carbon. This can occur with the help of a base like [AlCl4]- or [HSO4]-.

The directive influence of substituents refers to the ability of existing groups on the benzene ring to direct incoming groups to specific positions. Substituents can be ortho/para directors or meta directors.
Groups that direct incoming groups to the ortho and para positions are called ortho/para directing groups. Examples include the phenolic (-OH) group.
The mechanism of electrophilic substitution involves three steps: generation of the electrophile, formation of the arenium ion, and removal of a proton.
The sigma complex, also known as the arenium ion, is a intermediate formed during electrophilic substitution reactions. It has a loss of aromatic character due to the sp3 hybridized carbon.
Under ultra-violet light, three chlorine molecules add to benzene to produce benzene hexachloride, C6H6Cl6, also called gammaxane.
Groups like –OH, –NH2, –NHR, –NHCOCH3, –OCH3, –CH3, –C2H5 increase electron density on benzene ring, making it more reactive towards electrophilic substitution.
Groups like –NO2, –CN, –CHO, –COR, –COOH, –COOR, –SO3H decrease electron density on benzene ring, making it less reactive towards electrophilic substitution.

concepts.concept_title: Directing Effects of Substituents on Benzene Ring
concepts.description: Activating groups direct incoming electrophiles to ortho and para positions, while deactivating groups direct to meta position.

concepts.concept_title: Carcinogenicity and Toxicity of Polynuclear Hydrocarbons
concepts.description: Polynuclear hydrocarbons with more than two benzene rings fused together are toxic and carcinogenic.
Benzene burns with a sooty flame producing CO and H2O.
Hydrocarbons are classified into open chain saturated (alkanes), unsaturated (alkenes and alkynes), cyclic (alicyclic), and aromatic hydrocarbons based on their structure.
Alkanes undergo free radical substitution reactions, which involve the replacement of a hydrogen atom by a substituent.

concepts.concept_title: Addition Reactions in Alkenes and Alkynes
concepts.description: Alkenes and alkynes undergo addition reactions, which are mainly electrophilic additions, resulting in the addition of a molecule across a multiple bond.
Aromatic hydrocarbons undergo electrophilic substitution reactions, where an electrophile replaces a substituent on the aromatic ring.
Alkanes exhibit conformational isomerism due to free rotation along the C–C sigma bonds, resulting in staggered and eclipsed conformations.
Alkenes exhibit geometrical (cis-trans) isomerism due to restricted rotation around the carbon–carbon double bond.
Aromaticity is a property of compounds having a specific electronic structure characterized by Hückel's (4n+2)π electron rule.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Substituents on Aromatic Ring Reactivity
concepts.description: The nature of groups or substituents attached to the benzene ring affects its reactivity towards further electrophilic substitution and orientation of the incoming group.
Ozonolysis is a reaction of alkenes with ozone, resulting in the cleavage of the double bond and formation of carbonyl compounds.
Combustion reactions involve the burning of hydrocarbons in the presence of oxygen to produce carbon dioxide and water. These reactions are highly exothermic.
Cis and trans isomerism occurs in alkenes due to restricted rotation around the double bond. Cis isomers have similar groups on the same side, while trans isomers have them on opposite sides.
Benzene is extraordinarily stable due to its aromatic nature, which arises from the delocalization of electrons in its planar, ring-shaped structure.
For a system to be aromatic, it must be planar, have a continuous ring of p-orbitals, and contain (4n+2) π electrons.
Benzene undergoes electrophilic substitution reactions easily due to its high electron density, while nucleophilic substitutions are difficult.

concepts.concept_title: Classification of Carbon Atoms in Alkanes
concepts.description: Carbon atoms in alkanes can be classified into primary (1°), secondary (2°), and tertiary (3°) based on the number of carbon atoms bonded to them.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Branching on Boiling Point of Alkanes
concepts.description: Branching in alkanes decreases their boiling point due to reduced surface area and weaker intermolecular forces.
The addition of HBr to alkenes can occur through different mechanisms, leading to different products, depending on the presence of peroxides.
Ozonolysis is a reaction of alkenes with ozone, leading to the cleavage of the double bond and formation of carbonyl compounds.
The acidity of hydrocarbons depends on the stability of the conjugate base and the type of hybridization of the carbon atom.
Various compounds can be converted to benzene through different reactions, such as cyclization and dehydrogenation.

concepts.concept_title: Relative Reactivity of Compounds towards Electrophiles
concepts.description: The relative reactivity of compounds towards electrophiles depends on the substituents present and their electronic effects.
The Wurtz reaction is a coupling reaction of alkyl halides with sodium, leading to the formation of alkanes.
A solution is a homogeneous mixture of two or more components. It has uniform composition and properties throughout.
Solutions can be classified based on the state of solute and solvent. Common types include gaseous, liquid, and solid solutions.
The component present in the largest quantity is called the solvent. Other components are called solutes.
A solution consisting of two components is called a binary solution.

concepts.concept_title: Classification of Solutions Based on States of Components
concepts.description: Solutions can be classified into gaseous, liquid, and solid solutions based on the states of solute and solvent.
Mass percentage of a component in a solution is defined as the mass of the component in the solution divided by the total mass of the solution, multiplied by 100.
Volume percentage of a component in a solution is defined as the volume of the component divided by the total volume of the solution, multiplied by 100.
Mass by volume percentage is the mass of solute dissolved in 100 mL of the solution.
Parts per million is defined as the number of parts of the component divided by the total number of parts of all components of the solution, multiplied by 10^6.
Mole fraction of a component is defined as the number of moles of the component divided by the total number of moles of all components in the solution.

Mole fraction is the ratio of the number of moles of a component to the total number of moles of all components in a solution. It is a dimensionless quantity and can be used to describe the composition of a solution.

concepts.concept_title: Mole Fraction of a Binary Mixture
concepts.description: For a binary mixture, the mole fraction of one component is the number of moles of that component divided by the sum of the number of moles of both components.
The sum of the mole fractions of all components in a solution is equal to 1.
Molarity is defined as the number of moles of solute dissolved in one liter of solution. It is expressed in units of mol L-1 or M.
Molality is defined as the number of moles of solute per kilogram of solvent. It is expressed in units of mol kg-1 or m.
Molarity is a function of temperature, while mass percent, ppm, mole fraction, and molality are independent of temperature.
Mass percentage of a component in a solution is the ratio of the mass of that component to the total mass of the solution, multiplied by 100.
Mole fraction of a component in a solution is the ratio of the number of moles of that component to the total number of moles in the solution.
Molality of a solution is the number of moles of solute per kilogram of solvent.
Molarity of a solution is the number of moles of solute per liter of solution.
Solubility of a substance is its maximum amount that can be dissolved in a specified amount of solvent at a specified temperature.
A solute dissolves in a solvent if the intermolecular interactions are similar in the two, i.e., polar solutes dissolve in polar solvents and non-polar solutes in non-polar solvents.
A saturated solution is one in which no more solute can be dissolved at the same temperature and pressure, while an unsaturated solution is one in which more solute can be dissolved.
A state of dynamic equilibrium is reached when the rate of dissolution of solute equals the rate of crystallization.
Solubility is the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve in a given amount of solvent at a particular temperature. It depends on the nature of the substances, temperature, and pressure.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Temperature on Solubility of Solids
concepts.description: The solubility of a solid in a liquid is affected by temperature changes. If the dissolution process is endothermic, solubility increases with temperature, and if it's exothermic, solubility decreases.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Pressure on Solubility of Solids
concepts.description: Pressure does not have a significant effect on the solubility of solids in liquids, as solids and liquids are highly incompressible.
The solubility of gases in liquids is affected by pressure and temperature. Solubility increases with an increase in pressure.
At a constant temperature, the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas present above the surface of the liquid or solution.

Henry's Law states that the partial pressure of a gas in vapor phase is proportional to its mole fraction in the solution. Mathematically, it is expressed as p = Kx, where p is the partial pressure, x is the mole fraction, and K is the Henry's Law constant.

The Henry's Law constant (K) is a measure of the solubility of a gas in a liquid. It varies for different gases at the same temperature and is a function of the nature of the gas. A higher value of K indicates lower solubility of the gas in the liquid.
The solubility of gases in liquids is affected by temperature and pressure. Generally, the solubility of gases increases with decrease in temperature and increases with increase in pressure.

Henry's Law has several applications in industry and explains some biological phenomena, such as increasing the solubility of CO2 in soft drinks and soda water by sealing the bottle under high pressure, and scuba divers coping with high concentrations of dissolved gases.
The solubility of gases in liquids is affected by pressure and temperature. Increased pressure increases solubility, while increased temperature decreases solubility.
Bends occur when rapid decrease in pressure leads to formation of gas bubbles in blood, while anoxia occurs at high altitudes due to low oxygen levels.
The solubility of gases in liquids decreases with increase in temperature, as dissolution is an exothermic process.
Henry's Law states that the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas above the liquid.
The vapour pressure of a liquid solution is affected by the presence of solutes, and can be described by Raoult's Law.
Raoult's law states that for a solution of volatile liquids, the partial vapour pressure of each component is directly proportional to its mole fraction present in the solution.
Dalton's law states that the total pressure over a solution phase is the sum of the partial pressures of the components of the solution.

concepts.concept_title: Vapour Pressure and Mole Fraction Relationship
concepts.description: The partial vapour pressure of a component is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the solution, given by p = p0 * x.

concepts.concept_title: Total Vapour Pressure over a Solution
concepts.description: The total vapour pressure over a solution is the sum of the partial vapour pressures of the components, given by p_total = p1 + p2.

concepts.concept_title: Linear Relationship between Vapour Pressure and Mole Fraction
concepts.description: The vapour pressure of a component and the total vapour pressure over a solution vary linearly with the mole fraction of the component.
The composition of the vapour phase in equilibrium with a solution is determined by the partial pressures of the components.

Raoult's law states that the vapor pressure of a solution is directly proportional to the mole fraction of the solvent. Mathematically, it can be expressed as p = p0x, where p is the vapor pressure of the solution, p0 is the vapor pressure of the pure solvent, and x is the mole fraction of the solvent.
Ideal solutions are those that obey Raoult's law over the entire range of concentrations. They have two important properties: the enthalpy of mixing is zero, and the volume of mixing is also zero.
Non-ideal solutions do not obey Raoult's law over the entire range of concentrations. They have non-zero enthalpy and volume of mixing.

The vapor pressure of a solution is the pressure exerted by the vapor of the solution in equilibrium with the solution. It depends on the mole fraction of the components and their vapor pressures in the pure state.

The mole fractions of the components in the vapor phase can be calculated using the relation y = p/p, where y is the mole fraction of the component in the vapor phase, p is the partial pressure of the component, and p is the total vapor pressure of the solution.
A solution that obeys Raoult's law over the entire range of concentration. The intermolecular attractive forces between A-A, B-B, and A-B are nearly equal.
A solution that does not obey Raoult's law over the entire range of concentration. The vapour pressure of such a solution is either higher or lower than predicted by Raoult's law.
The vapour pressure of a solution is higher than predicted by Raoult's law. This occurs when A-B interactions are weaker than A-A or B-B interactions.
The vapour pressure of a solution is lower than predicted by Raoult's law. This occurs when A-B interactions are stronger than A-A or B-B interactions.
The nature of interactions between molecules, such as A-A, B-B, and A-B, affects the behaviour of a solution.
Hydrogen bonding between molecules can affect the interactions and behaviour of a solution.
Azeotropes are binary mixtures having the same composition in liquid and vapour phase and boil at a constant temperature.
Solutions showing large positive deviation from Raoult's law form minimum boiling azeotrope at a specific composition.
Solutions showing large negative deviation from Raoult's law form maximum boiling azeotrope at a specific composition.
Raoult's law states that the vapour pressure of a solution is directly proportional to the mole fraction of the solvent.
When the vapour pressure of a solution is less than the predicted vapour pressure by Raoult's law, it shows negative deviation.
When the vapour pressure of a solution is more than the predicted vapour pressure by Raoult's law, it shows positive deviation.
Colligative properties are properties of solutions that depend on the number of solute particles in a solution.
The relative lowering of vapour pressure is the ratio of the lowering of vapour pressure to the vapour pressure of the pure solvent.
Raoult's law states that the vapour pressure of a solution is directly proportional to the mole fraction of the solvent. Mathematically, it is expressed as p = x p 0.
The reduction in vapour pressure of a solvent due to the presence of a non-volatile solute is given by Dp = p 0 - p = p 0 (1 - x ).
The relative lowering of vapour pressure is given by Dp/p 0 = x , which is equal to the mole fraction of the solute.
The molar mass of a solute can be determined using the equation p 0 - p / p 0 = n / n , where n and n are the number of moles of solute and solvent.
The boiling point of a solution is higher than that of the pure solvent due to the decrease in vapour pressure. The vapour pressure of a liquid increases with temperature.
The elevation of boiling point is the increase in boiling point of a solution compared to the pure solvent. It depends on the number of solute molecules rather than their nature.
The boiling point elevation constant (Kb) is a constant of proportionality that relates the elevation of boiling point to the molal concentration of the solute. Its unit is K kg mol-1.

concepts.concept_title: Molality and Elevation of Boiling Point
concepts.description: The elevation of boiling point is directly proportional to the molal concentration of the solute in a solution. This relationship is expressed as ΔTb = Kb × m.

concepts.concept_title: Molar Mass Determination using Elevation of Boiling Point
concepts.description: The molar mass of a solute can be determined by measuring the elevation of boiling point of a solution. This is done using the expression M2 = Kb × 1000 × w2 / (ΔTb × w1).
The lowering of vapour pressure of a solution causes a lowering of the freezing point compared to that of the pure solvent. The decrease in freezing point is known as depression in freezing point.

concepts.concept_title: Molality and Depression of Freezing Point
concepts.description: Depression of freezing point (DT) for dilute solution (ideal solution) is directly proportional to molality, m of the solution. Thus, DT = K m, where K is Freezing Point Depression Constant or Molal Depression Constant.
The proportionality constant, K, which depends on the nature of the solvent is known as Freezing Point Depression Constant or Molal Depression Constant. The unit of K is K kg mol-1.

concepts.concept_title: Molar Mass Determination Using Freezing Point Depression
concepts.description: The molar mass of the solute can be determined using the equation: M2 = Kf × w2 × 1000 / (ΔTf × w1), where w1 and w2 are the masses of solvent and solute respectively.

concepts.concept_title: Relation Between Kf and Solvent Properties
concepts.description: The value of Kf can be ascertained from the relation: Kf = R × M1 × Tf^2 / (1000 × ΔHfus), where R is the gas constant, M1 is the molar mass of the solvent, Tf is the freezing point of the pure solvent, and ΔHfus is the enthalpy of fusion.
Freezing point depression is the decrease in freezing point of a solvent when a solute is added to it. It is a colligative property, which depends on the molality of the solution.
Molality is the number of moles of solute per kilogram of solvent. It is expressed in units of mol kg-1.
The freezing point depression constant is a measure of the freezing point depression of a solvent per mole of solute per kilogram of solvent. It is expressed in units of K kg mol-1.
Osmosis is the flow of solvent molecules through a semipermeable membrane from a region of higher solvent concentration to a region of lower solvent concentration.

Semipermeable membranes are membranes that allow certain molecules to pass through while restricting others. They have submicroscopic holes or pores that permit the passage of small solvent molecules.
Osmotic pressure is the pressure exerted by a solution on a semipermeable membrane due to the flow of solvent molecules through it.
The molar mass of a solute can be determined by measuring the freezing point depression or osmotic pressure of a solution.
The excess pressure required to prevent osmosis in a solution. It is a colligative property that depends on the number of solute molecules.
The osmotic pressure of a solution is given by the equation P = C R T, where P is the osmotic pressure, C is the molarity, R is the gas constant, and T is the temperature.
The osmotic pressure method can be used to determine the molar mass of a solute using the equation M = w R T / (P V).
Two solutions having the same osmotic pressure at a given temperature are called isotonic solutions.
Solutions with higher or lower osmotic pressure than a reference solution are called hypertonic or hypotonic solutions, respectively.
Osmosis is the movement of water molecules from a region of high concentration to a region of low concentration through a semi-permeable membrane.
A solution with a salt concentration less than 0.9% (mass/volume) is called hypotonic. In this solution, water flows into cells, causing them to swell.
Though not explicitly defined, an isotonic solution has a concentration of 0.9% (mass/volume) and does not cause cells to swell or shrink.
Osmotic pressure is the pressure required to prevent osmosis. It is a colligative property and depends on the concentration of the solution.

concepts.concept_title: Molar Mass Calculation using Osmotic Pressure
concepts.description: The molar mass of a solute can be calculated using the osmotic pressure equation: π = cRT, where c is the concentration of the solution.
Reverse osmosis is the process of applying pressure to a solution to remove pure solvent through a semi-permeable membrane.
The vapour pressure of a solvent decreases when a non-volatile solute is added to it. This decrease in vapour pressure is directly proportional to the mole fraction of the solute.
The relative lowering of vapour pressure is the ratio of the decrease in vapour pressure to the vapour pressure of the pure solvent.
The boiling point of a solvent increases when a non-volatile solute is added to it. This elevation in boiling point is directly proportional to the molality of the solute.
The freezing point of a solvent decreases when a non-volatile solute is added to it. This depression in freezing point is directly proportional to the molality of the solute.
The osmotic pressure of a solution is the pressure exerted by the solution to prevent the flow of solvent molecules into the solution through a semipermeable membrane.

The van't Hoff factor is a measure of the extent of dissociation or association of a solute in a solution. It is defined as the ratio of the actual number of particles in a solution to the number of particles if no dissociation or association occurred.
The molar mass of a solute that is either higher or lower than the expected value due to association or dissociation of the solute molecules.

The van't Hoff factor is a measure of the extent to which a solute dissociates or associates in a solution. It is defined as the ratio of the actual number of particles in a solution to the number of particles if the solute did not dissociate or associate.

Colligative properties are properties of a solution that depend on the concentration of the solute particles in a solution, such as relative lowering of vapor pressure, elevation of boiling point, depression of freezing point, and osmotic pressure.

The relative lowering of vapor pressure is the ratio of the decrease in vapor pressure to the original vapor pressure of the solvent. It is a colligative property that depends on the concentration of the solute particles.
The elevation of boiling point is the increase in boiling point of a solvent when a solute is added to it. It is a colligative property that depends on the concentration of the solute particles.
The depression of freezing point is the decrease in freezing point of a solvent when a solute is added to it. It is a colligative property that depends on the concentration of the solute particles.
The osmotic pressure is the pressure exerted by a solution on a semipermeable membrane. It is a colligative property that depends on the concentration of the solute particles.
The degree of association is the extent to which a solute associates in a solution. It is a measure of the fraction of solute particles that associate to form larger particles.
The dissociation constant is a measure of the extent to which a solute dissociates in a solution. It is a measure of the equilibrium between the dissociated and undissociated forms of the solute.

The van't Hoff factor (i) is the ratio of the actual number of particles in a solution to the number of particles if the solute did not dissociate or associate. It is used to express the extent of dissociation or association of a solute in a solution.
The degree of dissociation (x) is the fraction of a solute that dissociates into particles in a solution. It can be calculated using the van't Hoff factor.

Colligative properties are properties of solutions that depend on the number of solute particles and are independent of their chemical identity. Examples include lowering of vapour pressure, elevation of boiling point, depression of freezing point, and osmotic pressure.
Raoult's law states that the relative lowering of vapour pressure of a solvent over a solution is equal to the mole fraction of a non-volatile solute present in the solution.
Henry's law states that the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas.
Ideal solutions are solutions that obey Raoult's law over the entire range of concentration.
Azeotropes are mixtures of liquids that boil at a constant temperature and have a constant composition in the vapour phase.

Osmosis is the movement of solvent molecules from a region of high concentration to a region of low concentration through a semipermeable membrane. Osmotic pressure is the pressure required to prevent osmosis.
A solution is a homogeneous mixture of two or more substances. There are several types of solutions, including liquid solutions, solid solutions, and gaseous solutions.
Solutions can be classified into different types based on the state of the solute and solvent. Examples include aqueous solutions, solid solutions, and gaseous solutions.
Mole fraction is defined as the ratio of the number of moles of a component to the total number of moles in a solution.
Molality is defined as the number of moles of solute per kilogram of solvent.
Molarity is defined as the number of moles of solute per liter of solution.
Mass percentage is defined as the mass of a component per 100 grams of solution.
Henry's law states that the partial pressure of a gas over a solution is proportional to its mole fraction.
Raoult's law states that the vapor pressure of a solution is proportional to the mole fraction of the solvent.
Positive deviations occur when the vapor pressure of a solution is greater than expected, while negative deviations occur when it is less than expected.
Molecular interactions play a crucial role in determining the properties of a solution.
The solubility of gases in liquids decreases with increasing temperature.

Raoult's law states that the vapor pressure of a solution is directly proportional to the mole fraction of the solvent. It is expressed as p = p°x, where p is the vapor pressure of the solution, p° is the vapor pressure of the pure solvent, and x is the mole fraction of the solvent.
The vapour pressure of a solution is the pressure exerted by the vapour of the solution at equilibrium. It is an important colligative property of solutions.
The molar mass of a solute can be determined using the vapour pressure of a solution. This involves applying Raoult's law and using the given vapour pressure values.

The freezing point depression is a colligative property that depends on the molality of the solution. It is expressed as ΔT = Kf × m, where ΔT is the freezing point depression, Kf is the freezing point depression constant, and m is the molality of the solution.

The osmotic pressure of a solution is a colligative property that depends on the concentration of the solution. It is expressed as π = cRT, where π is the osmotic pressure, c is the concentration of the solution, R is the gas constant, and T is the temperature.

The van't Hoff factor is a measure of the extent to which a solute dissociates in a solution. It is expressed as i = ΔT(observed) / ΔT(calculated), where i is the van't Hoff factor, ΔT(observed) is the observed freezing point depression, and ΔT(calculated) is the calculated freezing point depression.

The solubility product is a measure of the solubility of a sparingly soluble salt. It is expressed as Ksp = [M+][X-], where Ksp is the solubility product, [M+] is the concentration of the cation, and [X-] is the concentration of the anion.

Molality and molarity are two different ways to express the concentration of a solution. Molality is expressed as moles of solute per kilogram of solvent, while molarity is expressed as moles of solute per liter of solution.
Intermolecular interactions are the attractive and repulsive forces between molecules. They play a crucial role in determining the physical properties of a substance.
Vapour pressure of a solution is the pressure exerted by the vapour of a solution at equilibrium. It depends on the mole fraction of the components.
Raoult's Law states that the vapour pressure of a solution is directly proportional to the mole fraction of the solvent. Mathematically, p = p°x
Ideal solutions are those that obey Raoult's Law over the entire range of composition. They have zero enthalpy of mixing and zero volume of mixing.
Henry's Law states that the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas above the liquid. Mathematically, p = k × x
Osmotic pressure is the pressure exerted by a solution to prevent the flow of solvent molecules into the solution through a semipermeable membrane.
Van 't Hoff factor is the ratio of the actual osmotic pressure of a solution to the calculated osmotic pressure assuming no dissociation or association.
An electrochemical cell is a device that converts chemical energy into electrical energy or vice versa. It consists of two electrodes and an electrolyte.
A galvanic cell is an electrochemical cell that converts chemical energy into electrical energy. It consists of two half-cells with different electrodes and electrolytes.
An electrolytic cell is an electrochemical cell that uses electrical energy to drive a non-spontaneous chemical reaction.
The Nernst equation is used to calculate the emf of a galvanic cell under non-standard conditions.
The standard potential of a cell is the potential difference between the cell's electrodes when all concentrations are unity.
Conductivity is the ability of a solution to conduct electricity. Molar conductivity is the conductivity of a solution containing one mole of an electrolyte.
Kohlrausch law states that the molar conductivity of an electrolyte at infinite dilution is the sum of the molar conductivities of its individual ions.
The quantitative aspects of electrolysis involve the calculation of the amount of substance deposited or liberated during electrolysis.
Primary batteries are non-rechargeable batteries, while secondary batteries are rechargeable batteries.
Fuel cells are devices that convert chemical energy into electrical energy through a continuous flow of fuel and oxidant.
Corrosion is an electrochemical process that involves the oxidation of a metal in the presence of an electrolyte.
A galvanic cell is an electrochemical cell that converts chemical energy of a spontaneous redox reaction into electrical energy.
A spontaneous redox reaction is a reaction that occurs on its own without any external influence.
A half-cell or redox couple is a portion of the cell where a half-reaction occurs.
The cell potential is the potential difference between the two half-cells of a galvanic cell.
The equilibrium potential is the potential at which the reaction stops and no current flows through the cell.
An electrolytic cell is a device that uses electrical energy to carry non-spontaneous chemical reactions.

The potential difference developed between the electrode and the electrolyte is called electrode potential. It arises due to the tendency of metal ions to deposit on the metal electrode and metal atoms to go into the solution as ions.

The electrode potential when the concentrations of all species involved in a half-cell are unity is called standard electrode potential. It is a measure of the tendency of a half-cell to undergo reduction or oxidation.
In a galvanic cell, the half-cell where oxidation occurs is called the anode (negative potential) and the half-cell where reduction occurs is called the cathode (positive potential).

The potential difference between the two electrodes in a galvanic cell is called cell potential. It is equal to the standard electrode potential of the cathode minus the standard electrode potential of the anode.
The standard hydrogen electrode is a reference half-cell with a standard electrode potential of zero. It is used to measure the standard electrode potentials of other half-cells.
Standard electrode potential is the potential difference between a metal and its ion in a solution. A negative value indicates that hydrogen ions can oxidise the metal.
A species with a high standard electrode potential is a strong oxidising agent, while a species with a low standard electrode potential is a strong reducing agent.
Half-cell reactions involve oxidation or reduction at an electrode. The overall reaction of a cell is the sum of two half-cell reactions.
The EMF of a cell is the difference between the standard electrode potentials of the two half-cells.
Inert electrodes, such as platinum or gold, do not participate in the reaction but provide a surface for oxidation or reduction.
A positive standard electrode potential indicates that the reduced form is more stable than hydrogen gas.

The Nernst equation relates the electrode potential of a cell to the standard electrode potential and the concentrations of the ions involved. It is given by: E = Eo - (RT/nF) * ln(Q), where Q is the reaction quotient.

The standard electrode potential is the electrode potential measured under standard conditions, i.e., with all ions at unit concentration. It is a measure of the tendency of a redox couple to act as a reducing or oxidizing agent.

The electrode potential depends on the concentration of the ions involved in the electrode reaction. The Nernst equation shows that the electrode potential changes with the logarithm of the concentration of the ions.
The cell potential is the difference between the electrode potentials of the cathode and anode. It is a measure of the tendency of a cell to undergo a redox reaction.
A negative Eo value indicates that the redox couple is a stronger reducing agent than the H+/H couple, while a positive Eo value indicates that it is a weaker reducing agent.

The Nernst equation relates the electrode potential of a cell to the standard electrode potential and the concentrations of the ions involved. It is used to calculate the cell potential under non-standard conditions.

concepts.concept_title: Dependence of Cell Potential on Concentration
concepts.description: The cell potential depends on the concentrations of both the oxidized and reduced species. It increases with an increase in the concentration of the oxidized species and decreases with an increase in the concentration of the reduced species.

At equilibrium, the Nernst equation can be used to relate the standard cell potential to the equilibrium constant of the reaction. This allows for the calculation of equilibrium constants from standard cell potentials.
The standard cell potential (Eo) is related to the equilibrium constant (Kc) by the equation: Eo = (RT/nF) * ln(Kc). This equation allows for the calculation of Kc from Eo.
Electrical work is related to the cell potential and the amount of charge transferred. It is an important concept in electrochemistry.

concepts.concept_title: Gibbs Free Energy and Cell Potential
concepts.description: The Gibbs free energy change (ΔG) is related to the cell potential (E) by the equation ΔG = -nFE, where n is the number of electrons transferred and F is the Faraday constant.

concepts.concept_title: Standard Gibbs Free Energy and Standard Cell Potential
concepts.description: The standard Gibbs free energy change (ΔG°) is related to the standard cell potential (E°) by the equation ΔG° = -nFE°.

concepts.concept_title: Equilibrium Constant and Gibbs Free Energy
concepts.description: The equilibrium constant (K) can be calculated from the standard Gibbs free energy change (ΔG°) using the equation ΔG° = -RT ln K.
The electrical resistance (R) of an object is directly proportional to its length and inversely proportional to its area of cross-section.

concepts.concept_title: Cell Reaction and Number of Electrons Transferred
concepts.description: The number of electrons transferred (n) in a cell reaction affects the Gibbs free energy change and cell potential.
Resistivity is a measure of a substance's resistance when it is one metre long and its area of cross-section is one m2. It is denoted by the symbol ρ (rho) and its SI units are ohm metre (W m).
Conductance is the inverse of resistance, denoted by the symbol G. It is a measure of a material's ability to conduct electricity. The SI unit of conductance is siemens (S).
Conductivity is the inverse of resistivity, denoted by the symbol κ (kappa). It is a measure of a material's ability to conduct electricity. The SI units of conductivity are S m-1.

Materials can be classified into conductors, insulators, and semiconductors based on their conductivity. Conductors have high conductivity, insulators have low conductivity, and semiconductors have conductivity between conductors and insulators.

Superconductors are materials that have zero resistivity or infinite conductivity. They can be metals, alloys, ceramic materials, or mixed oxides that exhibit superconductivity at low or high temperatures.
Electrical conductance through metals is called metallic or electronic conductance, which is due to the movement of electrons.
The conductivity of metals depends on the nature and structure of the metal, the number of valence electrons per atom, and temperature.
Electronically conducting polymers are organic polymers with properties like metals, discovered by MacDiarmid, Heeger, and Shirakawa in 1977.
The conductance of electricity by ions present in solutions is called electrolytic or ionic conductance.

concepts.concept_title: Factors Affecting Conductivity of Electrolytic Solutions
concepts.description: The conductivity of electrolytic solutions depends on the nature of the electrolyte, size of ions, solvation, solvent viscosity, concentration, and temperature.

concepts.concept_title: Measurement of Conductivity of Ionic Solutions
concepts.description: Accurate measurement of conductivity of ionic solutions requires using an alternating current source and a specially designed conductivity cell.
Wheatstone bridge is used for measuring unknown resistance, but it needs modification for measuring conductivity of ionic solutions.

The cell constant is a measure of the conductivity of a solution, calculated as the ratio of the distance between the electrodes to their area of cross-section. It depends on the geometry of the electrodes and can be determined by measuring the resistance of a solution with known conductivity.

The conductivity of solutions depends on the charge, size, and concentration of ions, as well as their mobility under a potential gradient. Different electrolytes have different conductivities due to these factors.

The cell constant can be measured by determining the resistance of a solution with known conductivity, typically using KCl solutions. This allows for the calculation of the cell constant using the equation G* = R κ.

Molar conductivity is a measure of the conductivity of a solution, taking into account the concentration of the electrolyte. It is denoted by the symbol Λ and is an important concept in electrochemistry.

The Wheatstone bridge is an electrical circuit used to measure the resistance of a solution. It consists of two resistances, a variable resistance, and the conductivity cell with the unknown resistance.
Molar conductivity is the conductivity of a solution containing one mole of an electrolyte. It is related to the conductivity of the solution by the equation: Λm = κ / c
The units of molar conductivity are S m^2 mol^-1 or S cm^2 mol^-1. The conversion factor is 1 S m^2 mol^-1 = 10^4 S cm^2 mol^-1
The cell constant is given by the equation: Cell constant = G* = conductivity × resistance. It is used to calculate the conductivity of a solution

Conductivity always decreases with decrease in concentration both, for weak and strong electrolytes. This can be explained by the fact that the number of ions per unit volume that carry the current in a solution decreases on dilution
Resistivity is the reciprocal of conductivity. It can be calculated using the equation: ρ = R × A / l
Molar conductivity is the conductance of a solution containing one mole of an electrolyte kept between two electrodes with a unit area of cross-section and a unit distance apart.

concepts.concept_title: Variation of Molar Conductivity with Concentration
concepts.description: Molar conductivity increases with a decrease in concentration. The total volume of the solution containing one mole of electrolyte increases, compensating for the decrease in conductivity.
When the concentration approaches zero, the molar conductivity is known as limiting molar conductivity, represented by the symbol Λ°m.
For strong electrolytes, Λm increases slowly with dilution and can be represented by the equation: Λm = Λ°m - Ac1/2.
The value of the constant 'A' depends on the type of electrolyte, i.e., the charges on the cation and anion produced on dissociation.

concepts.concept_title: Kohlrausch Law of Independent Migration of Ions
concepts.description: Kohlrausch law states that limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte is the sum of individual contributions of the anion and cation. It allows calculation of limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte from its ions.
Limiting molar conductivity is the conductivity of a solution containing one mole of an electrolyte when the concentration of the electrolyte is zero. It is denoted by L°m.
For strong electrolytes, L°m = l°+ + l°-, where l°+ and l°- are limiting molar conductivities of the cation and anion respectively.

concepts.concept_title: Degree of Dissociation of Weak Electrolytes
concepts.description: The degree of dissociation (a) of a weak electrolyte can be approximated as the ratio of molar conductivity Lm at concentration c to limiting molar conductivity L°m.

concepts.concept_title: Kohlrausch Law Application to Weak Electrolytes
concepts.description: Kohlrausch law can be used to calculate the limiting molar conductivity of weak electrolytes, which is essential for determining their degree of dissociation and other properties.
The conductivity of strong electrolytes decreases with increasing concentration, while that of weak electrolytes increases steeply on dilution.
Kohlrausch law states that the molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be calculated from the molar conductivity of its individual ions.
Molar conductivity can be calculated using the formula: Λm = λ+ + λ-, where λ+ and λ- are the molar conductivity of the cation and anion respectively.
Kohlrausch law can be used to calculate the molar conductivity of weak electrolytes and to determine the degree of dissociation of weak electrolytes.
The dissociation constant of a weak electrolyte can be calculated using the formula: K = cα^2 / (1-α), where c is the concentration and α is the degree of dissociation.
The conductivity of a solution decreases with dilution due to the decrease in the number of ions per unit volume.
An electrolytic cell is an electrochemical cell that uses an external source of voltage to bring about a chemical reaction.
Electrolysis is a process where an electric current is used to drive a chemical reaction. Electrochemical reduction is used to produce metals like Na, Mg, Al, etc. on a large scale.

Faraday's laws describe the quantitative aspects of electrolysis. The first law states that the amount of chemical reaction is proportional to the quantity of electricity passed. The second law states that the amounts of different substances liberated are proportional to their chemical equivalent weights.
The amount of chemical reaction which occurs at any electrode during electrolysis is proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte.
The amounts of different substances liberated by the same quantity of electricity passing through the electrolytic solution are proportional to their chemical equivalent weights.
The quantity of electricity (Q) passed is given by Q = It, where I is the current in amperes and t is the time in seconds.
The charge on one mole of electrons is equal to 96487 C mol–1, which is called Faraday (F). For approximate calculations, 1F ≃ 96500 C mol–1.
Metals like Na, Mg, Al, etc. are produced on a large scale by electrochemical reduction of their respective cations.
Electrolysis is the process of using an electric current to drive a chemical reaction. Faraday's law relates the amount of substance deposited to the quantity of electricity passed.
Inert electrodes do not participate in the chemical reaction, while reactive electrodes participate in the electrode reaction. This affects the products of electrolysis.
The standard electrode potential determines the feasibility of a reaction. A higher positive value indicates a greater tendency to occur.
Overpotential is the extra potential required for a reaction to occur due to kinetic limitations. This affects the products of electrolysis.
At the cathode, multiple reduction reactions can occur. The reaction with the higher standard electrode potential is preferred.

concepts.concept_title: Calculation of Mass of Substance Deposited
concepts.description: The mass of substance deposited can be calculated using Faraday's law and the standard electrode potential.
The Nernst equation takes into account the concentration effects on standard electrode potentials. It replaces standard electrode potentials with electrode potentials.
During the electrolysis of sulphuric acid, possible processes occur at the anode. Two possible reactions are given with their Eo values.
The quantity of electricity in coulombs can be calculated using the current and time. A current of 0.5 ampere flows through a metallic wire for 2 hours.
The quantity of electricity needed to reduce 1 mol of Cr2O72- can be calculated using the given reaction.
Primary batteries have a limited lifespan and cannot be reused. Examples include dry cells and mercury cells.
A galvanic cell converts chemical energy into electrical energy. Batteries are basically galvanic cells.
Electrode reactions occur in batteries, such as dry cells and mercury cells. Anode and cathode reactions are given.
Understanding the reactions occurring in a galvanic cell, including anode and cathode reactions.
Primary cells are not rechargeable and have a limited lifespan.
Secondary cells can be recharged and have a longer lifespan.
A lead storage battery consists of a lead anode and a grid of lead packed with lead dioxide as cathode.
A nickel-cadmium cell has a longer life than a lead storage cell but is more expensive.
Fuel cells convert chemical energy into electricity and are highly efficient.
Fuel cells are devices that convert chemical energy into electrical energy. They have high efficiency and are pollution-free.
The electrode reactions in a fuel cell involve the oxidation of hydrogen at the anode and the reduction of oxygen at the cathode.
Corrosion is the process of oxidation of metals by loss of electrons to oxygen and formation of oxides.
Rusting of iron is an example of corrosion that occurs in the presence of water and air.
Corrosion can be considered as an electrochemical phenomenon involving oxidation and reduction reactions.

Corrosion is the oxidation of metals by atmospheric oxygen and moisture. Prevention methods include covering the surface with paint or chemicals, using inert metals, or providing a sacrificial electrode.
Rusting of iron is an example of an electrochemical cell, where iron acts as the anode and atmospheric oxygen acts as the cathode.
Fuel cells use hydrogen as a fuel and produce only water as a product. The hydrogen economy aims to use hydrogen as a renewable and non-polluting source of energy.
Hydrogen can be produced by electrolysis of water using solar energy. This process is important for the hydrogen economy.
The standard electrode potential is a measure of the tendency of a metal to lose or gain electrons. Examples include the Eo values for the cathode and overall reactions.

Electrochemical cells are of two types: galvanic cell and electrolytic cell. Galvanic cell converts chemical energy into electrical work, while electrolytic cell uses electrical energy to carry out a non-spontaneous redox reaction.
The standard electrode potential for any electrode dipping in an appropriate solution is defined with respect to standard electrode potential of hydrogen electrode taken as zero.
The concentration dependence of the potentials of the electrodes and the cells are given by Nernst equation.
Molar conductivity, Lm, is defined by = k/c where c is the concentration. Conductivity decreases but molar conductivity increases with decrease in concentration.

concepts.concept_title: Law of Independent Migration of Ions
concepts.description: Kohlrausch found that molar conductivity at infinite dilution, for an electrolyte is sum of the contribution of the molar conductivity of the ions in which it dissociates.
key_terms: law of independent migration of ions

concepts.concept_title: Gibbs Free Energy and Equilibrium Constant
concepts.description: The standard potential of the cells are related to standard Gibbs energy (DrGo = –nFE (o cell)) and equilibrium constant (DrGo = – RT ln K) of the reaction taking place in the cell.
Depicting the galvanic cell in which a specific reaction takes place, and identifying the negatively charged electrode, carriers of current, and individual reactions at each electrode.
Calculating the standard cell potentials of galvanic cell in which a specific reaction takes place.
The Nernst equation is used to calculate the emf of a cell at a given temperature. It takes into account the concentrations of the ions involved in the reaction.
Standard electrode potentials are used to predict the feasibility of a reaction. They are measured at standard conditions and are used to calculate the emf of a cell.
Conductivity is the ability of a solution to conduct electricity. Molar conductivity is the conductivity of a solution containing one mole of an electrolyte.
The conductivity of a solution varies with concentration. As the concentration of an electrolyte increases, its conductivity also increases.
The cell constant is a measure of the conductivity of a cell. It is calculated using the conductivity of a solution and the resistance of the cell.
Electrolysis is the process of using electricity to drive a chemical reaction. Faraday's laws relate the amount of electricity used to the amount of substance produced.
The charge required for a reaction can be calculated using the number of moles of electrons transferred and Faraday's constant.
The products of electrolysis can be predicted using standard electrode potentials and the nature of the electrodes used.
Ksp is a measure of the solubility of a salt in water. It represents the equilibrium between a solid ionic compound and its ions in a solution. Ksp values are used to predict the solubility of salts.
The solubility of a salt can be calculated using its Ksp value. The relationship between Ksp and solubility is given by the equation Ksp = [ion concentrations]^(stoichiometric coefficients).

The common ion effect states that if a solution is at equilibrium, and one of the ions in the solution is also introduced through another compound, the equilibrium will shift to consume some of the added ion. This effect is used to predict changes in solubility.

concepts.concept_title: Gibbs Free Energy (ΔG) and Equilibrium Constant (K)
concepts.description: The Gibbs free energy change (ΔG) is related to the equilibrium constant (K) by the equation ΔG = -RT ln K. This relationship is used to predict the spontaneity of a reaction.
Chemical kinetics is the study of reaction rates and their mechanisms. It helps us understand how chemical reactions occur and the factors that affect their rates.

concepts.concept_title: Average and Instantaneous Rate of Reaction
concepts.description: The average rate of reaction is the change in concentration of reactants or products over a period of time. The instantaneous rate of reaction is the rate of reaction at a specific moment in time.
The rate of reaction can be expressed in terms of change in concentration of either reactants or products with time.
Elementary reactions occur in a single step, while complex reactions involve multiple steps.
Molecularity refers to the number of molecules involved in a reaction, while order of reaction refers to the dependence of the reaction rate on concentration.
The rate constant is a proportionality constant that relates the reaction rate to the concentration of reactants.
The rate of reaction is affected by concentration, temperature, pressure, and catalyst.
Collision theory explains how molecules collide and react to form products.
The speed of a reaction or the rate of a reaction can be defined as the change in concentration of a reactant or product in unit time.
The average rate of a reaction is represented by equations (3.1) and (3.2) and depends upon the change in concentration of reactants or products and the time taken for that change to occur.
The units of rate of a reaction are concentration time–1, such as mol L-1s–1 or atm s–1.
The rate of disappearance of a reactant and the rate of appearance of a product can be expressed as change in concentration per unit time.

The average rate of reaction is determined by dividing the change in concentration of a reactant or product by the change in time over a specific interval. It is expressed as the decrease in concentration of reactants or increase in concentration of products per unit time.

The instantaneous rate of reaction is the rate at a specific moment in time, obtained by considering the average rate over an infinitesimally small time interval (as the time interval approaches zero). It can be determined graphically by drawing a tangent to the concentration vs. time curve.

The rate of reaction can be expressed in terms of the disappearance of reactants or the appearance of products. When stoichiometric coefficients are not equal to one, the rate is divided by the respective stoichiometric coefficients to obtain a uniform rate expression.
The rate of a reaction is a measure of the change in concentration of reactants or products per unit time. It can be expressed in terms of the change in concentration of any reactant or product.

The average rate of a reaction is defined as the change in concentration of a reactant or product divided by the time taken for the change. It can be expressed in different units such as mol L-1 min-1 or mol L-1 s-1.
The rate law or rate expression is a mathematical expression that relates the rate of a reaction to the concentrations of reactants. It is also known as rate equation.
The rate of a reaction depends on several factors such as concentration of reactants, temperature, and presence of a catalyst.
The rate of a reaction may depend on the concentration of one or more reactants. The rate law expression is used to describe this dependence.
Rate law is an expression that relates the rate of a reaction to the concentration of reactants. It is given in terms of molar concentration of reactants with each term raised to some power.
The differential rate equation is a form of rate law that expresses the rate of reaction in terms of the concentrations of reactants and is given as: -d[R]/dt = k[A]^x[B]^y.
The rate constant (k) is a proportionality constant that relates the rate of a reaction to the concentrations of reactants.
The order of reaction is the sum of the exponents of the concentration terms in the rate law expression.
The rate law for a reaction can be determined experimentally by measuring the rate of reaction as a function of initial concentrations of reactants.
The sum of powers of the concentration of reactants in the rate law expression is called the order of a chemical reaction. It can be 0, 1, 2, 3, or a fraction.

concepts.concept_title: Determination of Order of a Reaction
concepts.description: The order of a reaction can be determined experimentally by analyzing the rate equation. For example, given the rate expression, the overall order can be calculated by adding the exponents of the concentrations of reactants.

The units of the rate constant depend on the order of the reaction. For example, for a zero-order reaction, the unit is mol L^-1 s^-1, for a first-order reaction, it is s^-1, and for a second-order reaction, it is L mol^-1 s^-1.

concepts.concept_title: Identifying Reaction Order from Rate Constant Units
concepts.description: The reaction order can be identified from the units of the rate constant. For example, a rate constant with units L mol^-1 s^-1 represents a second-order reaction, while a rate constant with units s^-1 represents a first-order reaction.
Molecularity is the number of reacting species (atoms, ions, or molecules) taking part in an elementary reaction.
Molecularity is the number of molecules that collide simultaneously to bring about a chemical reaction. It can be unimolecular, bimolecular, or trimolecular.
Elementary reactions can be classified into unimolecular, bimolecular, and trimolecular reactions based on the number of molecules involved.
The rate determining step is the slowest step in a reaction mechanism that controls the overall rate of the reaction.
An intermediate is a species formed during the course of a reaction but not in the overall balanced equation.
Order is an experimental quantity that can be zero or a fraction, while molecularity is a theoretical concept that cannot be zero or a non-integer.
Order is applicable to both elementary and complex reactions, while molecularity is only applicable to elementary reactions.
A reaction where the rate is independent of the concentration of reactants. The rate equation is rate = k[A]^0 = k.

concepts.concept_title: Integrated Rate Equation for Zero Order Reaction
concepts.description: The integrated rate equation for a zero order reaction is [R] = -kt + [R]0, where [R] is the concentration at time t and [R]0 is the initial concentration.

concepts.concept_title: Determination of Rate Constant for Zero Order Reaction
concepts.description: The rate constant k for a zero order reaction can be determined from the slope of a plot of [R] vs t, where k = -slope.
Examples of zero order reactions include decomposition of gaseous ammonia on a hot platinum surface and thermal decomposition of HI on gold surface.
The order of a reaction is the sum of the exponents of the concentration terms in the rate equation.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Concentration on Rate of Reaction
concepts.description: For a second order reaction, if the concentration of a reactant is increased to three times, the rate of formation of product will increase by nine times.
A reaction where the rate of reaction is independent of the concentration of the reactant.
A reaction where the rate of reaction is proportional to the first power of the concentration of the reactant.

concepts.concept_title: Integrated Rate Equation for First Order Reaction
concepts.description: The integrated rate equation for a first order reaction is ln[R] = -kt + ln[R]0.

concepts.concept_title: Half-Life of a First Order Reaction
concepts.description: The half-life of a first order reaction is related to the rate constant by the equation t1/2 = 0.693/k.

concepts.concept_title: Graphical Representation of First Order Reaction
concepts.description: A plot of ln[R] vs t or log[R]0/[R] vs t is a straight line for a first order reaction.

concepts.concept_title: Rate Constant of First Order Reaction
concepts.description: The rate constant of a first order reaction can be calculated using the equation k = (1/t) * ln[R]0/[R].
Examples of first order reactions include hydrogenation of ethene, decomposition of N2O5 and N2O, and radioactive decay.

concepts.concept_title: Partial Pressures in a Gaseous Reaction
concepts.description: The total pressure of a gaseous reaction mixture is the sum of the partial pressures of the individual gases. Partial pressure of a gas is the pressure it would exert if it alone occupied the entire volume.

For a first-order reaction, the rate constant (k) is related to the initial and final pressures of the reactant. The integrated rate law for a first-order reaction is given by: k = (2.303/t) * log(p_i / p_t).
The half-life of a reaction is the time required for the concentration of a reactant to decrease to one-half of its initial value. For a first-order reaction, t_(1/2) = 0.693 / k.
For a zero-order reaction, the rate constant (k) is given by: k = [R]_0 / (2 * t_(1/2)). The half-life of a zero-order reaction is directly proportional to the initial concentration of the reactant.

concepts.concept_title: Dependence of Half-Life on Initial Concentration
concepts.description: For a first-order reaction, the half-life is independent of the initial concentration of the reactant. For a zero-order reaction, the half-life is directly proportional to the initial concentration.

The half-life of a first-order reaction is the time required for the concentration of the reactant to reduce to half of its initial value. It is given by the equation t1/2 = 0.693/k, where k is the rate constant.

A first-order reaction is a type of chemical reaction where the rate of the reaction is directly proportional to the concentration of one reactant. The integrated rate law for a first-order reaction is given by ln[R] = ln[R]0 - kt.

A pseudo first-order reaction is a type of chemical reaction that appears to be a first-order reaction, but is actually a higher-order reaction. This occurs when one reactant is present in large excess, so its concentration does not change significantly during the reaction.

concepts.concept_title: Integrated rate laws for zero and first-order reactions
concepts.description: The integrated rate laws for zero and first-order reactions are summarized in a table. The table provides the differential rate law, integrated rate law, straight line plot, half-life, and units of k for each type of reaction.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of temperature on reaction rate
concepts.description: Most chemical reactions are accelerated by an increase in temperature. This is a general principle in chemical kinetics.
The rate of a chemical reaction increases with rise in temperature. A 10° increase in temperature nearly doubles the rate constant.

The Arrhenius equation (k = A e^(-Ea/RT)) explains the temperature dependence of reaction rate, where A is the frequency factor, Ea is the activation energy, R is the gas constant, and T is the temperature.
The energy required to form an activated complex, which is an unstable intermediate that leads to the formation of products.
A constant specific to a particular reaction, representing the frequency of collisions between reactant molecules.
The distribution of kinetic energy among molecules in a reacting species, described by plotting the fraction of molecules with a given kinetic energy vs kinetic energy.

The Maxwell Boltzmann distribution curve shows the distribution of energies among molecules at a given temperature. The curve shifts to the right with increasing temperature, indicating a greater proportion of molecules with higher energies.
The minimum energy required for a molecule to undergo a chemical reaction is called the activation energy (Ea). It is the energy barrier that must be overcome for a reaction to occur.
The Arrhenius equation relates the rate constant of a reaction to the temperature and activation energy: k = Ae^(-Ea/RT). It is a fundamental equation in chemical kinetics.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Temperature on Rate Constant
concepts.description: Increasing the temperature increases the rate constant of a reaction, while decreasing the activation energy also increases the rate constant. This is evident from the Arrhenius equation.
A plot of ln k vs 1/T gives a straight line, from which the activation energy (Ea) and pre-exponential factor (A) can be calculated.

The activation energy (Ea) and pre-exponential factor (A) can be calculated from the slope and intercept of the ln k vs 1/T plot, or using the Arrhenius equation and given rate constants at different temperatures.
The logarithmic form of the Arrhenius equation is log(k2/k1) = (Ea/2.303R) * (1/T1 - 1/T2), which can be used to calculate Ea and A.
The Arrhenius equation relates the rate constant of a reaction to the temperature at which it occurs. It is given by: log k = log k1 + (Ea / 2.303R) * (1/T1 - 1/T2).

A catalyst is a substance that increases the rate of a reaction without undergoing any permanent chemical change. It provides an alternate pathway or reaction mechanism by reducing the activation energy.
The intermediate complex theory explains the action of a catalyst by forming temporary bonds with the reactants, resulting in an intermediate complex.

The collision theory provides a greater insight into the energetic and mechanistic aspects of reactions. It assumes that reactant molecules are hard spheres and reaction occurs when molecules collide with each other.

concepts.concept_title: Activation Energy and Rate of Reaction
concepts.description: A lower activation energy results in a faster rate of reaction. A catalyst reduces the activation energy, increasing the rate of reaction.
A catalyst does not alter the Gibbs energy or the equilibrium constant of a reaction. It helps in attaining the equilibrium faster.
Collision frequency is the number of collisions between reactant molecules per unit time. It is represented by Z and is related to the rate constant.

Activation energy is the minimum energy required for a reaction to occur. Threshold energy is the energy required for a collision to be effective, which is equal to activation energy plus the energy possessed by reacting species.
Effective collisions are those in which molecules collide with sufficient kinetic energy and proper orientation to form products.
Steric factor or probability factor is a factor that takes into account the proper orientation of molecules during a collision.
Arrhenius equation relates the rate constant of a reaction to the activation energy and temperature.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Temperature on Rate Constant
concepts.description: The rate constant of a reaction increases with an increase in temperature.
key_terms: effect of temperature on rate constant

concepts.description: Mathematical representation of rate of a reaction. It has to be determined experimentally and cannot be predicted.
The sum of powers of concentration of terms for different reactants in the rate law equation.
Proportionality factor in the rate law. Can be determined from rate law or its integrated rate equation.
Defined only for an elementary reaction. Its values are limited from 1 to 3.
Temperature dependence of rate constants. k = Ae–Ea/RT.
Energy difference between activated complex and reactant molecules.
Orientation of molecules which collide. Contributes to effective collisions.
Temperature, activation energy, catalyst, steric factor.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Concentration on Reaction Rate
concepts.description: The rate of a reaction can be affected by changing the concentration of reactants. The effect of concentration on reaction rate can be studied by changing the concentration of one reactant at a time.
The rate constant of a reaction is affected by temperature. The effect of temperature on rate constant can be represented quantitatively using the Arrhenius equation.
A pseudo first order reaction is a reaction that appears to be first order with respect to one reactant, but is actually second order overall.
The differential rate equation is a mathematical expression that describes the rate of a reaction in terms of the concentrations of reactants.

The order of a reaction is the sum of the exponents in the rate law expression. It can be determined experimentally by measuring the initial rates of reaction at different concentrations of reactants.

The rate law expression describes the rate of a reaction in terms of the concentrations of reactants. The rate constant is a proportionality constant that relates the rate of reaction to the concentrations of reactants.

concepts.concept_title: Half-Life of a First Order Reaction
concepts.description: The half-life of a first order reaction is the time required for the concentration of the reactant to decrease by half. It is related to the rate constant by the equation t1/2 = ln 2 / k.

Radioactive decay is a first order process that describes the decay of radioactive isotopes. The half-life of a radioactive isotope is a characteristic property that can be used to identify the isotope.

concepts.concept_title: Determination of Age of a Sample
concepts.description: The age of a sample can be determined by measuring the amount of radioactive isotope present. This is based on the principle that the rate of radioactive decay is constant and can be used to calculate the age of the sample.
A reaction in which the rate depends on one reactant concentration. The integrated rate law is ln([A]t/[A]0) = -kt.
A constant that relates the rate of a reaction to the concentrations of reactants. Its units depend on the order of the reaction.
The time required for the concentration of a reactant to decrease by half. For a first-order reaction, t1/2 = ln(2)/k.
The minimum energy required for a reaction to occur. It can be calculated using the Arrhenius equation.
An equation that relates the rate constant to temperature: k = Ae^(-Ea/RT).
A constant that relates to the frequency of collisions between molecules. It can be calculated using the Arrhenius equation.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Temperature on Rate Constant
concepts.description: The rate constant increases with temperature. The relationship between k and T can be described by the Arrhenius equation.
The d-block elements are in groups 3-12 with progressively filled d orbitals. The f-block elements have 4f and 5f orbitals progressively filled.

concepts.concept_title: Transition metals and inner transition metals
concepts.description: Transition metals refer to d-block elements, while inner transition metals refer to f-block elements.

concepts.concept_title: Electronic configurations of d-block and f-block elements
concepts.description: The d-block elements have partially filled d orbitals, while f-block elements have partially filled 4f and 5f orbitals.
There are four series of transition metals: 3d, 4d, 5d, and 6d series.
Lanthanoids and actinoids are two series of inner transition metals with partially filled 4f and 5f orbitals.
Transition metals are defined as metals with an incomplete d subshell in their neutral atom or ions.
Transition elements exhibit various oxidation states and chemical behavior due to partially filled d or f orbitals.
Transition elements are metals that belong to the d-block of the periodic table. They have partially filled d orbitals and exhibit variable oxidation states.
The electronic configuration of transition elements is of the form (n-1)d1-10ns1-2. The (n-1)d orbitals receive electrons, giving rise to four rows of transition metals.

concepts.concept_title: Stability of Half and Completely Filled Orbitals
concepts.description: Half and completely filled sets of orbitals are relatively more stable. This leads to exceptions in electronic configurations, such as Cr (3d5 4s1) and Cu (3d10 4s1).

concepts.concept_title: d-Block Position in the Periodic Table
concepts.description: The d-block occupies the large middle section of the periodic table, flanked between s- and p-blocks.
Transition elements are defined as elements that have partially filled d orbitals in their ground state or in their common oxidation states.
The electronic configuration of transition elements is characterized by the presence of partially filled d orbitals.

Transition elements exhibit certain characteristic properties such as display of a variety of oxidation states, formation of coloured ions, and entering into complex formation with a variety of ligands.
Transition elements and their compounds exhibit paramagnetic behaviour due to the presence of unpaired electrons in their d orbitals.
Transition metals and their compounds exhibit catalytic property due to their ability to form complexes with other molecules.

concepts.concept_title: Similarities in Properties of Transition Elements
concepts.description: There are greater similarities in the properties of transition elements of a horizontal row than those of non-transition elements.
Transition elements display typical metallic properties such as high tensile strength, ductility, malleability, high thermal and electrical conductivity, and metallic lustre.
Transition metals have different lattice structures such as hcp, bcc, and ccp.
Transition metals have various crystal structures such as bcc, ccp, and hcp. The structure depends on the metal and its position in the periodic table.
Transition metals are very hard and have low volatility. They have high melting and boiling points due to strong interatomic metallic bonding.
Enthalpy of atomisation is a measure of the energy required to break the bonds between atoms in a metal. It is related to the standard electrode potential of a metal.

concepts.concept_title: Trends in Melting Points and Enthalpies of Atomisation
concepts.description: The melting points and enthalpies of atomisation of transition metals show trends across a series and down a group. The melting points rise to a maximum at d5 configuration.

concepts.concept_title: Variation in Atomic and Ionic Sizes
concepts.description: The atomic and ionic sizes of transition metals decrease across a series due to the poor shielding effect of d electrons. The sizes increase down a group.

The filling of 4f orbitals before 5d orbitals results in a regular decrease in atomic radii called Lanthanoid contraction. This contraction compensates for the expected increase in atomic size with increasing atomic number.
The shielding of one electron by another in the same set of orbitals is imperfect, leading to a decrease in size of the entire 4f orbitals along the series.
The atomic radii of transition elements generally decrease with increasing atomic number due to imperfect shielding.
Transition elements exhibit higher enthalpies of atomisation due to a large number of unpaired electrons in their atoms, resulting in stronger interatomic forces.
