Methanol and ethanol are highly poisonous in nature, methanol can cause blindness and death while ethanol can cause nausea, loss of consciousness, and interfere with spontaneous respiration.
Alcohols can be converted to ethers through dehydration in the presence of protic acids.
Acidic dehydration of alcohols produces alkenes and ethers through nucleophilic bimolecular and unimolecular reactions.
Williamson synthesis is a laboratory method for preparing symmetrical and unsymmetrical ethers using alkyl halides and sodium alkoxides.
The mechanism of Williamson synthesis involves the S2 attack of an alkoxide ion on a primary alkyl halide, producing an ether.
Williamson synthesis is limited by the fact that alkoxides are strong bases which favor elimination reactions over substitution, leading to the formation of alkenes instead of ethers.

concepts.concept_title: Preparation of Ethers through Williamson Synthesis
concepts.description: Ethers can be prepared through Williamson synthesis using primary alkyl halides and sodium alkoxides, although secondary and tertiary alkyl halides may lead to elimination reactions.
Friedel-Crafts alkylation reaction involves the addition of a methyl or ethyl group to the aromatic ring of anisole in the presence of an aluminum chloride catalyst.
Nitration of anisole involves the introduction of a nitro group into the aromatic ring of anisole in the presence of sulfuric acid and nitric acid.

concepts.concept_title: Bromination of Anisole in Ethanoic Acid Medium
concepts.description: Bromination of anisole in the presence of ethanoic acid involves the substitution of a bromine atom into the aromatic ring of anisole.
Friedel-Crafts acetylation of anisole involves the attachment of an acetyl group to the aromatic ring of anisole in the presence of an aluminum chloride catalyst.

concepts.concept_title: Mechanism of Addition of HBr to 3-Methylbutan-2-ol
concepts.description: The addition of HBr to 3-methylbutan-2-ol involves the formation of a secondary carbocation, which undergoes a hydride ion shift to form a more stable tertiary carbocation.
Aldehydes and ketones have specific naming conventions based on their structures and functional groups.
The carbonyl group is a fundamental functional group in organic chemistry, consisting of a carbon-oxygen double bond.
Aldehydes have a carbonyl group bonded to a carbon and a hydrogen atom, while ketones have a carbonyl group bonded to two carbon atoms.
Carboxylic acids have a carbonyl group bonded to a carbon and a hydroxyl group (-OH).
Amines and acyl halides are derived from carboxylic acids and have specific structures and properties.

concepts.concept_title: Common Names of Aldehydes and Ketones
concepts.description: Common names of aldehydes and ketones are derived from common names of carboxylic acids or names of alkyl/aryl groups.

concepts.concept_title: IUPAC Names of Aldehydes and Ketones
concepts.description: IUPAC names of aldehydes and ketones are derived from corresponding alkanes by replacing ending with –al and –one respectively.
Aldehydes and ketones are named based on their structure, including carbon chain length and substituents.
In cyclic ketones, the carbonyl carbon is numbered one, and substituents are prefixed in alphabetical order.
Aromatic aldehydes are named as substituted benzaldehydes or using suffix carbaldehyde.

concepts.concept_title: Naming Rules for Aldehydes and Ketones
concepts.description: Aldehydes and ketones are named based on longest carbon chain, substituents, and carbon chain length.

concepts.concept_title: Common Names of Some Aldehydes and Ketones
concepts.description: Common names of some aldehydes and ketones are given in Table 8.1.

concepts.concept_title: IUPAC Names of Some Aldehydes and Ketones
concepts.description: IUPAC names of some aldehydes and ketones are given in Table 8.1.
The carbonyl carbon atom is sp2-hybridised and forms three sigma bonds.
The carbonyl group is formed by overlap of the p-orbital of carbon and oxygen.
The carbonyl carbon-oxygen bond is polar due to higher electronegativity of oxygen.

concepts.concept_title: Electrophilic and nucleophilic centers in carbonyl compounds
concepts.description: The carbonyl carbon is an electrophilic center and the carbonyl oxygen is a nucleophilic center.
Aldehydes have a general formula RCHO, while ketones have a general formula RCOR.

concepts.concept_title: Methods of preparation of aldehydes and ketones
concepts.description: Aldehydes and ketones can be prepared by oxidation of primary and secondary alcohols, respectively.
Ozonolysis of alkenes followed by reaction with zinc dust and water gives aldehydes, ketones or a mixture of both.
Hydration of alkynes in the presence of H2SO4 and HgSO4 gives acetaldehyde or ketones.
Acyl chlorides are hydrogenated over catalyst to give aldehydes or ketones.
Carbonyl compounds exhibit resonance involving neutral and dipolar structures.
Reduction of alkyl halides or esters to aldehydes
Reduction of nitriles to aldehydes using stannous chloride and HCl
Selective reduction of nitriles and esters to aldehydes using DIBAL-H

concepts.concept_title: Oxidation of toluene to benzoic acid
concepts.description: Oxidation of methylbenzene to benzoic acid using strong oxidising agents
Conversion of methylbenzene to benzaldehyde using chromyl chloride
Side chain chlorination of toluene followed by hydrolysis to give benzaldehyde
Preparation of benzaldehyde and substituted benzaldehyde using carbon monoxide and HCl

concepts.concept_title: Acyl chloride reaction with cadmium chloride
concepts.description: Preparation of ketones from acyl chlorides using cadmium chloride
Preparation of ketones from benzene or substituted benzenes using acid chloride
Oxidation of alcohols to aldehydes or ketones using chromic acid and PCC

concepts.concept_title: Physical Properties of Aldehydes and Ketones
concepts.description: Physical properties of aldehydes and ketones include their boiling points, miscibility, and odour. Their boiling points are higher than hydrocarbons and ethers but lower than alcohols.

concepts.concept_title: Molecular Association and Intermolecular Forces in Aldehydes
concepts.description: Aldehydes form weak molecular association due to dipole-dipole interactions, resulting in higher boiling points compared to hydrocarbons and ethers.

concepts.concept_title: Intermolecular Hydrogen Bonding and Solubility of Aldehydes
concepts.description: Lower members of aldehydes form hydrogen bonds with water, resulting in high solubility, whereas higher members have lower solubility due to increased alkyl chain length.

concepts.concept_title: Solubility of Aldehydes and Ketones in Organic Solvents
concepts.description: Aldehydes and ketones are fairly soluble in organic solvents like benzene, ether, and chloroform.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Alkyl Chain Length on Odour of Aldehydes
concepts.description: The size of the alkyl chain affects the odour of aldehydes, with lower members having sharp pungent odours and higher members having less pungent and more fragrant odours.

concepts.concept_title: Boiling Point Trend in Aldehydes and Ketones
concepts.description: The boiling point trend in aldehydes and ketones increases with increasing molecular mass, with some exceptions due to intermolecular forces.

concepts.concept_title: Dipole-Dipole Interactions and Boiling Point in Aldehydes
concepts.description: Butanal has stronger intermolecular dipole-dipole attraction due to its polarity, resulting in a higher boiling point compared to ethoxyethane.

concepts.concept_title: Arrangement of Aldehydes and Ketones by Boiling Point
concepts.description: The boiling points of aldehydes and ketones can be arranged in increasing order based on their molecular mass and intermolecular forces.
Aldehydes and ketones exhibit different boiling points due to the carbonyl functional group. The boiling points increase as a result of intermolecular hydrogen bonding.
Aldehydes and ketones undergo nucleophilic addition reactions, resulting in the addition of a nucleophile and hydrogen across the carbon-oxygen double bond.
A nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon atom of the carbonyl group, resulting in a tetrahedral alkoxide intermediate, which captures a proton to form the final product.
Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones in nucleophilic addition reactions due to steric and electronic reasons, including reduced electrophilicity and hindered nucleophile approach.
Benzaldehyde is less reactive than propanal in nucleophilic addition reactions due to reduced electrophilicity of the carbonyl group caused by resonance.
Nucleophilic addition-elimination reactions involve the addition of a nucleophile followed by the elimination of a leaving group, resulting in the formation of addition products.
Hydrogen cyanide (HCN) reacts with aldehydes and ketones to form cyanohydrins, a useful synthetic intermediate.
Sodium hydrogensulphite (NaHSO3) adds to aldehydes and ketones to form addition products, with the equilibrium lying to the right for aldehydes and left for ketones.
Aldehydes and ketones can be reduced to primary and secondary alcohols respectively by sodium borohydride, lithium aluminium hydride or catalytic hydrogenation.

concepts.concept_title: Reduction of Aldehydes and Ketones to Hydrocarbons
concepts.description: The carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones can be reduced to CH group using zinc amalgam and concentrated hydrochloric acid (Clemmensen reduction) or hydrazine followed by heating with sodium or potassium hydroxide (Wolff-Kishner reduction).
Aldehydes are easily oxidised to carboxylic acids on treatment with common oxidising agents like nitric acid, potassium permanganate, potassium dichromate, etc.
Ketones are generally oxidised under vigorous conditions, unlike aldehydes.
Aldehydes react with one equivalent of monohydric alcohol in the presence of dry hydrogen chloride to yield an alkoxyalcohol intermediate, known as hemiacetal.
Aldehydes react with two equivalents of monohydric alcohol in the presence of dry hydrogen chloride to yield a gem-dialkoxy compound, known as acetal.
Ketones react with ethylene glycol under similar conditions to form a cyclic product known as ketal.
Acetals and ketals can be hydrolysed with aqueous mineral acids to yield corresponding aldehydes and ketones respectively.
Grignard reagents add to the carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones.

concepts.concept_title: Addition of Ammonia and its Derivatives
concepts.description: Ammonia and its derivatives add to the carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones to form imines, oximes, hydrazones, etc.
Ketones undergo oxidation to form a mixture of carboxylic acids with lesser number of carbon atoms at elevated temperatures.
Tollens' reagent oxidizes aldehydes to silver metal, forming a bright silver mirror.
Fehling's reagent oxidizes aldehydes to form a reddish brown precipitate, but aromatic aldehydes do not respond to this test.
Methyl ketones are oxidized by haloform reaction to form sodium salts of corresponding carboxylic acids.
Iodoform reaction is used for detection of CH3CO group or CH2CH(OH) group, which produces CH3CO group on oxidation.
Aromatic compounds containing aldehyde or ketone group form a yellow derivative with 2,4-DNP reagent.
Compounds containing a carbon-carbon double bond or a triple bond decolourise bromine water.
Aldehydes or ketones do not decolourise Baeyer's reagent.

concepts.concept_title: Acidity of α-hydrogen atoms of carbonyl compounds
concepts.description: The acidity of α-hydrogen atoms of carbonyl compounds is due to the strong electron withdrawing effect of the carbonyl group and resonance stabilisation of the conjugate base.
Aldehydes and ketones having at least one α-hydrogen undergo a reaction in the presence of dilute alkali to form β-hydroxy aldehydes (aldol) or β-hydroxy ketones (ketol).
Cross aldol condensation occurs when aldol condensation is carried out between two different aldehydes and/or ketones.
Aldehydes which do not have an α-hydrogen atom undergo self oxidation and reduction (disproportionation) reaction on heating with concentrated alkali.
Aromatic aldehydes and ketones undergo electrophilic substitution at the ring in which the carbonyl group acts as a deactivating and meta-directing group.
Aldehydes and ketones undergo nucleophilic addition reactions. The reactivity of aldehydes and ketones is influenced by steric effect and electronic effect.
The reactivity of aldehydes and ketones in nucleophilic addition reactions can be arranged in increasing order considering steric effect and electronic effect.

concepts.concept_title: Biological Importance of Aldehydes and Ketones
concepts.description: Aldehydes and ketones have various biological applications such as preparation of bakelite, perfumery, and in the manufacture of acetic acid and polymers.
Acetaldehyde is used in the manufacture of acetic acid, ethyl acetate, vinyl acetate, polymers, and drugs.
Benzaldehyde is used in perfumery and in dye industries.
Acetone and ethyl methyl ketone are common industrial solvents.
Carboxylic acids are carbon compounds containing a carboxyl functional group –COOH.
Carboxylic acids may be aliphatic (RCOOH) or aromatic (ArCOOH).

concepts.concept_title: Common and IUPAC Names of Carboxylic Acids
concepts.description: Carboxylic acids are named by replacing the ending –e in the name of the corresponding alkane with –oic acid in the IUPAC system.

In numbering the carbon chain, the carboxylic carbon is numbered one. For naming compounds containing more than one carboxyl group, the alkyl chain leaving carboxyl groups is numbered and the number of carboxyl groups is indicated by adding the multiplicative prefix, dicarboxylic acid, tricarboxylic acid, etc. to the name of parent alkyl chain.
Carboxylic acids have the general structure RCOOH, where R is an alkyl or aryl group.
The bonds to the carboxyl carbon lie in one plane separated by about 120°.
The carboxyl carbon is less electrophilic than carbonyl carbon due to resonance.
Carboxylic acids are named using IUPAC nomenclature, replacing the suffix of the parent compound with carboxylic acid.

concepts.concept_title: Oxidation of primary alcohols to carboxylic acids
concepts.description: Primary alcohols are oxidised to carboxylic acids using potassium permanganate (KMnO4) or potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) in neutral, acidic or alkaline media.
Carboxylic acids can be prepared from primary alcohols, aldehydes, alkylbenzenes, nitriles, and Grignard reagents.
Acid chlorides are hydrolyzed with water to give carboxylic acids and with aqueous base to give carboxylate ions.
Anhydrides are hydrolyzed to give corresponding carboxylic acids with water.
Esters undergo acidic hydrolysis to give carboxylic acids and basic hydrolysis to give carboxylates.

concepts.concept_title: Conversion of alcohols to carboxylic acids
concepts.description: Alcohols can be converted to carboxylic acids through oxidation.

Carboxylic acids are colorless liquids with unpleasant odors, have higher boiling points than aldehydes and ketones, exhibit association through hydrogen bonding, and are miscible with water up to four carbon atoms.
Carboxylic acids are soluble in water up to four carbon atoms and in less polar organic solvents beyond that.
Reactions of carboxylic acids can be classified into acid-base reactions, reactions with metals and alkalies, and cleavage reactions involving O–H bond breaking.
Carboxylic acids are organic compounds that are characterized by the presence of a carboxyl group.

concepts.concept_title: Reaction of Carboxylic Acids with Bases
concepts.description: Carboxylic acids react with strong bases to form salts, but react with weaker bases to evolve carbon dioxide.
Carboxylic acids dissociate in water to form carboxylate anions and hydronium ions.
The strength of an acid is indicated by its pKa value, with smaller pKa values representing stronger acids.

concepts.concept_title: Comparison of Carboxylic Acids and Phenol
concepts.description: Carboxylic acids are stronger acids than phenol due to the stabilizing resonance structures of the carboxylate ion.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Substituents on Carboxylic Acid Acidity
concepts.description: Electron-withdrawing groups increase the acidity of carboxylic acids by stabilizing their conjugate base.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Different Halogen Groups on Acidity
concepts.description: The effect of halogen groups, from Ph to F, increases the acidity of carboxylic acids in the order given.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Electronic Groups on Acidity
concepts.description: Electron-withdrawing groups such as NO_2 on the phenyl ring increase acidity, while electron-donating groups decrease acidity.
Carboxylic acids react with mineral acids or P2O5 to form anhydrides.
Carboxylic acids react with alcohols or phenols in the presence of a catalyst to form esters.
The esterification reaction involves nucleophilic acyl substitution with protonation of the carbonyl oxygen.
Carboxylic acids react with PCl3, PCl5, or SOCl2 to replace the hydroxyl group with a chlorine atom.
Carboxylic acids react with ammonia to form ammonium salts that can be converted to amides.
Carboxylic acids can be reduced to primary alcohols using lithium aluminium hydride or diborane.
Carboxylic acids lose carbon dioxide when their sodium salts are heated with sodalime.

Decarboxylation is the removal of a carboxyl group (-COOH) from a carboxylic acid, resulting in the formation of a hydrocarbon. Alkali metal salts of carboxylic acids undergo decarboxylation on electrolysis to form hydrocarbons.

Kolbe electrolysis is a reaction in which alkali metal salts of carboxylic acids undergo decarboxylation on electrolysis to form hydrocarbons having twice the number of carbon atoms present in the alkyl group of the acid.
The Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction is a reaction in which carboxylic acids undergo halogenation at the α-position, resulting in the formation of α-halocarboxylic acids.
Aromatic carboxylic acids undergo electrophilic substitution reactions, in which the carboxyl group acts as a deactivating and meta-directing group.

Friedel-Crafts reaction is a reaction in which aromatic compounds undergo electrophilic substitution, but carboxylic acids do not undergo this reaction because the carboxyl group is deactivating and the catalyst aluminium chloride gets bonded to the carboxyl group.

Acid strength is determined by the ability of an acid to donate a proton (H+ ion). Factors such as the presence of electron-withdrawing groups and the molecular structure of the acid affect the acid strength.

concepts.concept_title: Solubility of Aldehydes, Ketones, and Carboxylic Acids
concepts.description: Aldehydes, ketones, and carboxylic acids are polar molecules that form hydrogen bonds with water, resulting in high solubility in water. However, higher members of these compounds have large hydrophobic chains and are insoluble in water but soluble in common organic solvents.
Cyanohydrin is formed when a cyanide ion (CN-) adds to an aldehyde or a ketone.
An acetal is formed when two ethoxy or other similar groups replace the carbonyl group in an aldehyde or ketone.
A semicarbazone is formed when a semicarbazide molecule adds to an aldehyde or a ketone.
Aldol condensation is a reaction where two aldehyde or ketone molecules react in the presence of a base to form a beta-hydroxyaldehyde or a beta-hydroxyketone.
Cannizzaro reaction is a reaction where two aldehyde molecules react in the presence of a strong base to form a beta-hydroxyalcohol and the carboxylic acid.
Aldehydes and ketones can be reduced to alcohols using NaBH4, LiAlH4, or catalytic hydrogenation.
Clemmensen reduction is a reaction where a carbonyl group is reduced to a methylene group using zinc amalgam and a concentrated acid.
Wolff-Kishner reduction is a reaction where a carbonyl group is reduced to a methylene group using hydrazine and a high temperature.
Aldehydes can be oxidized to carboxylic acids using mild oxidizing agents such as Tollens' reagent and Fehling's reagent.
Carboxylic acids can be prepared by oxidation of primary alcohols, aldehydes, and alkenes by hydrolysis of nitriles, and by treatment of Grignard reagents with carbon dioxide.
Carboxylic acids can undergo a-halogenation with Cl2 and Br in the presence of red phosphorus (Hell-Volhard Zelinsky reaction).
Reaction of an aldehyde or ketone with PhMgBr followed by H O+ leads to alkyl phenyl ether.
Tollens' reagent is reduced by 1º-aldehydes to form a silver mirror, while ketones and 2º-aldehydes do not react.
Aldol condensation involves the reaction of two molecules of an aldehyde or ketone to form a β-hydroxy aldehyde or ketone.
Cannizzaro reaction involves the reaction of two molecules of an aldehyde to form an alcohol and a carboxylic acid.
Presence of a 2,4-DNP derivative indicates the presence of a carbonyl group.
Identification of an organic compound involves analysis of its molecular formula, functional groups, and chemical reactions.

concepts.concept_title: Oxidation of an Alcohol with Chromic Acid
concepts.description: Oxidation of an alcohol with chromic acid produces a carboxylic acid.

concepts.concept_title: Reactivity of Aldehydes and Ketones towards HCN
concepts.description: Reactivity of aldehydes and ketones towards HCN involves the addition of HCN to the carbonyl group.
Acid strength of carboxylic acids depends on the number and nature of substituents on the benzene ring.

concepts.concept_title: Chemical Tests to Distinguish between Compounds
concepts.description: Chemical tests can be used to distinguish between compounds based on their functional groups.
Esters can be prepared from benzene using various organic and inorganic reagents.

concepts.concept_title: Conversion of an Aldehyde to Different Functional Groups
concepts.description: An aldehyde can be converted to different functional groups such as an alcohol, a carboxylic acid, or an ester.
A reaction where an acetyl group is introduced into a molecule.
A reaction involving an aldehyde with two aldehyde groups reacting to form an alcohol.
A reaction involving the reaction of two different aldehydes to form a product with three carbon atoms.
A reaction where a carboxylic acid loses its carboxyl group.
A test used to detect the presence of aldehydes or ketones.
A test used to detect the presence of methyl ketones.

concepts.concept_title: Addition Compound with Sodium Hydrogen Sulphite
concepts.description: A reaction where an aldehyde or ketone reacts with sodium hydrogen sulphite to form an addition compound.
A comparison of the reactivity of phenoxide ion and carboxylate ion.
Amines are classified based on the number of hydrogen atoms replaced by alkyl or aryl groups in the ammonia molecule.
Nitrogen atom of amines is trivalent and carries an unshared pair of electrons, leading to a pyramidal geometry.

concepts.concept_title: Hybridization of Nitrogen Orbitals in Amines
concepts.description: Nitrogen orbitals in amines are sp3 hybridized.
The geometry of amines is pyramidal due to the presence of an unshared pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom.
Primary amines are prepared by replacing one hydrogen atom of ammonia with an alkyl or aryl group.
Secondary amines are prepared by replacing two hydrogen atoms of ammonia or one hydrogen atom of a primary amine with an alkyl or aryl group.

concepts.concept_title: Importance of Diazonium Salts in Organic Synthesis
concepts.description: Diazonium salts are important intermediates in the synthesis of aromatic compounds.
Amines are named in common system by prefixing alkyl group to amine and in IUPAC system by replacing 'e' of alkane by the word amine.
Aromatic amines are named by replacing 'e' of arene with 'amine' and in common system as aniline.

concepts.concept_title: Determination of Primary, Secondary, or Tertiary Nature of an Amine
concepts.description: Primary amines have one alkyl group attached to the nitrogen atom, secondary amines have two alkyl groups, and tertiary amines have three alkyl groups.
In IUPAC system, the suffix 'e' of arene is replaced by 'amine' to name aromatic amines.
Diamines have two amino groups in the molecule and are named by specifying the positions of the amino groups.
Isomeric amines are named by specifying the position of the amino group(s) in the molecule.

concepts.concept_title: Types of Isomerism Exhibited by Amines
concepts.description: Amines exhibit structural and positional isomerism.

concepts.concept_title: Reduction of Nitro Compounds to form Amines
concepts.description: Nitro compounds are reduced to amines by passing hydrogen gas in the presence of finely divided nickel, palladium, or platinum and also by reduction with metals in acidic medium.
Ammonia reacts with alkyl or benzyl halides to form amines through nucleophilic substitution reaction.
Nitriles are reduced with lithium aluminum hydride (LiAlH4) or catalytic hydrogenation to produce primary amines.
Amides are reduced with lithium aluminum hydride to produce amines.
Phthalimide is treated with ethanolic potassium hydroxide and then with alkyl halide followed by alkaline hydrolysis to produce primary amines.
Amides are treated with bromine to produce primary amines.

concepts.concept_title: Order of Reactivity of Halides with Amines
concepts.description: The order of reactivity of halides with amines is RI > RBr > RCl.

The degradation of amides by Hoffmann bromamide reaction, resulting in the formation of amines, is a specific type of chemical reaction. The amine formed contains one carbon less than the original amide.

concepts.concept_title: Reagents and Conditions for Hoffmann Bromamide Reaction
concepts.description: The reaction requires an aqueous or ethanolic solution of sodium hydroxide, and the conditions lead to the migration of an alkyl or aryl group from the carbonyl carbon to the nitrogen atom.

concepts.concept_title: Product Formation in Hoffmann Bromamide Reaction
concepts.description: The reaction product is an amine, which contains one carbon less than the original amide molecule, with the correct IUPAC name and structure.
The properties of amides and amines, such as solubility in water, boiling points, and intermolecular association, can be predicted based on nitrogen and oxygen electronegativities.
Lower aliphatic amines are gases with a fishy odor, primary amines with three or more carbon atoms are liquids, and the solubility decreases with an increase in molar mass.
Aromatic amines, such as aniline, are usually colorless but get colored on storage due to atmospheric oxidation and have specific physical properties.
Primary amines show more intermolecular association due to hydrogen bonding than secondary amines, with tertiary amines showing minimal intermolecular association.
Amines can exhibit primary, secondary, and tertiary isomerism based on the number of hydrogen atoms attached to the nitrogen atom.

concepts.concept_title: Intermolecular Hydrogen Bonding in Primary Amines
concepts.description: Primary amines exhibit intermolecular hydrogen bonding, leading to higher boiling points compared to primary alcohols and alkanes of similar molecular mass.
Amines exhibit basic character due to the presence of an unshared pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom, making them behave as nucleophiles.
Amines react with acids to form ammonium salts, which can be regenerated into the parent amine upon treatment with a base like NaOH.

concepts.concept_title: Basic Character of Amines in Terms of K_b and pK_b
concepts.description: The basic character of amines can be understood in terms of their K_b and pK_b values, with larger K_b values and smaller pK_b values indicating stronger basicity.

concepts.concept_title: Influence of Substituents on Basicity of Amines
concepts.description: The basicity of amines can be influenced by the presence of alkyl or aryl substituents, with alkyl groups generally exhibiting a +I effect and aryl groups exhibiting a -I effect.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Inductive Effect on Basic Strength of Amines
concepts.description: Inductive effect makes alkyl groups electron-releasing and increases the availability of the unshared electron pair on nitrogen for protonation.

concepts.concept_title: Solvation Effect on Basicity of Amines
concepts.description: Solvation by water molecules stabilizes the substituted ammonium cations, affecting the basicity of amines in aqueous solutions.

concepts.concept_title: Steric Hindrance and Basic Strength of Amines
concepts.description: Large alkyl groups cause steric hindrance to H-bonding, affecting the basic strength of amines in aqueous solutions.

concepts.concept_title: Relationship Between Basicity and Structure of Amines
concepts.description: The basicity of amines is related to the stability of the cation formed upon protonation and the ease of electron pair availability.
The basicity of aliphatic amines increases with the number of alkyl groups attached, but this trend is affected by the solvation effect in aqueous solutions.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Alkyl Group Size on Basic Strength of Amines
concepts.description: The size of the alkyl group affects the basic strength of amines, with smaller groups resulting in higher basic strength due to reduced steric hindrance.
Aniline's basicity is less than that of ammonia due to resonance in aniline making the unshared electron pair on nitrogen less available for protonation.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of Substituents on Basic Strength of Aniline
concepts.description: Electron releasing groups increase the basic strength of aniline, while electron withdrawing groups decrease it.

concepts.concept_title: Decreasing Order of Basic Strength of Amines
concepts.description: The decreasing order of basic strength of amines is (C6H5CH2)NH > C6H5NH2 > NH3 > C6H5CH2CH2NH2.
Alkylation of amines occurs with alkyl halides, and acylation of amines with acid chlorides, anhydrides, and esters forms amides.

concepts.concept_title: Formation of Salts with Carboxylic Acids
concepts.description: Amines form salts with carboxylic acids at room temperature.
Primary aliphatic and aromatic amines react with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide to form isocyanides.
Primary aliphatic amines react differently with nitrous acid, which is prepared in situ from a mineral acid and sodium nitrite.
Aromatic amines react with nitrous acid at low temperatures to form diazonium salts, which liberate nitrogen gas and alcohols.

concepts.concept_title: Reaction of Aromatic Diazonium Salts with Amino Acids and Proteins
concepts.description: Quantitative evolution of nitrogen from aromatic diazonium salts is used in estimation of amino acids and proteins.

concepts.concept_title: Reaction of Arylsulphonyl Chloride with Primary and Secondary Amines
concepts.description: Arylsulphonyl chloride (Hinsberg's reagent) reacts with primary and secondary amines to form sulphonamides.

concepts.concept_title: Reaction of Arylsulphonyl Chloride with Tertiary Amines
concepts.description: Tertiary amines do not react with benzenesulphonyl chloride.
Aromatic amines undergo electrophilic substitution, with the –NH group being ortho- and para-directing and a powerful activating group.
Aniline reacts with bromine water at room temperature to give a white precipitate of 2,4,6-tribromoaniline.

concepts.concept_title: Protecting the Activating Effect of -NH Group in Aromatic Amines
concepts.description: The activating effect of the –NH group in aromatic amines can be controlled by protecting the –NH group through acetylation.
The lone pair of electrons on nitrogen in acetanilide interacts with the oxygen atom due to resonance.
Direct nitration of aniline yields tarry oxidation products in addition to the nitro derivatives. Aniline is protonated to form the anilinium ion which directs meta direction.
Protecting the -NH group of aniline by acetylation reaction controls the nitration reaction and yields p-nitro derivative as the major product.
Aniline reacts with concentrated sulphuric acid to form anilinium hydrogensulphate which on heating with sulphuric acid produces p-aminobenzene sulphonic acid as the major product.
Aniline forms a salt with aluminium chloride, the Lewis acid catalyst, deactivating the nitrogen for further reaction.
Arrange the given amines in increasing order of their basic strength.
Write reactions of amines with HCl and name the products.
Write reactions of aniline with excess methyl iodide in the presence of sodium carbonate solution.

concepts.concept_title: Reaction of Aniline with Benzoyl Chloride
concepts.description: Write chemical reaction of aniline with benzoyl chloride and write the name of the product obtained.
The diazonium salts have the general formula RNX where R stands for an aryl group and X- ion. They are named by suffixing diazonium to the name of the parent hydrocarbon and the name of the anion.
Nitrous acid is prepared by the reaction of sodium nitrite with hydrochloric acid.
Primary aromatic amines are converted into diazonium salts through diazotisation.
Benzenediazonium chloride is a colourless crystalline solid, highly soluble in water, and stable at low temperatures.
The substitution of halide or cyanide ions into the benzene ring is achieved through the Sandmeyer reaction in the presence of Cu(I) ion.
Gatterman reaction involves the introduction of chlorine or bromine into the benzene ring by treating the diazonium salt solution with the corresponding halogen acid in the presence of copper powder.
Diazonium salts can decompose into arenes and a nitrogen gas under certain conditions.

concepts.concept_title: Replacement by Halide or Cyanide Ion
concepts.description: Nitrogen is replaced by halide or cyanide ions in the benzene ring through reactions involving diazonium salts and Cu(I) ion.
Diazonium salts can be reduced to arenes using mild reducing agents such as hypophosphorous acid or ethanol.
Diazonium salts can be hydrolysed to phenol at elevated temperatures.
Diazonium salts can be decomposed to aryl fluorides in the presence of fluoroboric acid.
The diazonium group can be replaced by a nitroso group or another diazonium salt through azo coupling reactions.
Diazonium salts react with phenol or aniline to form azo products with an extended conjugate system.
Diazonium salts are good intermediates for introducing various functional groups into the aromatic ring.

concepts.concept_title: Preparation of Aryl Fluorides and Iodides
concepts.description: Aryl fluorides and iodides cannot be prepared by direct halogenation, but can be obtained from diazonium salts.
The cyano group can be introduced into the aromatic ring through the diazonium salt route.
A step-by-step strategy for converting 4-nitrotoluene to 2-bromobenzoic acid involving multiple reactions.
A step-by-step strategy for converting 3-methylaniline to 3-nitrotoluene.
A step-by-step strategy for converting aniline to 1,3,5-tribromobenzene.
Properties, classification and basic nature of primary, secondary, and tertiary amines.
Amines are formed from nitro compounds, halides, amides, imides, etc.
Basic nature of amines, including influence of electron releasing, steric, and H-bonding factors.

concepts.concept_title: Influence of Substituents on Basic Character of Amines
concepts.description: In aromatic amines, electron releasing groups increase and electron withdrawing groups decrease their basic character.

concepts.concept_title: Classification and Identification of Aliphatic Amines
concepts.description: Primary, secondary, and tertiary amines can be identified based on the number of hydrogen atoms at the nitrogen atom.

concepts.concept_title: Acylation as a Method to Control Reactivity of Aromatic Amines
concepts.description: Acylation process using acetyl chloride or acetic anhydride can control the reactivity of aromatic amines.
Aryldiazonium salt undergoes coupling reaction with phenols or arylamines to form azo dyes.

concepts.concept_title: pKa of Amines and Its Significance
concepts.description: pKa of amines determines their basic strength and reactivity.

concepts.concept_title: Solubility of Aliphatic and Aromatic Amines in Water
concepts.description: Aliphatic amines are more soluble in water than aromatic amines due to their ability to form hydrogen bonds.

concepts.concept_title: Reaction of Methylamine with Ferric Chloride
concepts.description: Methylamine reacts with ferric chloride to form a precipitate of hydrated ferric oxide.
Aniline undergoes nitration to form m-nitroaniline.
Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction due to its basic nature.
Diazonium salts of aromatic amines are more stable than those of aliphatic amines.
Gabriel phthalimide synthesis is preferred for synthesizing primary amines.

concepts.concept_title: Arrangement of Amines in Order of pKa and Basic Strength
concepts.description: Amines can be arranged in order of pKa and basic strength based on their structure and substituents.
Biomolecules exhibit characteristics that lead to life processes in living systems.
Carbohydrates are essential constituents of our food and play a vital role in various biological processes.
Carbohydrates have a general formula of Cx(H2O)y.
Carbohydrates are optically active polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones or compounds that produce such units on hydrolysis.
Carbohydrates are classified on the basis of their behaviour on hydrolysis into monosaccharides, oligosaccharides, and polysaccharides.
DNA consists of deoxyribose, while RNA consists of ribose.
Biomolecules interact with each other and constitute the molecular logic of life processes.
Carbohydrates can be classified into monosaccharides, disaccharides, and polysaccharides based on the number of monosaccharide units they yield on hydrolysis.
Monosaccharides are simple sugars that yield only one glucose molecule on hydrolysis. Examples include glucose, fructose, and ribose.
Oligosaccharides are carbohydrates that yield 2-10 monosaccharide units on hydrolysis. They can be classified as disaccharides, trisaccharides, etc.
Polysaccharides are carbohydrates that yield a large number of monosaccharide units on hydrolysis. Examples include starch, cellulose, glycogen, and gums.
Sugars that reduce Fehling's solution and Tollens' reagent are referred to as reducing sugars. All monosaccharides are reducing sugars.
Monosaccharides can be classified based on the number of carbon atoms and the functional group present. They can be aldoses or ketoses.
Glucose can be prepared from sucrose by boiling it with dilute HCl or H2SO4 in an alcoholic solution.
Glucose can be prepared from starch by boiling it with dilute H2SO4 under pressure.
Glucose is an aldohexose and is also known as dextrose. It is the monomer of many larger carbohydrates, such as starch and cellulose.
The molecular formula of glucose is found to be C6H12O6.
Glucose forms n-hexane upon prolonged heating with HI.

concepts.concept_title: Presence of Carbonyl Group in Glucose
concepts.description: Reactions of glucose with hydroxylamine and hydrogen cyanide indicate the presence of a carbonyl group (>C=O).

concepts.concept_title: Oxidation of Glucose to Gluconic Acid
concepts.description: Glucose gets oxidised to gluconic acid on reaction with a mild oxidising agent like bromine water.
Acetylation of glucose with acetic anhydride gives glucose pentaacetate.

concepts.concept_title: Yield of Saccharic Acid from Glucose and Gluconic Acid
concepts.description: Glucose and gluconic acid yield saccharic acid upon oxidation with nitric acid.
The configuration of glucose is correctly represented by Fischer's structure.
D-configuration of carbohydrates is assigned based on the configuration of the lowest asymmetric carbon atom, comparable to (+) glyceraldehyde.
Pyranose structure is a six-membered cyclic ring formed by the hemiacetal reaction of a monosaccharide, explained for glucose.
Anomers of glucose are structural isomers that differ only in the configuration of the hydroxyl group at C1, denoted as a-form and b-form.
Fructose is a keto-hexose with a ketonic functional group at carbon number 2 and six carbons in a straight chain.
Fructose is a laevorotatory compound that exists in two cyclic forms, furanose, and its open-chain structure is shown.
A glycosidic linkage is an oxide linkage between two monosaccharide units through an oxygen atom, formed by the loss of a water molecule.
Non-reducing sugars are disaccharides in which the reducing groups of monosaccharides are bonded, e.g., sucrose.
Reducing sugars are disaccharides in which the reducing groups of monosaccharides are free, e.g., maltose and lactose.
Sucrose is composed of a-D-glucose and D-(-) fructose held together by a glycosidic linkage between C1 of glucose and C2 of fructose.
Sucrose is a non-reducing sugar because the reducing groups of glucose and fructose are involved in glycosidic bond formation.
Invert sugar is formed after hydrolysis of sucrose, showing a change in the sign of rotation from dextro (+) to laevo (-).
Maltose is composed of two a-D-glucose units in which C1 of one glucose is linked to C4 of another glucose unit.
Maltose is a reducing sugar because the free aldehyde group can be produced at C1 of the second glucose unit.
Lactose is composed of b-D-galactose and b-D-glucose, with a linkage between C1 of galactose and C4 of glucose.
Lactose is a reducing sugar because the free aldehyde group may be produced at C-1 of the glucose unit.
Polysaccharides are large molecules composed of many monosaccharide units joined together by glycosidic linkages, e.g., starch.
Starch is the main storage polysaccharide of plants, composed of many glucose units joined together by glycosidic linkages.
Starch is a polymer of a-glucose and consists of two components- Amylose and Amylopectin.
Amylose is a long unbranched chain with 200-1000 a-D-(+)-glucose units held together by C1– C4 glycosidic linkage.
Amylopectin is a branched chain polymer of a-D-glucose units with C1-C4 glycosidic linkage in the chain and C1-C6 glycosidic linkage in the branches.
Cellulose is a straight chain polysaccharide composed only of b-D-glucose units joined by C1-C4 glycosidic linkage.
Glycogen is a branched chain polysaccharide with structure similar to amylopectin.
Carbohydrates are essential for life, form a major portion of food, and have various uses in industries.
Amino acids can be classified as acidic, basic or neutral based on the relative number of amino and carboxyl groups in their molecule.
Amino acids contain an amino group (-NH2) which can accept a proton in aqueous solution.
Amino acids contain a carboxyl group (-COOH) which can lose a proton in aqueous solution.
In aqueous solution, amino acids form a zwitter ion due to the presence of both acidic (carboxyl group) and basic (amino group) groups.
Amino acids are represented by a three-letter symbol, one-letter symbol, or a full structural formula.
Twenty amino acids that occur naturally and are essential for protein synthesis.

concepts.concept_title: Acidic, Basic, and Neutral Amino Acids
concepts.description: Amino acids can be classified as acidic, basic, or neutral based on the relative number of amino and carboxyl groups.
Non-essential amino acids can be synthesized by the body, while essential amino acids cannot be synthesized and must be obtained through diet.
Amino acids form zwitter ions, which are neutral but contain both positive and negative charges due to the presence of NH3+ and COO- groups.
Amino acids show amphoteric behaviour as they can react with both acids and bases in their zwitter ionic form.
Except glycine, all other naturally occurring alpha-amino acids are optically active and exist in both 'D' and 'L' forms.
A peptide bond is an amide formed between the COOH group and NH2 group of two amino acids, resulting in the elimination of a water molecule.
When two or more amino acids are linked together by peptide bonds, the resulting products are called dipeptide, tripeptide, etc., depending on the number of amino acids.
Proteins can be classified into two types based on their molecular shape: fibrous proteins (insoluble in water) and globular proteins (soluble in water).
The structure and shape of proteins can be studied at four different levels: primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary.
The primary structure of a protein refers to the specific sequence of amino acids, which determines the unique three-dimensional structure and biological activity of the protein.
The secondary structure of a protein refers to the local arrangements of its polypeptide backbone, which can exist in a-helix or beta-pleated sheet structures.

The tertiary structure of a protein represents the overall three-dimensional structure formed by the folding of its polypeptide chains, and is stabilized by various forces such as hydrogen bonds and disulphide linkages.
The quaternary structure of a protein refers to the spatial arrangement of its polypeptide chains, which can be composed of multiple sub-units.
Denaturation of a protein refers to the loss of its native structure and biological activity due to physical or chemical changes such as changes in temperature or pH.
Enzymes lower activation energy by reducing the magnitude of activation energy.
Enzymes are very specific for a particular reaction and substrate.
Enzymes are named after the compound or class of compounds they work on.
Enzymes reduce activation energy, with the aid of a sequence of chemical reactions.
Vitamins are organic compounds required in small quantities to perform specific biological functions.
Vitamins are classified into different chemical classes and designated by alphabets.
Biomolecules like enzymes, vitamins, and hormones play a crucial role in living organisms.
Vitamins are classified into two groups based on their solubility: fat soluble (A, D, E, K) and water soluble (B group and C).
Vitamins A, D, E, and K are fat soluble and stored in liver and adipose tissues.
Vitamins B group and C are water soluble and must be regularly supplied through diet as they are excreted in urine.

concepts.concept_title: Beri Beri Caused by Thiamine Deficiency
concepts.description: Beri beri is a disease caused by deficiency of vitamin B (thiamine).
Vitamin A is found in fish liver oil, carrots, butter, and milk.
Vitamin D is formed in the body through exposure to sunlight, and also found in fish and egg yolk.
DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and RNA (ribonucleic acid) are long chain polymers of nucleotides, also called polynucleotides.
DNA molecules are composed of a pentose sugar, phosphoric acid, and nitrogenous bases. RNA molecules have the same sugar and acid, but different nitrogenous bases.
DNA contains four bases: adenine (A), guanine (G), cytosine (C), and thymine (T). RNA contains the same bases as DNA, except for uracil (U) instead of thymine (T)
A nucleoside is formed by attaching a base to the 1' position of a sugar. The sugar carbons are numbered 1', 2', 3' etc.
A nucleotide is formed by linking a nucleoside to phosphoric acid at the 5' position of the sugar.
Nucleotides are joined together by phosphodiester linkage between the 5' and 3' carbon atoms of the sugar.
The sequence of nucleotides in a nucleic acid chain is its primary structure.
DNA has a double strand helix structure with two strands wound about each other and held together by hydrogen bonds between pairs of bases.
Nucleic acids have a secondary structure, which involves the arrangement of bases and sugars in a three-dimensional structure.

concepts.concept_title: Functions of RNA and Types of RNA
concepts.description: RNA has different types, each performing different functions such as messenger RNA, ribosomal RNA, etc.
Denaturation of proteins occurs when their secondary or tertiary structure gets disturbed due to change in pH or temperature.
Enzymes speed up reactions in biosystems and are very specific and selective in their action.
Vitamins are classified as fat soluble (A, D, E, and K) and water soluble (B group and C).
DNA is a double-stranded molecule with 2-deoxyribose sugar and thymine as its base, while RNA is a single-stranded molecule with ribose sugar and uracil as its base.
DNA is the chemical basis of heredity and contains the coded message for proteins to be synthesized in the cell.
There are three types of RNA: mRNA, rRNA, and tRNA, which carry out protein synthesis in the cell.
Monosaccharides are simple sugars, and reducing sugars are those that can donate electrons.
Carbohydrates perform two main functions: provide energy and serve as structural components.
A glycosidic linkage is the type of bond between carbohydrate molecules.
Glycogen is a polysaccharide stored in animal cells, while starch is a polysaccharide stored in plant cells.

concepts.concept_title: Hydrolysis products of sucrose and lactose
concepts.description: Sucrose and lactose are disaccharides that hydrolyze into glucose and fructose, and glucose and galactose, respectively.

concepts.concept_title: Structural difference between starch and cellulose
concepts.description: The main structural difference between starch and cellulose is the orientation of their glucose units.
D-glucose undergoes various reactions, including oxidation and substitution.
Essential amino acids are required by the body, while non-essential amino acids can be synthesized within the body.
A peptide linkage is the type of bond between amino acids in a protein, and protein structure includes primary, secondary, and tertiary levels.

concepts.concept_title: Types of secondary structure in proteins
concepts.description: Proteins can have various secondary structures, including a-helix and beta-pleated sheet.
Globular proteins are compact and spherical in shape, while fibrous proteins are long and fibrous.
Amino acids have both acidic and basic properties, making them amphoteric in nature.
Enzymes are biological catalysts that speed up chemical reactions in living organisms.

concepts.concept_title: Effect of denaturation on protein structure
concepts.description: Denaturation of proteins disrupts their secondary and tertiary structures.
Vitamins are classified as fat-soluble and water-soluble.

concepts.concept_title: Importance of vitamins A and C
concepts.description: Vitamins A and C are essential for maintaining healthy vision and immune functions.
There are three types of RNA, including mRNA, rRNA, and tRNA.
